100.title: Vaginal Itching
100.diagnosis: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis (VVC) or other fungal infection, Bacterial Vaginosis (BV), Atrophic Vaginitis (in postmenopausal women), Contact dermatitis, or allergic reaction are possible causes of vaginal itching.
exam: Vaginal Itching — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
100.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
100.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Send lab work including wet mount, culture and sensitivity for vaginal fluid, KOH prep for fungal elements, CBC
Prescribe an antifungal cream or oral medication (such as fluconazole) for empiric treatment of VVC
Consider sending a swab for possible allergy testing if history of new feminine hygiene product use is present
Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: vital signs and oxygen are important for any patient, but especially in a case of vaginal itching where the diagnosis is unknown

interventions.label: Pregnancy test (β-hCG)
interventions.why: it is essential to rule out pregnancy in women of childbearing age, as certain conditions can affect both mother and fetus

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics for empiric treatment of VVC
decoys.why: VVC is a fungal infection, not bacterial. Antibiotics are ineffective against fungi.

decoys.label: Prescribe topical steroids for vaginal itching without confirming diagnosis
decoys.why: Topical steroids can mask symptoms and worsen underlying conditions such as BV or candidiasis if not used appropriately.

decoys.label: Perform bimanual pelvic exam for every vaginal itching case
decoys.why: Bimanual pelvic exam is an invasive procedure and may not be necessary in all cases of vaginal itching. It should be used only when other diagnostic tests are inconclusive.

decoys.label: Prescribe a non-estrogen cream for atrophic vaginitis without considering patient's menopausal status
decoys.why: Atrophic vaginitis is a condition that primarily affects postmenopausal women due to decreased estrogen levels. It would be inappropriate to prescribe a non-estrogen cream for a premenopausal woman.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
standard.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

easy.clinicalTip: You feel unwell and want help — the clinical team will guide next steps.
easy.objective: Share your symptoms clearly; ask what happens next.

standard.clinicalTip: Focus on what you feel, when it started, and what makes it worse or better.

hard.clinicalTip: Limited guidance — advocate for yourself if symptoms worsen.
hard.objective: Navigate uncertainty while the team works through the differential.

101.title: Fever
101.diagnosis: Undifferentiated Fever in an Adult or Pediatric Patient
exam: Fever — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
101.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
101.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Consider obtaining a lumbar puncture (in certain situations)
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing fever, which could be a symptom of various conditions including infections. Oxygen is necessary to support the patient's breathing.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: IV access is crucial for administering fluids and medications, especially in a critical situation like this.

interventions.label: universal labs + imaging
interventions.why: Universal labs such as CBC, CMP, UA, and imaging like a chest X-ray or CT scan are important for determining the cause of fever.

decoys.label: Administer antipyretics without first evaluating the cause of fever
decoys.why: Antipyretics should be used to alleviate symptoms, but it's crucial to identify the underlying cause of fever for appropriate treatment.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without blood culture
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be prescribed prior to obtaining blood cultures to confirm a bacterial infection.

decoys.label: Disregard fever history and perform unnecessary diagnostic tests
decoys.why: A thorough fever history is essential in guiding the selection of appropriate diagnostic tests, avoiding unnecessary procedures and costs.

decoys.label: Prescribe steroids without considering potential complications
decoys.why: Steroids should be used with caution due to potential complications such as immunosuppression, increased risk of infections, and adverse effects on bone growth in children.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

easy.clinicalTip: You feel unwell and want help — the clinical team will guide next steps.
easy.objective: Share your symptoms clearly; ask what happens next.

standard.clinicalTip: Focus on what you feel, when it started, and what makes it worse or better.
standard.objective: Participate in shared decision-making as the team stabilizes you.

hard.objective: Navigate uncertainty while the team works through the differential.

102.title: Generalized Weakness
102.diagnosis: Neurological Disorder (such as Guillain-Barre Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, or Multiple Sclerosis)
102.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
102.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
102.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Electrolytes, BUN, Creatinine, Glucose, LFTs, CMP, Coagulation Profile, Thyroid Function Tests (TFTs), Rheumatoid Factor, ANA, Vitamin B12 and Folate levels, CRP, ESR
Imaging studies: MRI with contrast of the brain and spine
Neurology consultation for further evaluation and management
Nerve conduction studies (if not performed already)
Respiratory support as needed due to potential risk of respiratory failure
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with generalized weakness, which could be a sign of hypoxia. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Establishing IV access and checking glucose levels are essential initial steps to address potential metabolic issues contributing to the patient's weakness.

decoys.label: Antibiotics
decoys.why: Neurological disorders are not caused by bacterial infections. Antibiotics would not be effective for treating symptoms related to neurological disorders.

decoys.label: Muscle relaxants
decoys.why: While muscle relaxants can be used to manage some symptoms of certain neurological disorders, they should be used with caution and under the guidance of a neurologist. In the early stages of disorders like Guillain-Barre Syndrome, for example, muscle relaxants may exacerbate weakness.

decoys.label: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can potentially worsen neurological symptoms by affecting blood flow to the brain. They are not typically used in the management of neurological disorders unless specifically indicated for a particular comorbidity.

decoys.label: Steroids
decoys.why: Steroids can be beneficial in certain situations (e.g., to reduce inflammation associated with Multiple Sclerosis), but they should generally not be the first line of treatment for neurological disorders. The correct choice would depend on the specific diagnosis and the stage of the disease.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: See HPI Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI

103.title: Routine Check-Up
exam: Routine Check-Up — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
103.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
103.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Complete Blood Count (CBC)
interventions.why: Helps to evaluate general health status, detect infections and anemia.

interventions.label: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP)
interventions.why: Assesses the metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, and helps to identify electrolyte imbalances, kidney function, and liver disease.

interventions.label: Urinalysis
interventions.why: Helps to evaluate kidney function, detect infections, or abnormalities such as protein or glucose in the urine.

interventions.label: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: Assesses the heart's electrical activity and can help identify potential cardiac problems.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps to diagnose conditions affecting the lungs, heart, and chest.

interventions.label: Consultation with Internal Medicine or Hospitalist
interventions.why: For further evaluation, management, and potential diagnostic procedures.

decoys.label: MRI of the brain
decoys.why: An MRI is typically not necessary for an initial workup for an undetermined condition, as it's primarily used to diagnose neurological disorders. The initial workup usually includes tests like CBC, CMP, urinalysis, ECG, and chest X-ray.

decoys.label: Lumbar Puncture
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture (spinal tap) is used to diagnose specific conditions such as meningitis or multiple sclerosis. It's not a routine test during an initial workup for an undetermined condition.

decoys.label: Bone Density Scan
decoys.why: A bone density scan is typically used to diagnose and monitor osteoporosis or other bone diseases. It's not usually part of the initial workup for an undetermined condition.

decoys.label: CT scan of abdomen
decoys.why: A CT scan of the abdomen is typically used when a specific organ or area in the abdomen needs to be imaged. In an initial workup for an undetermined condition, a chest X-ray would be more appropriate as it covers a broader area.

easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

104.title: Back Pain
104.diagnosis: Acute Low Back Pain of Undetermined Origin
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
104.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
104.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including the location, severity, onset, and aggravating/relieving factors of the back pain.
Perform a comprehensive physical examination with focus on neurological and orthopedic assessments.
Order diagnostic imaging such as X-rays or MRI to evaluate for potential causes like herniated discs, fractures, or spinal stenosis.
Refer patient to a specialist (e.g., Orthopedist, Neurologist, Physical Therapist) if the diagnosis remains uncertain after initial evaluation and imaging.
Prescribe non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for pain management as needed.

interventions.why: The text is clearly legible and appears to be a standard clinical order.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Back pain can be indicative of serious conditions that may require oxygen therapy. IV access is necessary for administering medication and fluids.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The patient may need fluids or medications intravenously.

decoys.label: Oxygen, IV access x2
decoys.why: Oxygen is not typically used in the treatment of acute low back pain. IV access might be needed for medications or fluids but not specifically for this condition.

decoys.label: Refer patient to a cardiologist
decoys.why: A cardiologist is not typically involved in the management of acute low back pain, unless there are cardiac complications or comorbidities that need to be ruled out.

decoys.label: Order a complete blood count (CBC)
decoys.why: While a CBC might be ordered as part of a broader workup, it is not typically the first diagnostic test for acute low back pain.

decoys.label: Prescribe opioids for long-term management
decoys.why: Opioids are generally reserved for short-term use in acute pain management due to their potential for addiction and side effects. Non-opioid medications like NSAIDs or muscle relaxants are usually preferred for chronic low back pain.

105.title: Abdominal Pain
105.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
105.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
105.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and physical examination
interventions.why: To assess the patient's overall condition and identify any immediate life-threatening conditions

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: CBC, Metabolic Panel, Lipase
Imaging: Abdominal CT scan with contrast
Consults: General Surgery, Emergency Medicine (if not the initial provider)
Treatment: NPO (nil per os) status, IV fluid administration, pain management
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain, which could indicate a variety of serious conditions that require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The patient needs IV access for administration of fluids and potentially other medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: A glucose check is important to rule out hypoglycemia as a possible cause of the abdominal pain.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without a definitive infection diagnosis
decoys.why: Administering antibiotics before a clear infection is identified can lead to unnecessary treatment and potential antibiotic resistance

decoys.label: Anti-inflammatory drugs for pain management in the initial stage of acute abdomen
decoys.why: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) can mask symptoms and delay the diagnosis, especially if there is a surgical emergency

decoys.label: Discharge the patient without further imaging or consultations
decoys.why: Without appropriate diagnostic tests and consultations, the cause of acute abdominal pain may remain undetermined and untreated, potentially leading to complications

decoys.label: Solid food or oral medication for a patient with NPO (nil per os) status
decoys.why: Administration of solid food or oral medication is contraindicated in patients who are NPO as it may obstruct diagnostic tests or complicate the clinical picture

106.title: Fatigue
106.diagnosis: Anemia (iron deficiency, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency anemia are common causes of fatigue in a patient without obvious symptoms)
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
106.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
106.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC) with differential and reticulocyte count
If CBC shows low hemoglobin, hematocrit or red blood cell count, consider ordering serum ferritin, total iron binding capacity, vitamin B12, and folate levels
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing fatigue, which could be a sign of hypoxia or respiratory distress. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for providing fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to fatigue. A glucose check helps rule out this potentially life-threatening condition.

decoys.label: Order calcium level
decoys.why: While calcium deficiency can cause fatigue, it is not a primary cause of anemia. Calcium levels would typically be ordered as part of the workup for hyperparathyroidism or other bone diseases, but not initially in anemia workup.

decoys.label: Order cortisol level
decoys.why: Cortisol levels are usually checked to diagnose Cushing's syndrome, Addison's disease, or other adrenal gland disorders. While chronic stress can lead to fatigue, it is not directly related to anemia.

decoys.label: Order urine analysis
decoys.why: Urine analysis is used to evaluate kidney function and diagnose urinary tract infections or other renal diseases. It does not provide information about anemia or the underlying cause of fatigue.

decoys.label: Order thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH)
decoys.why: Thyroid disorders can cause symptoms such as fatigue, but they are not typically associated with anemia. TSH levels would be ordered if hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism is suspected.

107.title: Paranoia
107.diagnosis: Psychiatric Emergency (e.g., Acute Psychosis, Schizophrenia Spectrum Disorder, Substance-Induced Psychosis)
exam: Paranoia — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
107.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
107.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs
interventions.why: To assess the general health status of the patient

interventions.label: Perform a thorough psychiatric evaluation including mental status examination
interventions.why: To evaluate the patient's mental state, cognitive function, and behavior

interventions.label: Obtain urine drug screen
interventions.why: To identify potential substance use that may be contributing to the patient's symptoms

interventions.label: Consult Psychiatry for further evaluation and management
interventions.why: For a thorough psychiatric assessment and treatment plan

interventions.label: Order electrolyte panel, complete blood count, glucose, thyroid function tests, and toxicology screen if indicated by clinical findings or substance use history
interventions.why: To rule out other medical causes that may be contributing to the patient's symptoms

interventions.label: If the patient is agitated or violent, administer haloperidol 5mg IM initially, followed by repeat doses as needed for control of symptoms (under close psychiatric monitoring)
interventions.why: To provide immediate management of acute agitation and aggression in a safe manner

interventions.label: Ensure patient safety: use soft restraints if necessary, observe continuously, and consider a brief hospitalization if needed
interventions.why: To ensure the safety of both the patient and staff during the initial evaluation and treatment process

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: For critical Principle in Step 3 CCS, if you delay stabilization while ordering labs, your score plummets — even with correct diagnosis.

decoys.label: Administer antidepressants
decoys.why: Antidepressants are typically not indicated for acute psychosis, schizophrenia spectrum disorder, or substance-induced psychosis. Instead, antipsychotics are usually prescribed.

decoys.label: Prescribe benzodiazepines as the primary treatment
decoys.why: Benzodiazepines may be used to manage symptoms of anxiety or agitation in psychiatric emergencies, but they are not typically the primary treatment for psychosis. Antipsychotics are usually prescribed instead.

decoys.label: Discharge the patient without a follow-up appointment
decoys.why: Patients experiencing a psychiatric emergency often require ongoing monitoring and support, including follow-up appointments with mental health professionals. Discharging them without appropriate follow-up could be detrimental to their recovery.

decoys.label: Administer electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) as an initial treatment
decoys.why: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is generally reserved for severe and treatment-resistant mood disorders, such as major depressive disorder or bipolar disorder. It is not typically the first line of treatment for acute psychosis, schizophrenia spectrum disorder, or substance-induced psychosis.

Further diagnostic workup based on imaging results
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide adequate oxygenation for the patient experiencing abdominal pain.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: For fluid resuscitation and potential administration of medications.

interventions.label: Placement of patient in appropriate location (ER → ICU vs floor)
interventions.why: Based on the nature and severity of the abdominal pain.

decoys.label: Administration of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can exacerbate gastrointestinal bleeding, which is a potential complication in acute abdomen. They should be avoided until the cause of abdominal pain has been determined.

decoys.label: Ordering only a Lipase test
decoys.why: While elevated lipase levels can indicate pancreatitis, a complete workup for acute abdomen should include other tests such as amylase, CBC, chemistry panel, and imaging to rule out other possible causes.

decoys.label: Prescribing oral pain medication
decoys.why: Oral medication may not be effective for patients with acute abdomen due to nausea or vomiting. Intravenous (IV) pain medication should be considered instead.

decoys.label: Ordering a contrast CT Abdomen without resuscitation
decoys.why: Patients with acute abdomen may require immediate fluid and oxygen resuscitation before undergoing imaging studies. A non-contrast CT Abdomen/Pelvis or Ultrasound Abdomen is typically performed first to avoid complications from contrast agents in unstable patients.

109.title: Unresponsive
109.diagnosis: Possible causes could include acute cerebrovascular event, sepsis, drug overdose, metabolic or electrolyte imbalance, or head trauma
exam: Unresponsive — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
109.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and initiate cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) if necessary.
Send laboratory tests: complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), coagulation studies, toxicology screen
Perform a non-contrast head CT to rule out acute cerebrovascular event or traumatic brain injury
Consult Neurology and Emergency Medicine for further management and potential intubation if airway compromise is suspected
Start fluid resuscitation with normal saline if patient is hypotensive
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is unresponsive, which can be a sign of respiratory distress or cardiac arrest. Oxygen is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: In an emergency situation such as this, large-bore IV access is crucial for rapid administration of fluids and medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause altered mental status and seizures. A glucose check is necessary to rule out hypoglycemia as a potential cause.

decoys.label: Administer anticoagulants
decoys.why: Inappropriate in this context as the patient may have an acute cerebrovascular event, and anticoagulation could worsen the condition.

decoys.label: Start a glucose drip
decoys.why: Without specific information about the patient's blood sugar levels, it is not clinically indicated to start a glucose drip. If the patient has been diagnosed with diabetes or has a history of hypoglycemia, monitoring blood sugar levels is important but starting a drip without checking blood sugar first would be incorrect.

decoys.label: Give benzodiazepines for seizure control
decoys.why: Seizures are not mentioned as a presenting symptom or possible complication. Benzodiazepines should only be given if seizure activity is observed or suspected.

decoys.label: Start an antibiotic without knowing the patient's temperature and culture results
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be administered based on clinical suspicion, microbiological data, and the patient's temperature. Starting an antibiotic without this information would be inappropriate.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

110.title: Rash
exam: Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
110.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
110.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including travel, recent medications, and exposure to potential allergens
Perform a comprehensive physical examination, focusing on the rash's distribution, morphology, and associated symptoms
Take skin scrapings or biopsy samples (with appropriate consent) for microscopic examination, cultures, and pathology
Order complete blood count (CBC), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and liver function tests (LFTs)
Consult Dermatology for further evaluation, potential diagnostic testing, and management guidance
Administer topical or systemic antipruritics to manage symptoms and prevent scratching-induced complications
Monitor the patient's vital signs, especially temperature, as some rashes can be associated with fever
Instruct the patient on appropriate skin care and hygiene to prevent infection
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with a rash, which could potentially require oxygen support if severe distress or AMS occurs.

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: Establishing IV access is crucial for administering medications and fluids.

decoys.label: Prescribe oral antibiotics without obtaining a culture
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be prescribed without first determining the cause of the rash, as many rashes are viral and antibiotics are ineffective against them. A culture is necessary to determine the cause.

decoys.label: Apply a topical steroid cream without dermatologist consultation
decoys.why: Topical steroids should be used with caution and under a dermatologist's guidance, as they can have side effects such as skin thinning and systemic absorption. They should not be used without proper evaluation of the rash.

decoys.label: Administer oral antihistamines for itching without monitoring vital signs
decoys.why: Oral antihistamines can cause drowsiness and other side effects. It is important to monitor the patient's vital signs, especially temperature, as some rashes can be associated with fever.

decoys.label: Ignore the rash and wait for it to resolve on its own without any evaluation
decoys.why: Many rashes can be serious or indicative of underlying conditions. It is important to perform a comprehensive physical examination, consult Dermatology, and order appropriate tests to determine the cause and appropriate management.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

111.title: Shortness of Breath
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
111.clinical_tip: Triad after long-bone fracture: hypoxemia, AMS, petechial rash (axilla/neck).
111.objective: ICU support — recognition and stabilization; no specific drug cure.

interventions.label: Intensive care unit
interventions.why: Respiratory failure and AMS need ICU.

interventions.label: Respiratory support as needed
interventions.why: Escalate to mechanical ventilation if tiring.

decoys.label: Antiplatelet medications (e.g., Aspirin)
decoys.why: Fat embolism syndrome is caused by the release of fat droplets into the bloodstream, leading to coagulation and thrombosis, not clot dissolution. Therefore, antiplatelet medications are not indicated.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: Fat embolism syndrome is characterized by inflammation and coagulation. NSAIDs, while they may help reduce symptoms of inflammation, could potentially worsen bleeding tendencies due to fat-induced thrombocytopenia.

decoys.label: Corticosteroids (e.g., Prednisone)
decoys.why: While corticosteroids may have a role in managing some complications of fat embolism syndrome, such as acute respiratory distress syndrome or multi-organ dysfunction, they should be used cautiously due to their immunosuppressive effects and risk of exacerbating the underlying fat emboli.

decoys.label: Antihypertensives
decoys.why: Fat embolism syndrome can cause acute hypertension due to release of catecholamines. However, treating hypertension in this context may lead to hypotension, which could worsen tissue perfusion and exacerbate organ damage.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Triad after long-bone fracture: hypoxemia, AMS, petechial rash (axilla/neck).
easy.objective: ICU support — recognition and stabilization; no specific drug cure. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Triad after long-bone fracture: hypoxemia, AMS, petechial rash (axilla/neck).
standard.objective: ICU support — recognition and stabilization; no specific drug cure.

112.title: Found Unconscious
112.diagnosis: Possible diagnoses: Stroke, Severe head injury, Cardiac arrest, Hypoglycemia, or other life-threatening conditions
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
112.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
Order a complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), coagulation profile, glucose level, electrocardiogram (ECG), and computed tomography (CT) scan of the head
Initiate Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) measures if there are signs of cardiac arrest
Administer intravenous dextrose 50% in case of suspected hypoglycemia
Obtain a detailed history from family or witnesses, if available
Consult Neurology, Neurosurgery, and/or Cardiology as needed based on findings
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is found unconscious, indicating potential respiratory distress. Therefore, oxygen is a life-saving intervention that must be ordered immediately.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access and glucose check are also necessary for immediate patient stabilization.

decoys.label: Administration of antihypertensive drugs
decoys.why: In a patient found unconscious, it is not appropriate to administer antihypertensive drugs without first obtaining blood pressure readings. The initial focus should be on stabilizing the patient's vital signs.

decoys.label: Ordering a lumbar puncture
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture (LP) is contraindicated in patients with a possible stroke or severe head injury due to an increased risk of causing herniation. A CT scan of the head should be performed first.

decoys.label: Initiating antibiotics
decoys.why: Without knowing the cause of unconsciousness, it is not appropriate to administer antibiotics. Antibiotics are typically used for infections, and the patient's condition may be due to a life-threatening condition other than an infection.

decoys.label: Ordering a chest X-ray
decoys.why: While a chest X-ray is important in some cases, it should not be the initial priority for a patient found unconscious with possible diagnoses of stroke, severe head injury, cardiac arrest, or hypoglycemia. The focus should be on stabilizing the patient and determining the cause of their condition.

113.title: Drowning
113.diagnosis: Acute Respiratory Failure due to Drowning (if not conscious or breathing is compromised)
exam: Drowning — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
113.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
113.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Oxygen via nasal cannula, non-rebreather mask or endotracheal intubation for airway management and oxygenation
Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis to assess respiratory and metabolic status
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing an unknown condition related to drowning, which could lead to respiratory distress. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Establishing IV access and checking glucose level are essential initial steps in stabilizing the patient.

decoys.label: Administration of diuretics
decoys.why: Diuretics are not indicated for acute respiratory failure due to drowning as they can further decrease the intravascular volume, exacerbating hypotension and compromising oxygen delivery.

decoys.label: Administering sedatives without airway management
decoys.why: Sedatives should not be administered before securing the airway due to the risk of worsening airway obstruction, hypoventilation, or loss of consciousness that could lead to aspiration.

decoys.label: Administering antibiotics without a known infection
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not typically indicated for acute respiratory failure due to drowning as it is usually caused by hypoxia and fluid aspiration, not bacterial infection. Overuse of antibiotics contributes to antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Ordering chest X-ray immediately upon arrival
decoys.why: A chest X-ray may be useful later to assess for complications such as pneumonia, but it is not usually the first diagnostic test needed for acute respiratory failure due to drowning. Immediate attention should be focused on airway management, oxygenation, and circulatory support.

114.title: Pain in Side
114.diagnosis: Possible Renal Colic due to obstructive uropathy or Appendicitis
exam: Pain in Side — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
114.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
114.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Chemistry Panel, Urinalysis
Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen administration is crucial for any case involving respiratory distress or AMS.

interventions.label: IV access x2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for rapid fluid resuscitation if necessary.

decoys.label: Amylase blood test
decoys.why: Although amylase can be elevated in appendicitis, it is not a specific diagnostic marker for this condition. It's more commonly used to diagnose pancreatitis.

decoys.label: Ultrasound of the kidney only
decoys.why: Although ultrasound can help identify kidney stones, it may not be sufficient for detecting the obstruction in ureters (obstructive uropathy). CT scan is more reliable for this purpose.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without further diagnostic workup
decoys.why: Without a definitive diagnosis, administering antibiotics could be inappropriate and potentially lead to unnecessary treatment or resistance development.

decoys.label: MRI instead of CT scan
decoys.why: CT scan is typically the imaging modality of choice for suspected renal colic or appendicitis due to its speed, high resolution, and ability to quickly detect stones and inflammation.

115.title: Agitation
115.diagnosis: Possible Neurological or Psychiatric Disorder, such as Delirium, Dementia, Seizure Disorder, Mood Disorders, Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder
exam: Agitation — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
115.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
115.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and perform a comprehensive physical examination to assess for any acute medical conditions
Obtain laboratory tests (CBC, Electrolytes, Glucose, LFTs, UA) to rule out metabolic causes or other systemic illnesses
Obtain a toxicology screen to evaluate for substance use
Order a head CT scan to assess for structural brain abnormalities
Consider EEG if seizure activity is suspected
Psychiatric consultation for evaluation and management of agitation and possible underlying psychiatric disorder
Administer haloperidol or other antipsychotics, if necessary, to manage acute symptoms under close monitoring due to potential side effects
Oxygen
interventions.why: Agitation can be a sign of respiratory distress or other serious conditions, necessitating immediate oxygen administration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: In case the patient's condition worsens, having large-bore IV access will ensure adequate fluid resuscitation.

decoys.label: Haloperidol without a thorough evaluation and management plan by a psychiatrist
decoys.why: Haloperidol should not be administered without a comprehensive assessment by a mental health professional to ensure appropriate dosing and monitoring for potential side effects.

decoys.label: Obtain laboratory tests (CBC, Electrolytes, Glucose, LFTs, UA) for the sole purpose of diagnosing a neurological or psychiatric disorder
decoys.why: Lab tests are used to rule out metabolic causes or other systemic illnesses that may contribute to agitation, not to diagnose neurological or psychiatric disorders directly.

decoys.label: Administer antipsychotics without close monitoring for potential side effects
decoys.why: Antipsychotics should be administered under close monitoring due to the risk of adverse effects such as extrapyramidal symptoms, QTc prolongation, and metabolic changes.

decoys.label: Order a head CT scan without considering EEG if seizure activity is suspected
decoys.why: Both head CT scan and EEG are used in the assessment of seizures, with EEG providing more information about the electrical activity of the brain.

116.title: Headache
116.diagnosis: Primary Headache (such as Migraine or Tension-type headache), although other serious conditions like Subarachnoid Hemorrhage cannot be ruled out
116.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
116.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
116.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Initial assessment and monitoring for potential signs of serious conditions (e.g., neurological examination, vital signs, and observation)
Imaging (CT or MRI) if the clinical situation warrants it to rule out structural causes such as Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide immediate support for the patient experiencing a headache, which could be a symptom of various critical conditions.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: For potential fluid resuscitation and administration of necessary medications.

decoys.label: Analgesic without first ensuring no contraindications or comorbidities
decoys.why: It's important to assess the patient for any underlying conditions, medication allergies, or current medications that could contraindicate the use of analgesics.

decoys.label: Over-the-counter pain relievers for acute headache management
decoys.why: Prescribing over-the-counter pain relievers may not provide adequate management for a potential Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. These medications do not address the underlying cause and may delay necessary diagnostic procedures.

decoys.label: Prescription of ergotamine tartrate
decoys.why: Ergotamine tartrate is not recommended as a first-line treatment for headaches due to its potential vasoconstrictive effects. Its use should be cautious, and only after other treatments have been tried, because it can worsen or mask signs of serious conditions such as Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.

decoys.label: Dismissing the need for neurological consultation
decoys.why: Patients with acute headaches require a neurological examination to rule out serious underlying conditions, even if the initial diagnosis suggests a primary headache. A neurologist can help determine whether further imaging is necessary or if close monitoring is sufficient.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 117.title: Chest Pain
117.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses for a patient presenting with unknown chest pain.
117.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
117.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
117.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: EKG: To evaluate for signs of ischemia or rhythm abnormalities
Troponin level: For diagnosis and monitoring of ACS, including myocardial infarction
Chest X-ray: May help identify conditions such as pneumonia, pulmonary edema, or aortic dissection
Cardiology consult: For further evaluation, management, and potential intervention
Pain management: Administration of pain relievers appropriate for acute chest pain
Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various conditions, including cardiac ischemia, and oxygen should be provided immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

interventions.label: order: time-sensitive interventions first
interventions.why: Stabilizing the patient's vital signs is crucial in managing chest pain cases.

decoys.label: MRI scan of the brain
decoys.why: An MRI scan of the brain is not necessary for a patient presenting with chest pain, as it does not provide information relevant to the diagnosis and management of acute coronary syndrome or other cardiac conditions.

decoys.label: Lab work-up for viral infection
decoys.why: While viral infections can cause chest pain, they are not the most likely causes in this context. The initial focus should be on diagnosing and managing potential cardiac conditions, such as acute coronary syndrome.

decoys.label: Diuretic for heart failure
decoys.why: Heart failure is a possible complication of acute coronary syndrome, but it's not the initial diagnosis in a patient with unknown chest pain. The priority should be to establish the correct diagnosis, either ACS or other cardiac conditions.

decoys.label: Adenosine stress test
decoys.why: An adenosine stress test is used for diagnosing coronary artery disease, but it's not appropriate in a patient with acute chest pain. In this context, the priority should be to evaluate for signs of ischemia or rhythm abnormalities first, as per the time-sensitive interventions.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

118.title: Muscle Pain
exam: Muscle Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
118.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
118.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory studies (CBC, ESR, CRP, Creatinine kinase, LFT)
Order imaging studies (MRI or Ultrasound for specific areas of pain)
MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

decoys.label: Prescribe non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) without initial laboratory studies
decoys.why: NSAIDs can cause gastrointestinal bleeding and kidney damage, therefore, it is important to assess renal function and complete blood count before starting NSAIDs.

decoys.label: Prescribe opioids for non-specific musculoskeletal pain without appropriate evaluation and management of the underlying cause
decoys.why: Opioids should be used as a last resort in managing chronic pain due to their potential for addiction, dependence, and serious side effects. Non-pharmacological interventions such as physical therapy, exercise, and cognitive behavioral therapy should be tried first.

decoys.label: Order X-ray instead of MRI or Ultrasound
decoys.why: X-rays are not sensitive enough to detect soft tissue injuries, such as muscles, ligaments, and tendons. MRI or ultrasound is preferred for evaluating non-specific musculoskeletal pain.

decoys.label: Prescribe steroids without initial laboratory studies
decoys.why: Steroids can have serious side effects, including osteoporosis, increased risk of infections, and adverse effects on glucose metabolism. Therefore, it is important to assess renal function, liver function, and complete blood count before starting steroids.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

119.title: Sore Throat
119.diagnosis: Streptococcal pharyngitis (strep throat) or viral pharyngitis as the most likely diagnosis in a patient presenting with sore throat of unknown etiology
exam: Sore Throat — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
119.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
119.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Prescribe antibiotics for suspected streptococcal pharyngitis (if rapid strep test is positive)
order1
interventions.why: Reason for why 'order1' should be avoided.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics for suspected viral pharyngitis
decoys.why: Antibiotics are ineffective against viral infections like viral pharyngitis. Only bacterial infections, such as streptococcal pharyngitis, respond to antibiotics.

decoys.label: Prescribe penicillin for suspected streptococcal pharyngitis
decoys.why: Penicillin resistance is common among some strains of Group A Streptococcus (the bacteria that causes strep throat). Therefore, it's essential to consider local antibiotic resistance patterns when choosing an appropriate antibiotic.

decoys.label: Prescribe a 10-day course of amoxicillin for suspected viral pharyngitis
decoys.why: As mentioned earlier, antibiotics are ineffective against viral infections. A 10-day course is typically prescribed for bacterial infections like strep throat.

decoys.label: Prescribe azithromycin for suspected streptococcal pharyngitis without obtaining a rapid strep test
decoys.why: Azithromycin is an effective treatment for strep throat when the diagnosis is confirmed by a rapid strep test or throat culture. Without confirmation, it could lead to unnecessary antibiotic use and contribute to antimicrobial resistance.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

120.title: Sore Throat
120.diagnosis: Streptococcal Pharyngitis (Strep throat) or Viral Pharyngitis
exam: Sore Throat — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
120.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
120.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a rapid streptococcal antigen test
interventions.why: To confirm or rule out Strep throat, as the test is quick and can guide appropriate antibiotic therapy.

interventions.label: Prescribe a broad-spectrum antibiotic for empiric treatment (e.g., Amoxicillin)
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with a sore throat, which could potentially indicate airway distress. Therefore, administering oxygen is the first priority.

decoys.label: Prescribe steroids
decoys.why: Steroids are not typically used for the treatment of strep throat or viral pharyngitis. They may be used for symptoms relief in severe cases but antibiotics are the primary treatment for bacterial strep throat.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antihistamine
decoys.why: Antihistamines are typically used to treat allergies, not sore throats. While they may provide some relief from symptoms, they do not address the underlying cause which is a bacterial or viral infection.

decoys.label: Order CT scan of the neck
decoys.why: A CT scan is generally not necessary for diagnosing strep throat or viral pharyngitis. It may be used to rule out other conditions such as abscesses or tumors, but these are less common.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without a rapid streptococcal antigen test
decoys.why: Before prescribing antibiotics for suspected strep throat, it is recommended to first perform a rapid streptococcal antigen test. This helps confirm the diagnosis and ensures that antibiotics are only given when necessary.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

121.title: Poor Feeding
121.diagnosis: Failure to Thrive (FTT) or Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) are common considerations for Poor Feeding of unknown cause in pediatric patients.
exam: Poor Feeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
121.clinical_tip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
121.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough history, including feeding habits, weight gain, vomiting, and bowel movements
Physical examination to assess growth parameters, signs of dehydration, abdominal distension, or other relevant findings
Investigations such as complete blood count (CBC), chemistry panel, urinalysis, stool studies, and electrolytes to rule out potential underlying causes like infections, metabolic disorders, or anemia
Consider a swallowing study (videofluoroscopy) or pH monitoring if GERD is suspected
Refer the patient to a pediatric gastroenterologist for further evaluation and management
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide supplemental oxygen and help maintain adequate oxygenation in the patient with poor feeding

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: To establish two large-bore IV access for fluid administration, medication delivery, and blood draws

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To quickly assess the blood glucose level of the patient to rule out hypoglycemia as a possible cause of poor feeding

decoys.label: Immediate discharge without thorough history or physical examination
decoys.why: It is crucial to gather comprehensive information about the child's feeding habits, weight gain, vomiting, bowel movements, and other relevant symptoms before making any decisions.

decoys.label: Prescribe medication for GERD without confirming diagnosis through diagnostic tests
decoys.why: Before prescribing medications for GERD, it is essential to first perform investigations such as a swallowing study or pH monitoring to confirm the suspicion of GERD.

decoys.label: Initiate force feeding of solid foods to an infant with Poor Feeding
decoys.why: Force-feeding can be harmful and counterproductive in cases of Poor Feeding. Instead, it is important to identify the underlying cause and provide appropriate nutrition support.

decoys.label: Neglect to monitor weight gain and growth parameters
decoys.why: Regular monitoring of weight gain and growth parameters is crucial for children with Poor Feeding as it can help track the effectiveness of interventions and prevent further complications.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
easy.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

122.title: Painful Rash
exam: Painful Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
122.clinical_tip: Burn unit or ICU transfer is mandatory — not a ward case.
122.objective: Aggressive fluids, pain control, electrolytes, ophthalmology involvement.

interventions.label: Burn unit / ICU transfer
interventions.why: Major point item — wrong location loses points.

interventions.label: IV fluid resuscitation
interventions.why: Large insensible losses from skin slough.

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are typically ineffective for Stevens-Johnson Syndrome as it is a cell-mediated hypersensitivity reaction, not an allergic reaction.

decoys.label: Topical steroids
decoys.why: Topical steroids can be harmful in Stevens-Johnson Syndrome due to the risk of skin absorption and systemic effects. Systemic corticosteroids may be used after the onset of mucocutaneous lesions but not before.

decoys.label: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs should be avoided in Stevens-Johnson Syndrome due to the risk of exacerbating the disease and causing gastrointestinal complications.

decoys.label: Oral antibiotics without infection confirmation
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be given empirically in Stevens-Johnson Syndrome. They should only be used if there is evidence of a secondary bacterial infection.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Burn unit or ICU transfer is mandatory — not a ward case.

123.title: Leg Pain
123.diagnosis: Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD), Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or Neuropathy
exam: Leg Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
123.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
123.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Request an Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) study
interventions.why: This noninvasive test is used to diagnose PAD by measuring the blood pressure in the arms and legs. A reduced ABI suggests PAD.

interventions.label: Consider ordering electrodiagnostic studies for neuropathy workup if neurological symptoms are present
interventions.why: These tests can help identify nerve damage or dysfunction.

interventions.label: Refer the patient to a vascular surgeon or interventional radiologist for further imaging and potential intervention
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide immediate support for the patient experiencing leg pain, which may be a sign of an underlying medical condition.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To quickly assess blood glucose levels and address any potential hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

decoys.label: Order MRI scan
decoys.why: MRI is not typically the first-line diagnostic test for leg pain caused by PAD, DVT, or Neuropathy. Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) study, Doppler ultrasound, and blood tests are usually performed initially.

decoys.label: Prescribe aspirin
decoys.why: Although aspirin has antiplatelet properties and may be prescribed for PAD or DVT prevention in some cases, it is not a diagnostic test and should not be the initial action.

decoys.label: Refer to a neurologist for a lumbar puncture
decoys.why: Lumbar puncture (LP) is used to diagnose certain neurological conditions, but it's not appropriate in the context of leg pain due to PAD, DVT, or Neuropathy. LP may be indicated for cases where neurological symptoms are present and suggestive of a central nervous system disorder.

decoys.label: Prescribe insulin therapy
decoys.why: Insulin therapy is used to manage diabetes, which can contribute to the development of peripheral neuropathy. However, it is not a correct order in the context of leg pain caused by PAD, DVT, or Neuropathy as an initial diagnosis.

easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

124.title: Chest Pain
124.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses in a patient presenting with chest pain of unknown etiology.
124.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
124.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
124.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain immediate ECG
interventions.why: To evaluate for signs of ischemia, such as ST-segment elevations, depression, or T-wave inversions

interventions.label: Order serum biomarkers such as Troponin I/T
interventions.why: To assess myocardial injury and help confirm a diagnosis of ACS

interventions.label: Admit the patient for continuous monitoring and further investigation
interventions.why: Given the unclear etiology, close observation is necessary to identify any signs of acute decompensation or progression

interventions.label: Consult Cardiology
interventions.why: For expert advice on management strategies and potential interventions such as PCI or CABG

interventions.label: Prescribe aspirin (unless contraindicated) and other antiplatelet agents as indicated
interventions.why: To reduce the risk of thrombosis, which is a common complication in ACS

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing chest pain, which could be a life-threatening condition. Oxygen is essential to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Establishing IV access is crucial for administering medications and fluids quickly if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to chest pain, so it's important to rule this out early.

decoys.label: Administer digitalis
decoys.why: Digitalis is not used in the treatment of acute coronary syndrome. It's primarily used for heart failure management.

decoys.label: Prescribe beta-blockers initially
decoys.why: Beta-blockers should be initiated with caution in ACS patients, and typically only after the patient is stable, due to their potential to mask symptoms or worsen heart failure.

decoys.label: Prescribe nitrates as the first line of treatment
decoys.why: While nitrates can help manage angina and acute coronary syndrome symptoms, they are typically used in conjunction with other treatments such as aspirin, heparin, and clopidogrel.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the brain
decoys.why: A CT scan of the brain is not indicated for a patient presenting with chest pain and suspected acute coronary syndrome. It would be appropriate if there were signs or symptoms suggesting a neurological issue.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

125.title: Chest Pain
125.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses for a patient presenting with Chest Pain of unknown etiology.
125.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
125.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
125.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain ECG
interventions.why: ECG can provide initial information about the possible cause of chest pain, such as ischemia or arrhythmia.

interventions.label: Order cardiac enzymes (troponin and CK-MB)
interventions.why: Cardiac enzyme levels can indicate myocardial damage, which is suggestive of ACS.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions, and immediate oxygen administration is crucial to support respiration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids and medications quickly in case of an emergency.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause or mimic symptoms of chest pain.

decoys.label: Antibiotics
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not indicated for Acute Coronary Syndrome, as it is a cardiovascular condition and not an infectious disease.

decoys.label: CT Scan of the Brain
decoys.why: Although brain imaging may be considered in differential diagnosis, it's less likely than cardiac imaging due to the patient's presentation of chest pain.

decoys.label: CPAP therapy
decoys.why: CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) is used for sleep apnea or respiratory failure, not for acute coronary syndrome.

decoys.label: Diuretic
decoys.why: Although diuretics may be used in heart failure management, they are usually not the initial treatment for Acute Coronary Syndrome. Initial focus is on reperfusion therapy and pain management.

126.title: Shortness of Breath
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
126.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
126.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Obtain arterial blood gases (ABG)
interventions.why: To assess the level of oxygenation and acid-base status

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: To evaluate for pulmonary edema, pneumonia, or consolidation

interventions.label: Echocardiogram
interventions.why: To evaluate cardiac function and identify any structural heart disease

interventions.label: D-dimer level
interventions.why: To help rule out deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism

interventions.label: Troponin I or T
interventions.why: To rule out acute coronary syndrome

interventions.label: Complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, infection, or inflammation

interventions.label: Coagulation studies
interventions.why: To help rule out DVT or pulmonary embolism

interventions.label: CPAP or BiPAP if respiratory distress is present
interventions.why: To provide oxygenation and ventilation support

interventions.label: Cardiology consult
interventions.why: For further evaluation and management of potential cardiac causes

interventions.label: Internal medicine consult
interventions.why: For further evaluation and management of potential non-cardiac causes

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen delivery is crucial for patients experiencing shortness of breath.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary to administer fluids, medications, and blood products.

decoys.label: Antihistamine
decoys.why: Antihistamines are typically used for allergic reactions, not dyspnea of unknown origin. The root cause could be various respiratory or cardiovascular conditions that require different treatment approaches.

decoys.label: Bronchodilator Inhaler
decoys.why: While bronchodilators can help with asthma and COPD symptoms, they may not be effective for dyspnea of unknown origin as the cause could be due to a variety of other conditions like heart failure, pneumonia, or pulmonary embolism.

decoys.label: Nebulized Saline
decoys.why: Nebulized saline is often used for relieving symptoms associated with colds and respiratory infections, but it may not be effective in treating dyspnea of unknown origin as the underlying cause could be different.

decoys.label: Oxygen without further investigation
decoys.why: While oxygen therapy can help alleviate symptoms for some patients with shortness of breath, it's important to perform investigations like ABG, chest X-ray, and echocardiogram to determine the underlying cause and guide appropriate treatment.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

127.title: Rash
exam: Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
127.clinical_tip: Stool guaiac and blood cultures are required red items in pediatrics.
127.objective: Isolated thrombocytopenia workup; treat if bleeding or very low platelets.

interventions.label: Office or ED as acuity dictates
CBC
interventions.why: Isolated low platelets after viral illness.

interventions.label: Peripheral smear
interventions.why: Rule out pseudothrombocytopenia and other causes.

interventions.label: Observation or IVIG / steroids
interventions.why: Treat if platelets <20k or active bleeding.

decoys.label: Unnecessary Chest X-ray
decoys.why: Chest X-ray is not relevant for diagnosing Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP), as it does not directly relate to platelet counts or the immune system.

decoys.label: Unnecessary Echocardiogram
decoys.why: While thrombocytopenia can potentially lead to bleeding complications, an echocardiogram is typically not indicated for diagnosing ITP unless there are specific signs of cardiac involvement such as pericardial effusion or valvular heart disease.

decoys.label: Unnecessary CT scan of abdomen and pelvis
decoys.why: CT scans are not routinely used in the diagnostic workup for ITP, as they do not provide specific information about platelet dysfunction or immune system involvement.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without infection signs
decoys.why: ITP is an autoimmune disorder and not typically associated with bacterial infections. Prescribing antibiotics without evidence of infection could expose the patient to unnecessary side effects and potential drug interactions.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stool guaiac and blood cultures are required red items in pediatrics.
easy.objective: Isolated thrombocytopenia workup; treat if bleeding or very low platelets. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Stool guaiac and blood cultures are required red items in pediatrics.
standard.objective: Isolated thrombocytopenia workup; treat if bleeding or very low platelets.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Isolated thrombocytopenia workup; treat if bleeding or very low platelets.
