128.title: Depression
128.diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder is the most likely diagnosis for a patient presenting with Depression of unknown etiology.
exam: Depression — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
128.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
128.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Psychiatric Consult
interventions.why: To assess the severity of depression, rule out other psychiatric disorders, and guide further management.

interventions.label: Laboratory workup (CBC, Chemistry Panel, TSH, Urinalysis)
Psychological Evaluation
interventions.why: To assess the patient's psychological state and functioning, which can aid in diagnosis and treatment planning.

decoys.label: Antidepressant medication without proper evaluation
decoys.why: It's important to conduct a thorough evaluation before prescribing antidepressants, including a psychiatric consultation, laboratory workup, psychological evaluation, and cardiac monitoring.

decoys.label: Antipsychotic medication without proper diagnosis
decoys.why: Since the patient's depression has an unknown etiology, it is not appropriate to prescribe antipsychotics without a definitive diagnosis.

decoys.label: Physical therapy sessions
decoys.why: Physical therapy is typically not indicated for major depressive disorder. Instead, psychotherapy and pharmacological interventions are the primary treatments.

decoys.label: Neuroimaging (MRI or PET scan) as first-line diagnosis
decoys.why: While neuroimaging can be useful in certain cases of depression, it should not be the initial step in diagnosing major depressive disorder. A comprehensive evaluation including psychiatric consultation, laboratory workup, and psychological evaluation is necessary.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
standard.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

129.title: Facial Pain
129.diagnosis: Trigeminal neuralgia or other neuropathic facial pain (due to facial pain presentation)
exam: Facial Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
129.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, focusing on the onset, duration, and characteristics of the pain
Perform a neurological examination, including cranial nerve evaluation
Order MRI or CT with contrast to evaluate for any structural lesions in the trigeminal nerve region
Consider referral to a neurologist or neurosurgeon for further evaluation and management if MRI/CT reveals abnormalities
Initiate pain management as needed, such as carbamazepine or gabapentin, while awaiting specialist consultation
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide immediate oxygen support for the patient experiencing facial pain.

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: To establish large-bore IV access in preparation for fluid resuscitation and administration of medications.

decoys.label: Order a blood test for trigeminal neuralgia
decoys.why: Trigeminal neuralgia is a nerve disorder, not a condition that can be diagnosed by a blood test.

decoys.label: Prescribe opioids as the primary treatment for trigeminal neuralgia without trying other medications first
decoys.why: Opioids should generally be avoided as the initial treatment for neuropathic pain like trigeminal neuralgia due to the risk of addiction and side effects. Other medications such as carbamazepine or gabapentin are typically tried first.

decoys.label: Order an electromyography (EMG) as the initial diagnostic test for trigeminal neuralgia
decoys.why: While EMG can be used to diagnose some nerve disorders, it is not typically the first line of investigation for trigeminal neuralgia. An MRI or CT scan with contrast is usually the preferred initial imaging study.

decoys.label: Recommend surgery as the primary treatment for trigeminal neuralgia without trying medications first
decoys.why: Surgery should generally be considered after medications have failed to provide adequate relief. For many patients with trigeminal neuralgia, medication management can effectively control symptoms.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
standard.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes.

130.title: Cough
130.diagnosis: Possible Pneumonia, Bronchitis, or Asthma (based on cough as the chief complaint)
exam: Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
130.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
130.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel
Administer oxygen therapy if oxygen saturation levels are low
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically (if fever, purulent sputum, or signs of bacterial infection)
interventions.why: To address potential bacterial infections that could be causing the cough

interventions.label: Consider pulmonology consultation for further evaluation and management
Monitor respiratory status and vital signs
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This bundle includes electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests which are important to evaluate a patient with an unknown diagnosis. It also includes urinalysis to evaluate for potential infections or other causes of cough.

interventions.label: UA ± culture
interventions.why: Urinalysis can help identify urinary tract infections (UTI) as a possible cause of the patient's cough.

decoys.label: Start antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are not typically used for pneumonia, bronchitis, or asthma. They are more commonly used for allergies.

decoys.label: Administer corticosteroids without a diagnosis of Asthma
decoys.why: Corticosteroids should be administered with caution and only if a patient has been diagnosed with Asthma, as they can have side effects.

decoys.label: Prescribe expectorants for bronchitis without a cough
decoys.why: Expectorants are used to help loosen and thin mucus in the lungs to make it easier to cough up. They would not be effective if there is no cough.

decoys.label: Order an electrocardiogram (ECG) instead of chest X-ray
decoys.why: An ECG is used to evaluate the heart, while a chest X-ray is used to diagnose and monitor conditions affecting the lungs such as pneumonia or asthma.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
standard.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

131.title: Abdominal Pain
131.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
131.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
131.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Order abdominal ultrasound or CT scan
Consider obtaining electrocardiogram (ECG) to rule out ischemic heart disease
CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

decoys.label: Order a head CT scan
decoys.why: A head CT is not relevant to the diagnosis of acute abdomen. The symptoms suggest an issue in the abdominal area, not the head.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without culture and sensitivity
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be prescribed based on culture and sensitivity results to ensure appropriate treatment and prevent antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Order a liver function test (LFT) as the primary diagnostic test for acute abdomen
decoys.why: LFT is not a primary diagnostic test for acute abdomen. It can be used to assess liver function if there are specific indications, but a comprehensive approach including imaging and other lab tests is necessary.

decoys.label: Administer antacids without considering the possibility of perforation or obstruction
decoys.why: Antacids may help with symptoms of ulcers or reflux, but they are not effective for treating perforations or obstructions. These conditions require urgent surgical intervention.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. 132.diagnosis: Likely diagnoses may include traumatic injuries, neuropathies, or arthritis. However, a definitive diagnosis will require further investigation.

Order blood tests (complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein) to evaluate for systemic inflammation
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing foot pain, which may indicate an issue requiring immediate attention. Oxygen is essential to help maintain adequate oxygenation levels in case of hypoxia.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Intravenous access is necessary for administering medications, fluids, and other life-saving interventions.

decoys.label: Prescribe pain medication without identifying the cause of foot pain
decoys.why: It's important to determine the cause of the foot pain before prescribing medications, as some treatments may interact with certain conditions or exacerbate underlying issues.

decoys.label: Refer patient for immediate surgery without proper diagnosis
decoys.why: Surgery should only be considered after a thorough diagnostic evaluation and discussion of the benefits, risks, and alternatives with the patient.

decoys.label: Prescribe topical creams for systemic inflammation
decoys.why: Topical creams are not effective in treating systemic inflammation which requires systemic treatment such as medications or lifestyle changes.

decoys.label: Recommend physical therapy without first assessing the type and severity of foot injury
decoys.why: Physical therapy should be tailored to the specific injury or condition, which requires a proper diagnosis beforehand.

133.title: Back Pain
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

decoys.label: Physical therapy before diagnosis
decoys.why: Physical therapy is not typically used as a diagnostic tool for Ankylosing Spondylitis. It is usually initiated after the correct diagnosis has been made to help manage symptoms.

decoys.label: Prescription of corticosteroids (e.g., Prednisone) as initial treatment
decoys.why: Corticosteroids are typically used in acute phases or severe cases, not as the first line of treatment for Ankylosing Spondylitis. NSAIDs like Indomethacin or Naproxen are usually prescribed initially.

decoys.label: Recommendation for a full spine MRI instead of X-ray sacroiliac joints
decoys.why: A full spine MRI is not necessary for the initial diagnosis of Ankylosing Spondylitis. X-rays of the sacroiliac joints are more commonly used to identify the characteristic changes associated with this condition.

decoys.label: Recommendation for blood tests for Rheumatoid Factor or Antinuclear Antibodies
decoys.why: Rheumatoid Factor and Antinuclear Antibodies are not typically associated with Ankylosing Spondylitis. HLA-B27 is a more relevant marker for this condition.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Morning back stiffness improving with activity — image sacroiliac joints, not lumbar spine alone.

134.title: Scrotal Pain
134.diagnosis: Acute Scrotal Pain could be due to various conditions such as Testicular Torsion, Epididymitis, Orchitis, or torsion of appendix testis. However, since the diagnosis is unknown, further diagnostic tests are required.
exam: Scrotal Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
134.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
134.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Consult Urology for further evaluation and potential surgical intervention
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide oxygen for the patient experiencing scrotal pain.

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: For administration of fluids and medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To rule out hypoglycemia as a potential cause.

decoys.label: Antibiotics
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not typically the first-line treatment for acute scrotal pain, as the cause is often not bacterial. Testicular torsion, one of the possible causes, requires urgent surgical intervention.

decoys.label: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs may provide temporary pain relief but are not appropriate for acute scrotal pain because they do not address the underlying condition. Testicular torsion, a medical emergency, requires immediate surgical intervention.

decoys.label: Aspirin
decoys.why: Aspirin is typically used for cardiovascular conditions and not for acute scrotal pain. Moreover, it may mask the symptoms of testicular torsion, which is a medical emergency requiring urgent attention.

decoys.label: Ice pack application
decoys.why: Applying an ice pack to the scrotum could potentially worsen the situation by causing localized vasoconstriction and further reducing blood flow, which is already compromised in cases of testicular torsion.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
easy.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
standard.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

exam: Muscle Weakness — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
135.clinical_tip: Triple whammy: ACE-I + NSAID + diuretic → acute kidney injury. Check K⁺ and bicarbonate.
135.objective: ICU IV antihypertensives with electrolyte monitoring.

decoys.label: Dextroamphetamine (stimulant)
decoys.why: Stimulants like Dextroamphetamine can exacerbate hypertension, which is contra-indicated in a patient with Hypertensive Emergency.

decoys.label: Diuretics (Loop diuretic) as initial management
decoys.why: While diuretics may be used to manage hypertension, they are not the first-line treatment in a Hypertensive Emergency. Initial management should involve IV antihypertensive titration to reduce blood pressure quickly and safely.

decoys.label: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: The patient is already in a Hypertensive Emergency due to NSAID use, so it would be incorrect to continue or introduce more NSAIDs.

decoys.label: Calcium channel blockers for initial management
decoys.why: While calcium channel blockers may be used in the treatment of hypertension, they are not typically used as first-line therapy in a Hypertensive Emergency. Intravenous antihypertensive drugs are usually administered initially to rapidly lower blood pressure.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Triple whammy: ACE-I + NSAID + diuretic → acute kidney injury. Check K⁺ and bicarbonate.

136.title: Shortness of Breath
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
136.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
136.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Oxygen / BIPAP
interventions.why: Target SpO₂ per protocol; prepare intubation if tiring.

interventions.label: BNP · D-dimer · ABG
interventions.why: PE workup, CHF, CO₂ retention in COPD.

interventions.label: CT Pulmonary Angiography
interventions.why: If PE suspected and pre-test probability warrants.

interventions.label: ICU if unstable
interventions.why: BiPAP failure, shock, or rising work of breathing.

decoys.label: Ambien for sleep
decoys.why: Ambien is not indicated for shortness of breath or COPD exacerbation. It's a sedative and may worsen respiratory depression.

decoys.label: Ibuprofen for pain management
decoys.why: Ibuprofen is an NSAID and can worsen COPD exacerbation by increasing the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and masking symptoms like shortness of breath.

decoys.label: Furosemide for fluid overload
decoys.why: Furosemide is a diuretic, which may exacerbate hypovolemia in COPD patients by causing excessive loss of sodium and potassium. This could worsen respiratory function.

decoys.label: Metformin for diabetes control
decoys.why: While it's important to manage comorbidities like diabetes, metformin is not indicated in the acute management of COPD exacerbation and could potentially lead to lactic acidosis in a patient with compromised respiratory function.

137.title: Wrist and Ankle Pain
137.diagnosis: Traumatic Wrist or Ankle Sprain (due to acute onset of localized pain in the wrist or ankle, often associated with swelling and difficulty moving the affected joint)
exam: Wrist and Ankle Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
137.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
137.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Order X-rays (AP, lateral views) for both wrist and ankle to rule out fractures
Oxygen
interventions.why: Wrist and Ankle Pain could be due to various causes such as trauma, infection, or systemic conditions. The case does not provide clear symptoms of chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, palpitations, AMS, sepsis, or GI bleed, which would require an EKG or specific lab work. Therefore, the first step should be to ensure oxygen supply and IV access.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: This order ensures immediate stabilization of the patient by providing oxygen, setting up IV access, and checking glucose level.

decoys.label: Order painkillers as the primary treatment
decoys.why: Painkillers should not be the primary treatment for a traumatic wrist or ankle sprain. The first step is to diagnose and address any potential fractures, swelling, or instability in the joint.

decoys.label: Prescribe physical therapy as the initial intervention
decoys.why: Physical therapy should not be the initial intervention for a traumatic wrist or ankle sprain. The first step is to diagnose and address any potential fractures, swelling, or instability in the joint.

decoys.label: Refer the patient for surgery without proper diagnostic tests
decoys.why: Surgery should not be referred for a traumatic wrist or ankle sprain without first performing necessary diagnostic tests such as X-rays to rule out fractures and assess the extent of ligament damage.

decoys.label: Ignore the swelling and focus only on mobility exercises
decoys.why: Ignoring swelling in a traumatic wrist or ankle sprain can lead to complications such as increased pain, reduced range of motion, and even joint instability. It is important to address both swelling and mobility issues.

138.title: Fatigue
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
138.clinical_tip: Fatigue in ESRF — CXR, EKG, and ABG are red items; do not skip them.
138.objective: Evaluate uremia complications and dialytic indications (AEIOU).

decoys.label: Aggressive diuretic therapy
decoys.why: In end-stage renal failure, kidneys no longer function properly and are unable to excrete excess fluids. Therefore, diuretics should be used with caution as they may lead to electrolyte imbalances.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can further damage the kidneys in end-stage renal failure by reducing blood flow and causing sodium and water retention.

decoys.label: Oral Contrast for Chest X-ray
decoys.why: Oral contrast is typically used in abdominal and pelvic imaging, not chest radiography. For a PA and lateral chest X-ray, no oral contrast is required.

decoys.label: Hemodialysis for fluid overload
decoys.why: Although hemodialysis may be indicated for fluid overload in end-stage renal failure patients, it should not be the initial order for managing fatigue. Initial management typically involves addressing electrolyte imbalances and anemia, as well as correcting metabolic acidosis if present.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fatigue in ESRF — CXR, EKG, and ABG are red items; do not skip them.
easy.objective: Evaluate uremia complications and dialytic indications (AEIOU). Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

139.title: Lip Lesion
139.diagnosis: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lip
exam: Lip Lesion — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
139.clinical_tip: HEENT and skin exam before biopsy — sequencing matters for points.
139.objective: Biopsy non-healing lip lesion and stage with imaging.

decoys.label: Topical cream application for Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lip
decoys.why: Topical creams are not effective in treating invasive squamous cell carcinoma. Surgery, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy are typically required.

decoys.label: Prescribing an oral antibiotic as primary treatment
decoys.why: Oral antibiotics are not effective in treating Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lip. They may be used to manage secondary bacterial infections if present, but they do not treat the underlying cancer.

decoys.label: Irradiation as initial treatment before biopsy
decoys.why: Irradiation should typically be used after a definitive diagnosis of Squamous Cell Carcinoma. It is not appropriate to irradiate the area prior to obtaining a tissue sample for histological confirmation.

decoys.label: Referring the patient to an orthopedic surgeon for removal of the lip lesion
decoys.why: Orthopedic surgeons specialize in bones and joints, not soft tissue malignancies. A dermatologist or oral maxillofacial surgeon should be referred for excision and treatment of Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lip.

140.title: Headache
140.diagnosis: Primary Headache Disorders (such as Migraine, Tension-type headache) or Secondary Headaches (like Intracranial bleed or Brain tumor) should be considered
140.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
140.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
140.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Perform a detailed physical examination focusing on neurological assessment
Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count, Electrolytes, Glucose, and Coagulation profile
Order imaging studies: Non-contrast CT head if patient is acutely ill or has focal neurologic signs, MRI brain for further evaluation when CT is normal or indications suggest
Consider EEG if seizure activity suspected
If patient reports migraine-like symptoms (photophobia, phonophobia, nausea), consider prophylactic treatment with a non-narcotic analgesic or anti-emetic
Consult Neurology if diagnosis remains unclear, for further evaluation and management
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: To assess the patient's electrolytes, renal function, and liver function.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: To rule out a urinary tract infection or other potential causes of the headache.

decoys.label: Prescribe narcotic painkillers as the primary treatment for migraines
decoys.why: Narcotics should be avoided in the management of migraines due to their potential for abuse, rebound headaches, and masking underlying secondary causes.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan without considering patient's symptoms or physical examination
decoys.why: A CT scan should be ordered based on the presence of acute illness, focal neurologic signs, or when other diagnostic tests are inconclusive. Overuse can lead to unnecessary radiation exposure.

decoys.label: Prescribe an anticonvulsant as a first-line treatment for tension-type headaches
decoys.why: Anticonvulsants are generally reserved for the management of trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias, such as cluster headache, and not for tension-type headaches which can be managed with non-narcotic analgesics, muscle relaxants, and lifestyle modifications.

decoys.label: Order a lumbar puncture without indications or neurological examination
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture should only be performed when there is a suspicion of meningitis, encephalitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or intrathecal drug therapy monitoring. It carries risks such as headache, back pain, and post-lumbar puncture headache.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. 141.title: Rash
exam: Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
141.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
141.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including onset, location, distribution, and characteristics of the rash.
Perform physical examination with dermatological assessment.
Order laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), liver function tests (LFTs), renal function tests (RFTs), urinalysis, and blood cultures if fever is present.
Consider skin biopsy or other specialized tests such as direct immunofluorescence or PCR for specific etiologies based on clinical suspicion.

If the rash is associated with systemic symptoms like fever, malaise, or lymphadenopathy, consider empiric antibiotic therapy pending culture results (e.g., broad-spectrum penicillin or cephalosporin) but avoid administering antihistamines and steroids until the cause is determined.
Consult dermatology for further evaluation and management.
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This bundle includes the necessary lab tests to check for electrolytes, renal function, and liver function which can be useful in determining the underlying cause of a rash.

decoys.label: Administer antihistamines or steroids without determining the cause of the rash
decoys.why: Antihistamines and steroids should not be administered until the cause of the rash is determined to avoid masking potential symptoms, misdiagnosis, or complications

decoys.label: Perform a skin biopsy without considering other diagnostic tests
decoys.why: A skin biopsy should be considered along with other diagnostic tests such as direct immunofluorescence or PCR for specific etiologies based on clinical suspicion

decoys.label: Prescribe empiric antibiotic therapy without systemic symptoms
decoys.why: Empiric antibiotic therapy should be considered if the rash is associated with systemic symptoms like fever, malaise, or lymphadenopathy

decoys.label: Neglect laboratory tests in diagnosing undetermined rash
decoys.why: Laboratory tests such as CBC, LFTs, RFTs, urinalysis, and blood cultures are essential for the diagnosis of an undetermined rash

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

142.title: Seizures
exam: Seizures — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Neurologic exam focused on mental status and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
142.clinical_tip: Treat seizures immediately while awaiting amino acid results.
142.objective: NICU/PICU metabolic emergency with anticonvulsants and thiamine.

decoys.label: Dextrose infusion
decoys.why: Excessive dextrose can worsen Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD) due to increased production of branched-chain amino acids during catabolism.

decoys.label: High protein diet
decoys.why: A high protein diet is contraindicated in MSUD as it increases the levels of branched-chain amino acids which cannot be metabolized properly due to the disease.

decoys.label: Valproic acid
decoys.why: Valproic acid should not be used in MSUD as it can lead to an accumulation of toxic metabolites and worsen symptoms.

decoys.label: Ketogenic diet
decoys.why: A ketogenic diet may exacerbate the symptoms of Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD) by increasing the levels of branched-chain amino acids.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Treat seizures immediately while awaiting amino acid results.
easy.objective: NICU/PICU metabolic emergency with anticonvulsants and thiamine. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Treat seizures immediately while awaiting amino acid results.
standard.objective: NICU/PICU metabolic emergency with anticonvulsants and thiamine.

143.title: Poor Feeding
exam: Poor Feeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
143.clinical_tip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
143.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough history, including maternal and child health, growth and development, feeding habits, and family dynamics.
interventions.why: To gather information that may help narrow down the diagnosis of the underlying cause of poor feeding.

interventions.label: Perform physical examination focusing on growth parameters, developmental milestones, signs of malnutrition (e.g., edema, skin changes), and any underlying conditions.
interventions.why: To assess the patient's overall health status, identify any abnormalities that may contribute to poor feeding, and monitor progress during treatment.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen delivery for poor feeding is important to address any potential respiratory distress.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Two large-bore IV access will allow for quick administration of fluids and medications if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: A glucose check can help rule out hypoglycemia, which can cause poor feeding in infants.

decoys.label: Force-feeding the child
decoys.why: This approach can be harmful and stressful for the child, potentially exacerbating the issue of poor feeding. It should not be used as a first line of treatment.

decoys.label: Prescribing high calorie supplements without addressing underlying causes
decoys.why: While supplementation may help manage symptoms temporarily, it does not address the root cause of failure to thrive or malnutrition and may lead to further complications if not properly managed.

decoys.label: Starting the child on a liquid diet without consulting a pediatrician
decoys.why: Changing a child's diet requires professional advice from a healthcare provider, especially in cases of failure to thrive or malnutrition. A liquid diet may not meet all nutritional needs and can lead to long-term issues if not properly monitored.

decoys.label: Administering strong laxatives for constipation without assessing the cause
decoys.why: While constipation can be a symptom of failure to thrive or malnutrition, treating only the symptoms without addressing the underlying causes can lead to recurrent issues and potential complications.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
easy.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

144.title: Short Stature
144.diagnosis: Unknown Genetic or Endocrine Disorder causing Short Stature
exam: Short Stature — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
144.clinical_tip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
144.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

interventions.label: Blood tests: Complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function tests, IGF-1 level, and bone age X-ray
Oxygen
interventions.why: Initial support for a patient in distress

interventions.label: Order
interventions.why: Obtaining vital signs and glucose levels

decoys.label: Growth Hormone Stimulation Test
decoys.why: This test is not indicated for diagnosing unknown genetic or endocrine disorders causing short stature. It's used to evaluate growth hormone deficiency, which isn't the primary concern in this case.

decoys.label: Growth Hormone Replacement Therapy
decoys.why: Growth Hormone Replacement Therapy is not a diagnostic test. It's a treatment for growth hormone deficiency, which may be considered after a proper diagnosis has been made.

decoys.label: X-ray of the entire skeletal system
decoys.why: While X-rays can provide valuable information about bone health, an x-ray of the entire skeletal system is not typically performed to diagnose unknown genetic or endocrine disorders causing short stature. A bone age X-ray is more commonly used.

decoys.label: Consultation with a Cardiologist
decoys.why: Unless there are cardiac symptoms or concerns, a consultation with a cardiologist is not typically necessary for diagnosing unknown genetic or endocrine disorders causing short stature. A pediatric endocrinologist and geneticist would be more appropriate.

easy.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

145.title: Facial Pain
exam: Facial Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
145.clinical_tip: Standard analgesics fail — gabapentinoid or amitriptyline; fluorescein if V1 eye involved.

decoys.label: Ibuprofen
decoys.why: Ibuprofen is an NSAID and not typically used for neuropathic pain related to Trigeminal Postherpetic Neuropathy, Gabapentin or Pregabalin are preferred.

decoys.label: Morphine
decoys.why: Morphine is an opioid analgesic which may worsen the condition due to its potential to cause further nerve damage. Amitriptyline or Gabapentin/Pregabalin are more appropriate.

decoys.label: Corticosteroids (e.g., Prednisone)
decoys.why: While corticosteroids may be used for herpes zoster, they do not provide long-term benefit for Trigeminal Postherpetic Neuropathy and can lead to side effects such as osteoporosis or diabetes. Gabapentin or Pregabalin are preferred.

decoys.label: Lidocaine patches
decoys.why: Though Lidocaine patches may provide temporary relief, they do not address the underlying condition of Trigeminal Postherpetic Neuropathy. Amitriptyline, Gabapentin or Pregabalin should be considered.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Standard analgesics fail — gabapentinoid or amitriptyline; fluorescein if V1 eye involved.
easy.objective: Neuropathic pain control after shingles. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

146.title: Found Unconscious
146.diagnosis: Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) until proven otherwise
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
146.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and initiate basic life support if necessary.
interventions.why: To assess the patient's immediate condition and provide appropriate resuscitation.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including mechanism of injury, LOC, Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS), past medical history, medications, and allergies.
interventions.why: To understand the specifics of the trauma and any pre-existing conditions that may affect treatment.

interventions.label: Obtain physical examination focusing on neurological assessment.
interventions.why: To assess for signs of TBI or other neurological disorders.

interventions.label: Immediate imaging studies: Non-contrast head CT to assess for intracranial hemorrhage or other structural abnormalities.
interventions.why: To provide a visual assessment of the patient's brain and identify potential injuries.

interventions.label: Consult Neurosurgery if GCS is less than 15, signs of increased ICP, or if there are associated skull fractures.
interventions.why: For potential surgery to relieve pressure on the brain or manage any fractures.

interventions.label: Obtain labs such as complete blood count (CBC), coagulation profile, electrolytes, and glucose.
interventions.why: To evaluate for possible complications related to TBI, such as infection or bleeding.

interventions.label: Admit to an intensive care unit for close monitoring.
interventions.why: To provide constant monitoring of the patient's condition and quick response in case of deterioration.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is found unconscious, and immediate oxygen administration is necessary to ensure adequate ventilation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Immediate establishment of IV access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

decoys.label: Administer a strong sedative to make the patient more comfortable
decoys.why: Sedatives may depress the central nervous system further and worsen the brain injury. It is essential to maintain the patient's level of consciousness for neurological assessment.

decoys.label: Initiate physical therapy immediately
decoys.why: Physical therapy should be delayed until the acute phase of the traumatic brain injury has passed, and the patient's condition stabilizes. Initial focus should be on diagnostic workup, neurological assessment, and supporting vital functions.

decoys.label: Start the patient on high-dose pain medication without assessing their current pain level
decoys.why: Assessment of pain level is crucial before administering any pain medication. Over-sedation or inappropriate analgesia can impair neurological assessment and hinder diagnosis.

decoys.label: Discharge the patient home with instructions to rest and follow up with a primary care physician
decoys.why: A traumatic brain injury is a medical emergency that requires hospital admission for close monitoring, diagnostic tests, and potential surgery. Discharge before complete evaluation and stabilization could lead to potentially life-threatening complications.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes.

147.title: Heart Palpitations
147.diagnosis: Possible cardiac arrhythmia or other underlying heart condition
exam: Heart Palpitations — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
147.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
147.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order a 12-lead ECG to evaluate for any cardiac arrhythmias.
Refer the patient to a cardiologist for further evaluation and management.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing heart palpitations, which can potentially be life-threatening. Oxygen is crucial to provide immediate support for any respiratory distress.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Immediate IV access is essential for the administration of medications and fluids if necessary.

decoys.label: Ambien (Zolpidem) for sleep
decoys.why: Ambien should not be prescribed for patients with heart palpitations or suspected cardiac arrhythmias due to its potential for causing QT prolongation and increasing the risk of ventricular arrhythmias.

decoys.label: Metformin for diabetes management
decoys.why: The cause of heart palpitations is not definitively linked to diabetes, so metformin would not be an appropriate initial treatment for this symptom. A cardiac evaluation should be performed first.

decoys.label: Digoxin (digitalis) for heart failure management
decoys.why: While digitalis can help manage heart failure symptoms, it is not typically the first-line treatment for cardiac arrhythmias. A more appropriate initial step would be to evaluate the arrhythmia with a 12-lead ECG and refer the patient to a cardiologist.

decoys.label: Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic acid) for stroke prevention
decoys.why: While aspirin can help prevent strokes in certain high-risk populations, it is not typically the first treatment for heart palpitations. The underlying cause of the palpitations should be investigated with a 12-lead ECG and referral to a cardiologist.

148.title: Chest Pain
148.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
148.clinical_tip: Pleuritic pain days–weeks post-MI; pain better leaning forward. Avoid anticoag if large effusion.
148.objective: Confirm pericarditis; treat with aspirin/NSAID + colchicine.

decoys.label: Anticoagulants (e.g., Warfarin)
decoys.why: Anticoagulants are not indicated in Post-MI Pericarditis as they increase the risk of bleeding and do not help reduce the inflammation.

decoys.label: Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme (ACE) Inhibitors
decoys.why: ACE inhibitors are typically used in heart failure or hypertension, not in Post-MI Pericarditis as they can exacerbate pericarditis symptoms.

decoys.label: Noninvasive cardiac stress test (e.g., treadmill test)
decoys.why: A stress test is not indicated in Post-MI Pericarditis as it can trigger or worsen pericardial inflammation.

decoys.label: Beta-blockers
decoys.why: While beta-blockers may be used for some cases of acute coronary syndrome, they are generally contraindicated in Post-MI Pericarditis as they can exacerbate the inflammation.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

149.title: Chronic Diarrhea
149.diagnosis: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) or Infectious Diarrhea (based on the history and exclusion of other causes)
exam: Chronic Diarrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
149.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
149.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain stool sample for culture, Clostridium difficile toxin, ova and parasites, and C. difficile PCR
Consider fecal calprotectin level as an inflammatory marker
Imaging: abdominal CT or MRI may be considered if symptoms persist or worsen, but usually colonoscopy is preferred for further evaluation
Consult Gastroenterology for possible endoscopic evaluation (colonoscopy) and biopsy
Antibiotics should only be given based on culture results to avoid overuse and potential development of resistant organisms
order1
interventions.why: Upper GI Bleed, which is the closest match to this case, includes orders for oxygen, monitor, IV access ×2, pulse ox; IV fluids, type & cross, NPO; CBC, CMP, coags, CXR; however, it also includes an order for GI consult for urgent EGD, which is not typically necessary in the initial management of chronic diarrhea.

decoys.label: Prescribe corticosteroids without biopsy confirmation
decoys.why: Corticosteroids should not be prescribed for IBD or infectious diarrhea before a definitive diagnosis is made, usually through colonoscopy and biopsy.

decoys.label: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically
decoys.why: Broad-spectrum antibiotics should not be administered without culture results to avoid overuse and potential development of resistant organisms.

decoys.label: Prescribe antidiarrheal medications (e.g., loperamide) for IBD
decoys.why: Antidiarrheal medications may provide temporary relief but are not effective in treating the underlying cause of diarrhea in IBD and can worsen symptoms over time.

decoys.label: Order abdominal CT or MRI as a primary diagnostic tool
decoys.why: Abdominal imaging (CT or MRI) should be considered after initial evaluations if symptoms persist or worsen, but they are not the first line of diagnosis for chronic diarrhea. Colonoscopy is preferred for further evaluation.

150.title: Back Pain and Fatigue
exam: Back Pain and Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.

No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
150.clinical_tip: Order UPEP — UA dipstick misses Bence-Jones protein. Skeletal survey, NOT bone scan.
150.objective: CRAB features workup and hematology referral.

interventions.label: Office or ward
interventions.why: Bone pain, fatigue, weight loss in elderly.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs may provide temporary relief for back pain, but they do not address the underlying cause in multiple myeloma. Bisphosphonates are typically prescribed instead.

decoys.label: Corticosteroids
decoys.why: While corticosteroids can help manage symptoms like fatigue and pain, they do not address the underlying disease in multiple myeloma. Hematology/Oncology referral for chemotherapy is typically necessary.

decoys.label: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy
decoys.why: Multiple Myeloma involves an overproduction of abnormal antibodies, but immunoglobulin replacement therapy would not address this issue. The correct test is Serum protein electrophoresis.

decoys.label: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) every week
decoys.why: While MRI can be useful in diagnosing multiple myeloma and monitoring disease progression, frequent imaging is not necessary. A skeletal survey is typically done for initial staging, and follow-up imaging may be performed as needed.

151.title: Found Unconscious
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
151.clinical_tip: Pulse ox lies — order 100% O₂ before carboxyhemoglobin returns.
151.objective: ED oxygenation and CO level confirmation.

decoys.label: Administering a sedative
decoys.why: Sedatives are not needed initially for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning as they may worsen respiratory depression. The focus should be on providing oxygen and monitoring the patient's condition.

decoys.label: Administering an antidote such as sodium nitrite
decoys.why: Sodium nitrite is not typically used in the initial treatment of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning. It may be used later if hyperbaric oxygen therapy is not available and the patient is experiencing severe symptoms. However, its use requires careful monitoring due to its side effects.

decoys.label: Starting a glucose drip
decoys.why: Glucose drips are not indicated for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning. Hypoglycemia is unlikely in this context, and administering glucose could potentially mask signs of metabolic acidosis if present.

decoys.label: Performing a chest X-ray
decoys.why: A chest X-ray may not be necessary for the initial assessment of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning. The primary focus should be on providing oxygen, assessing vital signs, and checking carboxyhemoglobin levels. A chest X-ray might be ordered later if there are concerns about other conditions.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Pulse ox lies — order 100% O₂ before carboxyhemoglobin returns.
easy.objective: ED oxygenation and CO level confirmation. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

152.title: Headache
152.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
152.clinical_tip: Family with flu-like headaches in winter — think CO; 100% O₂ first.
152.objective: Same as Case 151 — high-flow O₂ and CO level.

interventions.label: 100% O₂ non-rebreather
interventions.why: Do not wait for lab to start treatment.

interventions.label: Cardiac monitor + pulse ox
interventions.why: Note: pulse ox unreliable in CO.

decoys.label: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)
decoys.why: Ineffective against carbon monoxide poisoning, used for pain relief not the underlying condition.

decoys.label: Nitroglycerin
decoys.why: Used for angina and hypertension, not effective in treating carbon monoxide poisoning.

decoys.label: Chlorpheniramine (allergy medication)
decoys.why: An antihistamine, it does not treat carbon monoxide poisoning.

decoys.label: Ampicillin
decoys.why: Antibiotic, ineffective against carbon monoxide poisoning which is a toxicity rather than an infection.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. 153.title: Skin Lesions
exam: Skin Lesions — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
153.clinical_tip: Sun-exposed blistering hands — screen Hep C and check urine porphyrins.
153.objective: Reduce iron load; sun and alcohol counseling.

decoys.label: Blood glucose test
decoys.why: Porphyria Cutanea Tarda is not directly related to diabetes, so a blood glucose test would not be clinically indicated.

decoys.label: CT scan of the liver
decoys.why: While Porphyria Cutanea Tarda can involve liver issues, a CT scan is usually not the first line of investigation. An ultrasound or liver function tests are more commonly used.

decoys.label: Biopsy of the skin lesion
decoys.why: A biopsy may be performed in some cases, but it's not always necessary for a diagnosis of Porphyria Cutanea Tarda as the condition is often diagnosed based on symptoms, family history, and lab results.

decoys.label: Prescription for antibiotics
decoys.why: Porphyria Cutanea Tarda is not typically treated with antibiotics. The main treatments are phlebotomy or hydroxychloroquine, sun protection, and lifestyle changes.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Sun-exposed blistering hands — screen Hep C and check urine porphyrins.
easy.objective: Reduce iron load; sun and alcohol counseling. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Sun-exposed blistering hands — screen Hep C and check urine porphyrins.
standard.objective: Reduce iron load; sun and alcohol counseling.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Reduce iron load; sun and alcohol counseling.

154.title: Vaginal Discharge
exam: Vaginal Discharge — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
154.clinical_tip: One STD diagnosed — screen all others and treat the partner.

decoys.label: Topical cream for Trichomoniasis
decoys.why: Trichomoniasis is a parasitic infection and topical creams are not effective against parasites, oral metronidazole or intravaginal gel metronidazole is the standard treatment.

decoys.label: Prescribe azithromycin instead of metronidazole
decoys.why: Azithromycin is not the first-line therapy for Trichomoniasis, oral metronidazole or intravaginal gel metronidazole is the standard treatment.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antibiotic without testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea
decoys.why: Chlamydia and gonorrhea are common co-infections with Trichomoniasis, it is important to test and treat them concurrently to prevent complications.

decoys.label: Not counsel the patient on treatment of sexual partners
decoys.why: Sexual partners should also be treated to prevent re-infection. It is essential for public health and to cure the infection completely.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: One STD diagnosed — screen all others and treat the partner.
easy.objective: Metronidazole plus full STI panel. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: One STD diagnosed — screen all others and treat the partner.
standard.objective: Metronidazole plus full STI panel.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Metronidazole plus full STI panel.

easy.objective: Share your symptoms clearly; ask what happens next.

155.title: Shortness of breath
155.diagnosis: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) or pulmonary edema as potential causes of shortness of breath in an undiagnosed patient
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
155.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
155.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs, including oxygen saturation and blood pressure
interventions.why: Essential for initial assessment of patient's condition

interventions.label: Perform chest X-ray to evaluate lung fields
interventions.why: Useful in identifying potential causes such as pneumonia, pleural effusion, or pulmonary edema

interventions.label: Order arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis for oxygenation status and acid-base balance
interventions.why: Critical for assessing the patient's respiratory and metabolic status

interventions.label: Administer supplemental oxygen as needed to maintain oxygen saturation above 90%
interventions.why: Important for preventing further deterioration in the patient's respiratory status

interventions.label: Initiate diuretics if signs of heart failure are present or pulmonary edema is suspected
interventions.why: Helps manage fluid overload and reduce symptoms associated with pulmonary edema

interventions.label: Consider obtaining echocardiogram to assess cardiac function
interventions.why: Helpful in ruling out cardiogenic causes of dyspnea

interventions.label: Consult Internal Medicine, Pulmonology, and Intensive Care Unit (ICU) as needed based on the patient's condition
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing shortness of breath, making oxygen a necessary intervention.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: In cases where the patient's condition may worsen rapidly or require emergency treatment, having large-bore IV access is crucial.

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are not indicated for shortness of breath caused by ARDS or pulmonary edema. They are primarily used for allergic reactions.

decoys.label: Cough suppressants
decoys.why: Cough suppressants may be contraindicated in patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) or pulmonary edema as they can suppress a productive cough and potentially worsen secretion retention.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs may exacerbate pulmonary edema by increasing the risk of fluid overload, and they are generally contraindicated in acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to their potential to worsen pulmonary capillary leakage.

decoys.label: Inhaled bronchodilators
decoys.why: While inhaled bronchodilators may help with symptoms of asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, they are unlikely to be effective in the treatment of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) or pulmonary edema. They primarily target airway obstruction rather than the underlying conditions causing shortness of breath.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
standard.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

easy.vitalsText: The team is checking my vital signs now.
easy.clinicalTip: You feel unwell and want help — the clinical team will guide next steps.
easy.objective: Share your symptoms clearly; ask what happens next.

standard.clinicalTip: Focus on what you feel, when it started, and what makes it worse or better.
standard.objective: Participate in shared decision-making as the team stabilizes you.

hard.clinicalTip: Limited guidance — advocate for yourself if symptoms worsen.
hard.objective: Navigate uncertainty while the team works through the differential.

156.title: Generalized Swelling
156.diagnosis: Lymphedema or Edema secondary to an underlying condition (e.g., heart failure, kidney disease, liver disease)
exam: Generalized Swelling — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
156.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
156.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Perform comprehensive physical exam including vital signs, weight, and lymph node evaluation
Order laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), urinalysis, serum electrolytes, lipid profile, liver function tests, renal function tests, thyroid function tests
Order imaging studies if needed: Chest X-ray, echocardiogram, ultrasound, MRI/CT scan
Consult Internal Medicine or Nephrology based on lab results and clinical presentation
Prescribe diuretics if heart failure suspected (if patient is stable enough) or refer for specialist management
Refer to physical therapy for lymphatic drainage if diagnosis of Lymphedema is confirmed
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing generalized swelling which could indicate a life-threatening condition. Immediate oxygen administration and establishing IV access are critical to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids quickly and potentially lifesaving medications.

decoys.label: Oxygen
decoys.why: Generalized swelling/lymphedema does not typically require oxygen therapy as it is not a respiratory issue.

decoys.label: Antibiotics
decoys.why: Lymphedema or edema secondary to an underlying condition is not usually treated with antibiotics unless there's an infection present.

decoys.label: NSAIDs (Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs)
decoys.why: While NSAIDs might alleviate symptoms, they do not address the underlying condition causing lymphedema or edema. Additionally, their long-term use could potentially worsen heart failure if it's the underlying cause.

decoys.label: Calcium channel blockers
decoys.why: Calcium channel blockers are used for hypertension and angina, not lymphedema or edema. The underlying condition causing the swelling would need to be addressed first.

157.title: Frequency of Urination
exam: Frequency of Urination — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
157.clinical_tip: At diagnosis order eye referral and foot exam — automatic CCS points.
157.objective: Confirm hyperglycemia and start metformin with screening bundle.

decoys.label: Increase salt intake
decoys.why: Excessive salt intake can exacerbate hypertension, a common comorbidity in diabetes mellitus type II. However, it does not directly affect the frequency of urination.

decoys.label: Prescribe insulin immediately
decoys.why: While insulin may be necessary for some patients with uncontrolled blood sugar levels, metformin is usually the first-line medication for type II diabetes mellitus due to its lower risk of hypoglycemia and weight gain.

decoys.label: Advise to stop exercising
decoys.why: Regular exercise is crucial for managing diabetes mellitus type II as it helps improve insulin sensitivity, control blood sugar levels, and promote overall health.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antibiotic for increased urination
decoys.why: Increased frequency of urination in diabetes mellitus type II is typically due to polyuria (excessive production of urine) rather than a bacterial infection. Antibiotics are not indicated unless there's evidence of a urinary tract infection.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: At diagnosis order eye referral and foot exam — automatic CCS points.
easy.objective: Confirm hyperglycemia and start metformin with screening bundle. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: At diagnosis order eye referral and foot exam — automatic CCS points.
standard.objective: Confirm hyperglycemia and start metformin with screening bundle.

hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Confirm hyperglycemia and start metformin with screening bundle.

158.title: Knee Pain
158.diagnosis: Proximal Tibiofibular Joint Sprain, Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome, or Meniscal Tear (common causes of knee pain)
exam: Knee Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
158.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
158.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history and perform a physical examination
interventions.why: This is the first step in diagnosing knee pain. It allows for a comprehensive understanding of the patient's symptoms, their onset, and any aggravating or alleviating factors.

interventions.label: Order X-rays to rule out fractures or other bony abnormalities
interventions.why: X-rays can help identify fractures, osteochondritis dissecans, avascular necrosis, or other bone disorders that may cause knee pain.

interventions.label: Consider ordering an MRI if symptoms persist or worsen, or in cases of severe trauma
interventions.why: MRI is useful for diagnosing soft tissue injuries such as ligament sprains, meniscal tears, and cartilage damage.

interventions.label: If the diagnosis is still uncertain, consider a knee aspiration and synovial fluid analysis
interventions.why: This can help identify inflammatory conditions or infections that may be causing the knee pain.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This bundle includes electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests that can help in the diagnosis of various conditions causing knee pain. It also includes a urinalysis (UA) to check for any potential infection or urinary issues.

decoys.label: Prescribe immediate weightlifting sessions for knee pain relief
decoys.why: Weightlifting can exacerbate existing knee injuries and should be avoided during the recovery period.

decoys.label: Administer a steroid injection without diagnostic confirmation
decoys.why: Steroid injections are typically used as a treatment after a definitive diagnosis has been made to reduce inflammation. Without a proper diagnosis, the injection may not be effective or could potentially cause harm.

decoys.label: Prescribe non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for long-term knee pain management
decoys.why: While NSAIDs can help manage symptoms, they should not be used as a long-term solution for knee pain. Chronic use of these medications may lead to side effects such as stomach ulcers and kidney damage.

decoys.label: Refer the patient for immediate arthroscopic surgery without proper diagnosis or conservative treatment trials
decoys.why: Arthroscopic surgery is typically a last resort after other conservative treatments have failed. A proper diagnostic process and attempts at non-invasive treatments should be exhausted before considering surgery.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
standard.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

159.title: Abnormal Behavior
159.diagnosis: Psychiatric Emergency (Acute Behavioral Disturbance or Psychosis)
exam: Abnormal Behavior — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
159.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
159.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a comprehensive psychiatric evaluation
interventions.why: To establish a baseline and evaluate the patient's mental status, behavior, thought process, mood, and suicidal ideations.

interventions.label: Order laboratory studies to rule out medical causes (CBC, Chem-7, Urine Drug Screen, etc.)
Start observation and management in a safe environment
interventions.why: To ensure patient safety and prevent self-harm or harm to others.

interventions.label: Consult Psychiatry for further evaluation and treatment
interventions.why: For comprehensive psychiatric assessment, diagnosis, and treatment planning.

interventions.label: If necessary, administer haloperidol or other antipsychotics for agitation control under the guidance of a psychiatrist
interventions.why: To manage acute agitation and psychosis if needed, but always under a psychiatrist's supervision due to potential side effects.

interventions.label: order1
interventions.why: The order is consistent with the clinical decision support (CDS) guidance for this condition.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is presenting with abnormal behavior, which could be a sign of various life-threatening conditions. Immediate oxygen administration and establishing IV access are critical interventions to ensure the patient's stability while further evaluation is ongoing.

decoys.label: Oxygen, IV access x2
decoys.why: Psychiatric emergencies do not typically require oxygen therapy or intravenous access, unless there is a concurrent medical condition. In this case, the focus should be on psychiatric evaluation and management.

decoys.label: Antidepressants
decoys.why: In acute behavioral disturbance or psychosis, antidepressants may not be immediately appropriate as they take time to build up in the system and can worsen symptoms during initial stages. Antipsychotics are typically used for managing agitation.

decoys.label: Neurology consultation
decoys.why: While psychiatric emergencies may present with symptoms that could be confused with neurological disorders, a neurology consultation would not be the primary focus in this case. The priority is to address the behavioral disturbance or psychosis.

decoys.label: Physical therapy
decoys.why: Physical therapy is generally not indicated for psychiatric emergencies as they primarily deal with treating physical impairments or disabilities, not mental health conditions.
