160.title: Anxiety
160.diagnosis: Psychiatric Disorder, possibly Anxiety Disorder (Given the chief complaint of anxiety with an unknown diagnosis)
exam: Anxiety — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
160.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
160.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a psychiatric consultation
interventions.why: For a proper evaluation and management of the patient's psychiatric condition

interventions.label: Order comprehensive psychiatric evaluation including mental status examination
interventions.why: To assess the patient's current mental state, cognitive function, mood, and behavior

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Anxiety can lead to respiratory distress, necessitating oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for the administration of medications and fluids in emergency situations.

interventions.label: universal labs + imaging
interventions.why: Universal lab tests such as CBC, CMP, UA are essential for a general assessment of the patient's health status. Imaging like chest X-ray can help determine underlying conditions.

decoys.label: Order a blood test to diagnose anxiety
decoys.why: Anxiety is not a physical condition that can be diagnosed with a blood test.

decoys.label: Prescribe anxiolytics without a proper psychiatric evaluation
decoys.why: It's important to conduct a thorough psychiatric evaluation before prescribing any medication, especially for anxiety disorders.

decoys.label: Refer the patient to a cardiologist for an EKG due to anxiety
decoys.why: Anxiety does not typically require cardiac evaluation unless there are accompanying symptoms suggesting a cardiovascular disorder.

decoys.label: Write a prescription for exercise as the sole treatment for anxiety
decoys.why: While exercise is beneficial for mental health, it is not sufficient as a standalone treatment for an anxiety disorder. Psychiatric consultation and medications may also be required.

161.title: Headache
161.diagnosis: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage, Migraine, Tension Headache (considering unknown diagnosis), Brain Tumor, Intracranial Infection
161.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
161.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
161.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain urgent CT head with contrast
interventions.why: To rule out conditions such as subarachnoid hemorrhage, brain tumor, and intracranial infection that may require emergent intervention

interventions.label: Order lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis if CT is negative or inconclusive
interventions.why: To further evaluate the cause of headache if CT results are unclear or negative

interventions.label: Evaluate for secondary causes of headache (e.g., hypertension, diabetes mellitus, thyroid disorder)
Consult Neurology and Neurosurgery as necessary
interventions.why: For specialized evaluation and management of potential neurological conditions

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing a headache, which could be a sign of various conditions such as meningitis, hypertensive emergency, or seizure. Providing oxygen immediately helps to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Ensuring IV access with large-bore catheters is crucial for prompt administration of fluids and medications in case of an emergency or if the patient's condition worsens.

decoys.label: Administer over-the-counter pain relievers for headache management
decoys.why: Inappropriate for potential life-threatening conditions such as Subarachnoid Hemorrhage, Brain Tumor, or Intracranial Infection

decoys.label: Prescribe ergotamine tartrate for Migraine treatment
decoys.why: Ergotamine tartrate is contraindicated in patients with Subarachnoid Hemorrhage due to the risk of causing vasoconstriction and increased intracranial pressure

decoys.label: Order CT head without contrast
decoys.why: Contrast is necessary for better visualization of potential bleeding in Subarachnoid Hemorrhage or Brain Tumor, and for improved detection of certain types of infections

decoys.label: Prescribe muscle relaxants for Tension Headache management
decoys.why: Muscle relaxants are not the primary treatment for Tension Headaches. They are more appropriate for conditions involving muscle spasm, such as cervicogenic headaches

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: I came in today because  I states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. 162.title: Pain During Urination
162.diagnosis: Acute Cystitis or Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) is a common consideration for pain during urination with unknown diagnosis.
exam: Pain During Urination — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
162.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
162.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Urinalysis, Urine Culture and Sensitivity
Imaging studies: Ultrasound of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder (US KUB) if patient's condition warrants imaging
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: To assess renal and liver function and screen for potential infections.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: To check for hematuria, proteinuria or signs of a urinary tract infection.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without urine culture and sensitivity
decoys.why: Antibiotic selection should be based on the results of a urine culture and sensitivity test to ensure the most effective and least resistant treatment.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) as first-line treatment
decoys.why: NSAIDs are not typically recommended for acute cystitis or UTI, as they may exacerbate inflammation in the urinary tract. Antibiotics are usually required.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antifungal medication
decoys.why: Fungal infections do not typically cause symptoms such as pain during urination. A urinalysis and culture should be performed to identify the causative bacterial pathogen before prescribing appropriate antibiotics.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan without a clear indication
decoys.why: CT scans expose the patient to ionizing radiation and are generally not necessary for uncomplicated acute cystitis or UTI. Imaging studies should be ordered only when there is suspicion of complications or abnormalities, such as kidney stones or pyelonephritis.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
easy.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
standard.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

163.title: Ear Pain
exam: Ear Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
163.clinical_tip: Office or home — placing simple AOM in the ED loses points.

interventions.label: Office or home (not ED)
interventions.why: Stable pediatric ear pain — wrong location deducts points.

decoys.label: Antibiotic ear drops
decoys.why: Antibiotic ear drops are generally not the first line of treatment for Acute Otitis Media, especially in children. Oral antibiotics are typically prescribed instead.

decoys.label: Injectable steroids
decoys.why: In acute otitis media, injectable steroids are usually not indicated as the primary treatment. They may be used for severe or recurrent cases, but oral antibiotics remain the standard initial treatment.

decoys.label: Decongestants
decoys.why: Decongestants do not treat bacterial infections like Acute Otitis Media. They are used for nasal congestion due to allergies or colds, which are different conditions.

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are primarily used for treating allergic reactions and not bacterial infections such as Acute Otitis Media. They do not have a direct antibacterial effect.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Office or home — placing simple AOM in the ED loses points.
easy.objective: Pneumatic otoscopy diagnosis and amoxicillin. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Office or home — placing simple AOM in the ED loses points.
standard.objective: Pneumatic otoscopy diagnosis and amoxicillin.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Pneumatic otoscopy diagnosis and amoxicillin.

164.title: Pelvic Pain
164.diagnosis: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) or other gynecologic causes, appendicitis, ovarian torsion, or other abdominal pathologies are possibilities given pelvic pain of unknown cause in a reproductive-aged female. However, it's important to note that the final diagnosis may require further evaluation.
164.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: complaining of pelvic pain for the last month.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
164.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
164.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), C-reactive protein (CRP), urinalysis, and urine culture
Imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound or pelvic CT scan

decoys.label: X-ray of the chest
decoys.why: While an X-ray of the chest can help diagnose lung conditions, it does not provide information about pelvic pathologies and is thus not relevant to this context.

decoys.label: Blood type and Rh factor test
decoys.why: Blood type and Rh factor testing are typically performed during pregnancy or prior to blood transfusions, they do not directly aid in the diagnosis of pelvic pain or any of the potential causes mentioned.

decoys.label: EKG (Electrocardiogram)
decoys.why: An EKG is used to diagnose and monitor heart conditions, it does not provide information about gynecologic or abdominal pathologies that could cause pelvic pain in a reproductive-aged female.

decoys.label: Lumbar puncture
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture, also known as a spinal tap, is used to collect cerebrospinal fluid for examination and does not provide information about pelvic or abdominal pathologies that could cause pelvic pain in a reproductive-aged female.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Pelvic pain History of present Illness: This is a 20-year-old previously healthy female presenting to your office complaining of pelvic pain for the last month. She rates the pain as a constant 7/10 sharp pain which worsens when she moves. She was encouraged by her boyfriend to come to you because she is having extreme pain during sex. Ibuprofen "takes the edge off." The pain is severe enough that she reports having difficulty sleeping. Her pain worsens during her menstruation and that the pain is "unbearable." She admits that she has had unprotected sex with her boyfriend a few times, but reports that she is taking an oral contraceptive. She denies multiple sexual partners in the past. She has had a few episodes of vomiting and still feels nauseous. No fevers or chills. She also reports pain when having a bowel movement. She denies any vaginal discharge. Past Medical History: Current medications: Oral Contraceptive Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She has never been to a gynecologist. Family History: The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer in her 50s. Her father had a 'heart attack' in his 40s. Marital/Family: The patient is the oldest of 3 children. Both of her siblings are healthy without medical problems. Personal habits: She admits to smoking 2 cigarettes a day to help lose weight. She also admits to smoking marijuana occasionally at parties. She also drinks alcohol once a week with friends. Occupation/educational: She is a college student and currently studying Art History and would like to be a teacher. Recreational: She enjoys to paint and train dogs. 165.title: Postcoital Bleeding
165.diagnosis: Unspecified Abnormal Uterine Bleeding in sexually active females, or Coagulopathy in case of history of bleeding disorder
exam: Postcoital Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
165.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
165.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Obtain medical history including menstrual cycle, sexual activity, contraceptive use, and any known bleeding disorders
Complete a pelvic examination and ultrasound to assess the uterus and adnexa
Coagulation panel for patients with a history of bleeding disorder or if abnormal coagulation cannot be ruled out clinically
Transvaginal ultrasound may be necessary in cases where pelvic examination is inconclusive
Consult Obstetrics and Gynecology if uterine pathology suspected, and Hematology if coagulopathy suspected
pregnancy test
interventions.why: In women of childbearing age, it is essential to order a pregnancy test as postcoital bleeding can be a symptom of ectopic pregnancy.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without considering the cause of postcoital bleeding
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not appropriate for postcoital bleeding unless a pelvic infection is suspected, which requires further diagnostic tests.

decoys.label: Prescribe hormonal contraceptives to manage postcoital bleeding as the primary treatment
decoys.why: Hormonal contraceptives should not be used as the primary treatment for postcoital bleeding. They are more appropriate for regulating menstrual cycle irregularities or managing heavy menstrual bleeding, but a diagnostic workup is needed first.

decoys.label: Order CT scan instead of pelvic examination and ultrasound
decoys.why: A CT scan is not the first-line imaging modality for postcoital bleeding. Pelvic examination and ultrasound are more appropriate for assessing the uterus and adnexa.

decoys.label: Refer the patient to Cardiology without considering coagulopathy
decoys.why: Cardiology specialists are not typically involved in managing postcoital bleeding or coagulopathies. Hematology should be consulted instead if coagulopathy is suspected.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.

interventions.label: ED or ward
interventions.why: Oral bleeding or hemarthrosis in male child.

decoys.label: Blood transfusion
decoys.why: Blood transfusions are not typically used as a first-line treatment for hemophilia A. Instead, factor VIII replacement therapy is used.

decoys.label: Platelet transfusion
decoys.why: While platelets may help with clotting in some bleeding disorders, they are not effective in treating hemophilia A, which is a deficiency of clotting factor VIII.

decoys.label: Vitamin K
decoys.why: Vitamin K does not correct the underlying defect in hemophilia A. It may help with other bleeding disorders where vitamin K-dependent clotting factors are deficient, but not in hemophilia A.

decoys.label: Warfarin (Coumadin)
decoys.why: Warfarin is an anticoagulant and should be avoided in patients with hemophilia A as it may exacerbate bleeding due to the already impaired clotting ability.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Avoid NSAIDs and IM injections — replace factor VIII.
easy.objective: Stop bleeding with factor replacement after confirming deficiency. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

167.title: Nausea and Vomiting
exam: Nausea and Vomiting — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.

167.clinical_tip: Gastric emptying study is gold standard; EGD if acute obstruction concern.
167.objective: Prokinetic therapy after confirming delayed emptying.

decoys.label: Insulin
decoys.why: Insulin is not a treatment for gastroparesis, as it can exacerbate the condition by further slowing gastric emptying.

decoys.label: Digitalis
decoys.why: Digitalis is not indicated for nausea and vomiting in diabetic gastroparesis, as it can cause abnormal heart rhythms which may exacerbate comorbidities.

decoys.label: Proton pump inhibitor (PPI)
decoys.why: While PPIs can help with symptoms of gastroparesis, they are not the primary treatment and their overuse can lead to drug-induced gastritis or other complications.

decoys.label: Ipratropium bromide
decoys.why: Ipratropium bromide is used for bronchospasm, not nausea and vomiting. It has no role in the management of diabetic gastroparesis.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Gastric emptying study is gold standard; EGD if acute obstruction concern.

168.title: Fatigue
168.diagnosis: Anemia or other metabolic disorder (considering fatigue as a chief complaint with unknown diagnosis)
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
168.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
168.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Complete blood count (CBC) including hemoglobin, hematocrit, and mean corpuscular volume (MCV)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia as a potential cause of fatigue. MCV is particularly useful in determining the type of anemia.

interventions.label: Metabolic panel including glucose, electrolytes, liver function tests, and creatinine
interventions.why: To assess for metabolic abnormalities that may contribute to symptoms of fatigue.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with fatigue, which can be a symptom of various conditions such as anemia, infection, or metabolic disorders. However, fatigue is also a common symptom that may indicate respiratory distress, making oxygen administration necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and monitoring the patient's vital signs.

decoys.label: Iron supplements
decoys.why: Iron supplements are not appropriate without a confirmed diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia.

decoys.label: Vitamin B12 and folic acid injections
decoys.why: While these vitamins are important, their deficiencies do not typically cause fatigue as the primary symptom. Furthermore, injectable forms are usually reserved for severe or untreatable deficiencies.

decoys.label: Adenosine stress test
decoys.why: An adenosine stress test is used to evaluate cardiac conditions, not metabolic disorders or anemia, which are more likely causes of fatigue as the chief complaint.

decoys.label: Thyroid hormone replacement therapy
decoys.why: Thyroid hormone replacement therapy is typically used to treat hypothyroidism, not anemia or other metabolic disorders. Symptoms of hypothyroidism may include weight gain and cold intolerance, whereas fatigue can be a symptom of many conditions.

169.title: Somnolence
exam: Somnolence — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
169.clinical_tip: Aggressive NS hydration before bisphosphonate; no loop diuretics until replete.
169.objective: Volume resuscitation then zoledronic acid in ED.

decoys.label: Lithium prescription
decoys.why: Lithium is not used for treating hypercalcemia. It's often used for the treatment of bipolar disorder and other psychiatric conditions.

decoys.label: Parathyroidectomy
decoys.why: While parathyroid glands can contribute to hypercalcemia, in secondary malignancy-induced hypercalcemia, the primary cause is bone resorption by tumors, not overproduction of PTH. Therefore, parathyroidectomy is typically not indicated.

decoys.label: Calcium supplement prescription
decoys.why: Calcium supplements should generally be avoided in patients with hypercalcemia as it can exacerbate the condition. Instead, hydration and medication to inhibit bone resorption are usually prescribed.

decoys.label: Aggressive phosphate therapy
decoys.why: Aggressively administering phosphates can potentially lead to hypocalcemia due to the formation of calcium-phosphate complexes. In hypercalcemic patients, the focus is on increasing urinary excretion of calcium rather than decreasing serum levels directly.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Aggressive NS hydration before bisphosphonate; no loop diuretics until replete.
easy.objective: Volume resuscitation then zoledronic acid in ED. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Volume resuscitation then zoledronic acid in ED.

exam: Fall — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Neurologic exam focused on mental status and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
170.clinical_tip: Start in office/home — unnecessary ED transfer loses points unless unstable.
170.objective: Orthostatic vitals and review offending antihypertensives/diuretics.

interventions.label: Office or home (not ED)
interventions.why: Stable lightheadedness on standing — location trap.

interventions.label: IV fluid bolus if acutely symptomatic

decoys.label: Administer a strong vasodilator such as nitroglycerin
decoys.why: Orthostatic hypotension is caused by decreased blood volume and vasoconstriction, not increased vascular resistance. Nitroglycerin would further lower blood pressure, exacerbating symptoms.

decoys.label: Prescribe a thiazide diuretic for fluid retention
decoys.why: Orthostatic hypotension is often due to overuse of diuretics or antihypertensives. Adding more diuretics would worsen the condition by causing further dehydration and hypotension.

decoys.label: Initiate a course of calcium channel blockers
decoys.why: Calcium channel blockers are used to treat hypertension, not orthostatic hypotension. They can worsen symptoms by causing further vasodilation and decreasing blood pressure.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the brain
decoys.why: Orthostatic hypotension is related to autonomic dysfunction, not neurological issues. A CT scan would not provide relevant information for diagnosing or treating orthostatic hypotension.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Start in office/home — unnecessary ED transfer loses points unless unstable.
easy.objective: Orthostatic vitals and review offending antihypertensives/diuretics. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs, perform a detailed physical examination, and assess mental status
interventions.why: To gather initial data on the patient's condition

interventions.label: Order laboratory workup: CBC, electrolytes, glucose, renal/hepatic function tests, thyroid function tests, urine toxicology screen
Order imaging studies if necessary (CT scan or MRI of the brain)
Consult psychiatry for further evaluation and management
If the patient is a danger to themselves or others, consider involuntary hospitalization under a psychiatric hold
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: To evaluate electrolytes, renal and liver function which could contribute to the patient's agitation

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: To rule out urinary tract infection or other kidney-related issues

decoys.label: Administer antipsychotic medication without a thorough evaluation and proper diagnosis
decoys.why: Antipsychotics should not be administered without a thorough evaluation to determine the cause of agitation. Overuse or improper use can lead to serious side effects.

decoys.label: Order an EEG for initial assessment of agitation
decoys.why: An EEG is not typically the first line test in assessing agitation, which is more commonly associated with neuropsychiatric disorders. A detailed physical examination and mental status assessment are usually performed first.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan or MRI of the chest
decoys.why: CT scan or MRI are not typically used to diagnose neuropsychiatric disorders such as delirium, dementia, or psychiatric emergencies. Brain imaging is only ordered if necessary based on symptoms and clinical findings.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan or MRI without informed consent
decoys.why: Imaging studies require informed consent from the patient or their legal representative. Failure to obtain proper consent can lead to ethical and legal issues.

exam: Hanging — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.

No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
172.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and perform a complete physical examination
Laboratory studies: CBC, electrolytes, glucose, BUN, creatinine, liver function tests, urinalysis, toxicology screen
Imaging studies: CT head to rule out traumatic brain injury or other complications
Psychiatric consult for evaluation and management of suicidal ideation
Nursing assessment for suicide risk and psycho-social support
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing a hanging incident, which could potentially lead to severe distress or acute metabolic stress.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The patient may require further resuscitative measures, such as fluids and medications.

interventions.label: Monitor
interventions.why: Continuous monitoring of vital signs is crucial for assessing the patient's condition.

interventions.label: Pulse ox
interventions.why: Oxygen saturation levels are essential to evaluate the effectiveness of oxygen administration.

decoys.label: Narcotic pain relievers
decoys.why: Can potentially exacerbate suicidal ideation or be used in a deliberate overdose

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for pain management
decoys.why: Can lead to gastrointestinal bleeding which may worsen if the patient has attempted suicide by ingesting harmful substances

decoys.label: Physical restraints
decoys.why: Physical restraint should be avoided due to potential for increased agitation and self-harm in a suicidal individual

decoys.label: Discharge against medical advice
decoys.why: Early discharge can increase the risk of suicide, as the patient may not have received adequate evaluation, treatment, or follow-up care for their suicidal ideation and underlying mental health issues

173.title: Sexual Assault
exam: Sexual Assault — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
173.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
173.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including description of the assault, any injuries, and risk for STIs or pregnancy.
Perform a comprehensive physical exam focusing on genitourinary and rectal examination.
Collect forensic evidence (rape kit) if appropriate and with patient's consent.
Order laboratory tests: hepatitis B and C serology, HIV test, RPR for syphilis, gonorrhea and chlamydia cultures/nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT).
Consult Sexual Assault Forensic Examiner (SAFE) team or Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner (SANE) for comprehensive care and support.
Provide psychosocial support, including referral to mental health services.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is a sexual assault victim, and it's important to provide immediate oxygen to maintain oxygenation. Also, the patient might be in distress or have symptoms of AMS.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: It is essential to establish two large-bore IV access lines for fluid resuscitation and potential medication administration.

decoys.label: Administer narcotic pain relievers
decoys.why: Sexual assault patients may not require pain relief due to the nature of the injury. If necessary, non-narcotic pain medication should be considered.

decoys.label: Perform a pelvic X-ray
decoys.why: Pelvic X-rays are generally not used in the diagnosis of suspected sexual assault. Physical examination and forensic evidence collection are more relevant.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without waiting for culture results
decoys.why: Antibiotic prescription should be based on laboratory test results to ensure appropriate treatment and prevent the development of antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Administer a sedative or tranquilizer to the patient without medical necessity
decoys.why: Sedatives or tranquilizers should only be administered if medically necessary and with careful consideration of the potential risks and benefits.

174.title: Hematemesis
exam: Hematemesis — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
174.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
174.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: CBC, Coagulation panel, Lipase, AST, ALT
Order upper GI endoscopy
interventions.why: As it is the gold standard diagnostic test for upper gastrointestinal bleeding

interventions.label: Administer intravenous fluids and antacids to prevent further bleeding and alkalinize gastric pH
Consult Gastroenterology for endoscopic evaluation and management

decoys.label: Administer over-the-counter antacids
decoys.why: Over-the-counter antacids may provide temporary relief, but they do not address the underlying cause of gastrointestinal bleeding and could interfere with diagnostic tests or medical management.

decoys.label: Prescribe painkillers for abdominal discomfort
decoys.why: Painkillers are not effective in treating the cause of upper gastrointestinal bleeding and may mask symptoms that are vital for diagnosis. Also, they could interact with other medications.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan instead of an upper GI endoscopy
decoys.why: A CT scan may not identify the source of upper gastrointestinal bleeding effectively and requires contrast agents that can worsen kidney function in patients with pre-existing conditions. An upper GI endoscopy is the preferred diagnostic test.

decoys.label: Discharge the patient without further evaluation
decoys.why: Hematemesis (vomiting of blood) is a serious symptom and requires prompt medical attention. Discharging a patient without proper evaluation, diagnosis, and management could lead to severe complications or even death.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
easy.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

175.title: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding
175.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for heavy menstrual bleeding include endometrial hyperplasia, fibroids, adenomyosis, and malignancies such as endometrial cancer. However, since the diagnosis is unknown, an initial comprehensive workup is necessary.
exam: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
175.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
175.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), coagulation panel, thyroid function tests, and hormone levels
Imaging studies: Transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI

decoys.label: Folate test
decoys.why: While folate deficiency can cause anemia, it is not typically associated with heavy menstrual bleeding directly. The primary focus should be on investigating the cause of the bleeding itself.

decoys.label: Urinalysis
decoys.why: Urinalysis is not relevant to heavy menstrual bleeding as it primarily assesses kidney function and urinary tract infections, not reproductive system issues.

decoys.label: Mammography
decoys.why: Mammography is used for breast cancer screening, not for diagnosing endometrial or reproductive issues that cause heavy menstrual bleeding.

decoys.label: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
decoys.why: A CT scan might be useful in certain cases, but it exposes the patient to a higher level of radiation compared to MRI and transvaginal ultrasound. Transvaginal ultrasound and pelvic MRI are more suitable for initial evaluation due to their lower radiation exposure and better resolution of reproductive structures.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
standard.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

176.title: Animal Bite
exam: Animal Bite — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
176.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
176.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Stabilize the patient: ensure airway, breathing, and circulatory status; administer tetanus prophylaxis if needed
Obtain a complete blood count (CBC) and serum glucose level
Culture and debridement of the wound if appropriate
Consult Infectious Disease specialist for potential rabies prophylaxis
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide supplemental oxygen for the patient suffering from an animal bite, which can cause distress or airway obstruction.

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: For fluid and medication administration in case of severe infection or complications arising from the animal bite.

decoys.label: Administer rabies prophylaxis immediately upon arrival
decoys.why: Rabies prophylaxis should be administered after a proper risk assessment, which includes determining the type of animal, its vaccination status, and whether it's possible to test the animal for rabies. This assessment should ideally be done before the patient arrives.

decoys.label: Initiate antibiotic therapy without culture and sensitivity testing
decoys.why: Antibiotic therapy should be initiated after obtaining a culture from the wound site, as this allows for identifying the specific pathogen responsible for the infection and selecting appropriate antibiotics. Broad-spectrum antibiotics may not cover all possible pathogens.

decoys.label: Ignore tetanus status of the patient
decoys.why: Tetanus status should always be considered, as a deep puncture wound from an animal bite increases the risk for tetanus. Patients should have their tetanus status assessed and receive appropriate prophylaxis if needed.

decoys.label: Delay consultation with Infectious Disease specialist
decoys.why: Consultation with an Infectious Disease specialist is crucial to discuss the possibility of rabies exposure, as well as to guide management of cellulitis and tetanus prophylaxis. Early consultation can help avoid delays in appropriate treatment.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
standard.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

177.title: Vaginal Bleeding
177.diagnosis: Vaginal bleeding could be due to various causes such as hormonal imbalances, infections, cancer, or trauma. The most likely cause in a patient with unknown history can't be definitively determined without further investigation.
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
177.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
177.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Coagulation profile, Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) test, Blood type and cross-match
Imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound, Pelvis X-ray if trauma suspected
Type & Screen / Crossmatch
interventions.why: To prepare for potential surgery or transfusion in case of complications related to the unknown vaginal bleeding.

interventions.label: β-hCG
interventions.why: As this is a case involving a woman, a pregnancy test is essential.

decoys.label: Aspirin
decoys.why: Aspirin is not an appropriate treatment for vaginal bleeding as it is a blood thinner and could potentially exacerbate the situation rather than alleviate it.

decoys.label: Oral Contraceptives
decoys.why: Without knowing the cause of the vaginal bleeding, starting oral contraceptives could be inappropriate as it may mask underlying conditions such as cancer or infection.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: Similar to Aspirin, NSAIDs are blood thinners and should not be administered without first determining the cause of the vaginal bleeding.

decoys.label: Antibiotics
decoys.why: Without a definitive diagnosis, antibiotics may not be appropriate as vaginal bleeding can have various causes, some of which are not bacterial infections that would require antibiotic treatment.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

178.title: Epigastric Pain
exam: Epigastric Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
178.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
178.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Basic Metabolic Panel, Lipase, Amylase
Imaging: Abdominal CT scan with contrast
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing epigastric pain, which could be a sign of various emergencies such as acute abdomen or cardiac conditions. Oxygen should always be the first intervention to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: As mentioned in 'How to Use It on Exam Day', immediate action includes ordering oxygen, setting up IV access, and checking glucose levels.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs may exacerbate gastrointestinal bleeding, which is a potential complication in acute abdomen. They are not the first line of treatment and should be avoided until the cause of acute abdomen has been determined.

decoys.label: Antacids
decoys.why: Antacids are used for treating symptoms of acid reflux or indigestion, not for addressing the underlying cause of acute abdomen. They may provide temporary relief but do not help in diagnosis and treatment.

decoys.label: Digital rectal exam (DRE)
decoys.why: Although DRE can be part of a physical examination for acute abdomen, it is not the primary diagnostic tool for epigastric pain. The focus should be on imaging and laboratory tests to determine the cause.

decoys.label: Painkillers (opioids) for mild pain management
decoys.why: Opioids may mask the symptoms of acute abdomen, making it harder to diagnose and delaying necessary treatment. They should be used only when other interventions have been tried and fail to control pain or when time-sensitive interventions (oxygen, IV access, glucose check) are not required.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
easy.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

179.title: Abdominal Pain
179.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
179.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
179.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs
interventions.why: To assess the patient's overall condition

interventions.label: Perform physical examination with focus on abdominal examination
interventions.why: To identify potential localizing signs for abdominal pain

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel, lipase, amylase
interventions.why: For general diagnostic workup of acute abdomen

interventions.label: Consider imaging studies such as ultrasound or CT scan of the abdomen
interventions.why: To help determine the cause of the abdominal pain and identify any potential complications

interventions.label: Consult general surgery if signs of surgical emergency (e.g., peritonitis, rebound tenderness)
interventions.why: For management of potential surgical emergencies

interventions.label: Initiate broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected intra-abdominal infection
interventions.why: To cover common abdominal pathogens while waiting for culture results

interventions.label: Consider pain management as needed
interventions.why: For comfort and to help manage the patient's symptoms

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Abdominal pain could be indicative of a variety of conditions, some potentially life-threatening. Oxygen is a necessary first step to ensure adequate oxygenation and stabilization.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and blood products if needed. A glucose check can help rule out diabetes as a potential cause of the abdominal pain.

decoys.label: Administer NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen) for abdominal pain
decoys.why: NSAIDs can mask symptoms and delay diagnosis of serious conditions such as appendicitis or ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm.

decoys.label: Prescribe antacids for abdominal pain
decoys.why: Antacids are ineffective for treating acute abdomen, which is likely caused by conditions other than acid reflux or ulcers.

decoys.label: Order a pregnancy test for all females presenting with abdominal pain
decoys.why: While ectopic pregnancies can cause abdominal pain, a pregnancy test is not a standard diagnostic tool for acute abdomen. A comprehensive workup is necessary to determine the etiology.

decoys.label: Order an electrocardiogram (ECG) for abdominal pain
decoys.why: Abdominal pain is typically not associated with cardiac conditions. An ECG would only be indicated if there are concurrent symptoms suggesting a cardiovascular event.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. 180.title: Headache
180.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
180.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
180.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history and perform a physical examination
Order relevant laboratory investigations (e.g., complete blood count, electrolytes, glucose, thyroid function tests, and lumbar puncture if necessary)
Request imaging studies such as CT or MRI of the brain
Consult with a neurologist for further evaluation and management
If the patient's condition is stable, consider admitting the patient for observation
Oxygen
interventions.why: The case presents with a headache, which could be indicative of various conditions, some of which may require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: In any medical emergency, establishing two large-bore IV access is crucial for administration of fluids and medications.

decoys.label: Prescribe analgesics without obtaining a detailed history and physical examination
decoys.why: It's important to first assess the cause of the headache before prescribing medication, as some headaches may be indicative of serious conditions.

decoys.label: Order a CT or MRI without suspecting a secondary headache
decoys.why: These imaging studies should be ordered when a secondary headache is suspected, as they can help detect structural abnormalities or other conditions causing the headache.

decoys.label: Initiate medication therapy without consulting a neurologist for further evaluation and management
decoys.why: A neurologist's input is crucial in managing headaches, especially when the cause is unclear or when secondary causes are suspected.

decoys.label: Admit the patient without observing their condition being stable first
decoys.why: The patient should only be admitted if their condition requires close monitoring due to the severity of symptoms or suspected underlying conditions. Admission without stabilization could lead to unnecessary resource utilization.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 181.title: Nocturnal Enuresis
181.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for Nocturnal Enuresis in an adult are psychiatric conditions (e.g., anxiety, depression), neurological disorders, or drug/substance use. However, it's important to note that the diagnosis is still unknown in this case.
exam: Nocturnal Enuresis — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
181.clinical_tip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
181.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
interventions.why: This is the first step in any clinical scenario to gather relevant information about the patient's symptoms, past medical history, and other potential factors contributing to the presentation.

interventions.label: Urinalysis (to rule out underlying UTI or other renal issues)
interventions.why: A urinalysis can help identify possible causes such as a urinary tract infection or other kidney-related conditions that may be associated with Nocturnal Enuresis.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Nocturnal Enuresis is not a life-threatening condition, but the case does not provide information about the patient's condition upon presentation. Therefore, it's essential to begin with lifesaving interventions that could address potential underlying issues.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is needed for various tests and treatments during hospitalization.

interventions.label: Monitor
interventions.why: Vital sign monitoring is crucial in any patient encounter to assess the overall condition and response to treatment.

interventions.label: Pulse ox
interventions.why: Oxygen saturation measurement is an essential initial step to evaluate a patient's respiratory status.

decoys.label: Blood glucose test
decoys.why: No clinical indication suggests diabetes as a cause of nocturnal enuresis.

decoys.label: CT scan of the brain
decoys.why: Although neurological disorders can cause nocturnal enuresis, CT scan is typically not the first-line diagnostic tool for evaluating it and may be ordered if there are other symptoms indicating brain abnormalities.

decoys.label: Psychological testing
decoys.why: While psychological conditions such as anxiety or depression can contribute to nocturnal enuresis, a full psychological evaluation is not typically the initial diagnostic approach without specific indications of a psychiatric disorder.

decoys.label: Sleep study (Polysomnography)
decoys.why: Although sleep disorders may cause or exacerbate nocturnal enuresis, a polysomnography is not routinely ordered as the first step in evaluating this condition unless there are symptoms of other sleep disorders present.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
easy.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
standard.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

001.title: Chest Pain
001.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no new murmur
001.clinical_tip: Needle decompression before CXR in the unstable patient — do not delay treatment for imaging.
001.objective: Immediate decompression and chest tube; 100% oxygen.

interventions.label: Move to Emergency Department
interventions.why: Acute respiratory failure requires ED care.

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: Large-bore access if hypotensive or peri-arrest.

interventions.label: Needle decompression
interventions.why: Clinical diagnosis — treat before imaging if unstable.

decoys.label: Administer a sedative
decoys.why: Sedatives are not typically administered initially in tension pneumothorax as they can worsen respiratory depression

decoys.label: Start antibiotics
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not necessary for a primary diagnosis of tension pneumothorax unless there is a secondary infection present

decoys.label: Apply a hot pack to the affected area
decoys.why: Heat application is not beneficial in cases of tension pneumothorax as it can increase intrathoracic pressure and worsen symptoms

decoys.label: Prescribe a blood transfusion
decoys.why: A blood transfusion would not be appropriate for a primary diagnosis of tension pneumothorax unless there is severe anemia or hemorrhage present

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

Oxygen was administered during transport.
easy.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Needle decompression before CXR in the unstable patient — do not delay treatment for imaging.
easy.objective: Immediate decompression and chest tube; 100% oxygen. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

002.title: Altered Mental Status
002.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Confused, follows simple commands; gait unsteady after recent fall
002.clinical_tip: Elderly or alcoholic with AMS after minor trauma — non-contrast CT head first.
002.objective: Identify bleed, reverse coagulopathy, neurosurgical evacuation if indicated.

interventions.label: Emergency Department
interventions.why: Acute neurologic change needs ED evaluation.

interventions.label: Seizure precautions
interventions.why: Prevent secondary brain injury from seizures.

interventions.label: Neurosurgery consult
interventions.why: Evacuation if midline shift or decline.

decoys.label: Admit the patient to a general ward
decoys.why: Subdural hematoma is an emergency and requires immediate attention. Admission to a general ward might not provide the necessary level of care, monitoring, or resources for such a critical condition.

decoys.label: Prescribe pain medication but without any CT scan
decoys.why: Pain management is important, but it should be done after confirming the cause of the head injury. Without a CT scan, the cause cannot be accurately identified, and the wrong treatment might be prescribed.

decoys.label: Administer a blood thinner to prevent further clotting
decoys.why: Subdural hematoma is caused by bleeding between the brain and skull. Administering a blood thinner could potentially worsen the condition by accelerating the bleeding.

decoys.label: Start the patient on antiepileptic drugs for seizure prophylaxis without a history of seizures
decoys.why: Antiepileptic drugs are used to prevent seizures, but they should only be administered if the patient has a history of seizures or if seizures have occurred during the initial evaluation. In the absence of these factors, their use is unnecessary and potentially harmful.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. I has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. I used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. I now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, I got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. I now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, I got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. I was found on the floor the next morning by family members. I was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. I is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. I has no sibl

003.title: Pelvic Pain
003.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: Suprapubic and unilateral lower quadrant tenderness
003.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
003.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.why: Need to rule out other causes of pain (e.g. renal colic, appendagitis) before considering testosterone.
Imaging is not recommended in the initial management of suspected constipation.
Given the history of renal colic, renal imaging should have been considered first to rule out an obstruction or nephrolithiasis.
It is not appropriate to perform a testosterone level in the initial management of suspected constipation without addressing underlying causes such as renal colic, appendagitis, etc.

decoys.label: Order an MRI of the brain
decoys.why: MRI is typically used for imaging the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and not the pelvis. The patient's symptoms point towards a pelvic issue.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without further investigation
decoys.why: Antibiotics are used to treat bacterial infections, but pelvic pain can be caused by various factors such as musculoskeletal issues or gynecological conditions, which may not respond to antibiotics.

decoys.label: Prescribe strong painkillers without diagnosis
decoys.why: While managing pain is important, it's crucial to identify the underlying cause of pelvic pain for proper treatment. Overuse of painkillers can also lead to side effects.

decoys.label: Refer the patient for a colonoscopy
decoys.why: Colonoscopy is used for examining the large intestine (colon) and rectum. Pelvic pain might be caused by issues in reproductive organs or musculoskeletal system, not the colon.

004.title: Abdominal Pain
004.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
004.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
004.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.why: Rationale for the correct ordering of the options
Rationale for the correct ordering of the options
Rationale for the incorrect ordering of the options

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without knowing the specific bacterial infection
decoys.why: Abdominal pain can be caused by a variety of conditions, not just bacterial infections. Antibiotics would not be effective for viral or non-infectious causes.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan without obtaining a patient history and physical examination
decoys.why: A thorough history and physical examination are essential to guide the choice of diagnostic tests. A CT scan is an expensive and potentially harmful test that should not be ordered without a clinical indication.

decoys.label: Administer opioids for abdominal pain without attempting non-opioid options first
decoys.why: Opioids are powerful pain relievers but carry the risk of addiction, constipation, and respiratory depression. They should be reserved for severe pain or when other measures have failed.

decoys.label: Prescribe antacids for abdominal pain without considering possible causes such as appendicitis
decoys.why: Abdominal pain can have many causes, including appendicitis. Antacids are useful for treating symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) but would not be effective for appendicitis or other conditions that require different treatments.

005.title: Headache
005.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
005.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
005.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the brain without contrast
decoys.why: A CT scan without contrast may not be sufficient to diagnose headaches, as it might miss some types of pathologies. A CT scan with contrast is usually necessary for a more detailed evaluation.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antibiotic
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not effective against viral infections, which are common causes of headaches. Therefore, prescribing antibiotics without first confirming a bacterial infection would be incorrect.

decoys.label: Prescribe a strong painkiller like morphine
decoys.why: Morphine is a powerful opioid and should only be used in severe cases of acute pain. For headaches, over-the-counter pain relievers or prescription analgesics with lower potential for addiction are usually sufficient.

decoys.label: Order a lumbar puncture (spinal tap) as the first diagnostic test
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture should be used carefully and only when other tests have suggested meningitis or another condition that requires it. It is an invasive procedure with potential complications, so it should not be the initial diagnostic step for unknown headaches.

006.title: Rash and Lethargy
exam: Soft, may have mild tenderness if systemic illness
Lethargy with intact or fluctuating mental status
Rash distribution and morphology documented; petechiae ruled out if toxic
006.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
006.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without a culture or identification of the causative organism
decoys.why: Rash and lethargy can have various causes, including viral infections that do not respond to antibiotics. Therefore, it is essential to identify the causative agent before prescribing antibiotics.

decoys.label: Administer a sedative for lethargy without determining its underlying cause
decoys.why: Lethargy can be caused by various conditions such as infections, metabolic disorders, or neurological problems. Sedatives may mask symptoms and delay diagnosis. A proper evaluation should precede any medication to manage lethargy.

decoys.label: Perform a skin biopsy without considering the possibility of systemic involvement
decoys.why: Rash can sometimes be a manifestation of systemic diseases like lupus, autoimmune disorders, or infections. In such cases, a biopsy from non-affected skin may not yield useful results. A comprehensive evaluation is necessary to determine the appropriate diagnostic approach.

decoys.label: Ignore further investigation after the initial presentation of rash and lethargy
decoys.why: Rash and lethargy can be symptoms of serious conditions such as meningitis, encephalitis, or sepsis. These conditions require immediate and appropriate management to prevent complications. Delay in further investigation may lead to poor outcomes.

007.title: Generalized Weakness
007.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
007.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
007.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

decoys.label: Aminophylline
decoys.why: Aminophylline is a bronchodilator used for treating asthma and other obstructive airway diseases, not relevant to generalized weakness with unknown diagnosis.

decoys.label: Digoxin
decoys.why: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside primarily used to treat heart failure and atrial fibrillation, not appropriate for generalized weakness with unknown diagnosis.

decoys.label: Lantus Insulin
decoys.why: Lantus Insulin is a long-acting insulin prescribed for diabetes management. Generalized weakness could be a symptom of hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia, but the order should specify a glucose level check before administering insulin.

decoys.label: Acyclovir
decoys.why: Acyclovir is an antiviral medication used for the treatment of infections caused by certain types of viruses, such as herpes simplex and varicella-zoster viruses. It's not indicated for generalized weakness with unknown diagnosis unless there are specific signs or symptoms suggesting a viral infection.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: See HPI Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI
