008.title: Burning During Urination
008.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 89 beat/min, Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
008.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
008.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: R1 - B1
interventions.why: All other orders are not correct

interventions.label: A2 - D3
interventions.why: Other orders have been done, but they were incorrect

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without a urine culture
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be prescribed only after a positive urine culture to confirm bacterial infection. Otherwise, it may lead to unnecessary treatment and antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan without considering the possibility of a simple UTI
decoys.why: A CT scan is not indicated for a simple urinary tract infection (UTI). It's an invasive test and should be reserved for more complex cases, such as pyelonephritis or kidney stones.

decoys.label: Prescribe painkillers without addressing the cause
decoys.why: While painkillers can alleviate symptoms temporarily, it's crucial to diagnose and treat the underlying condition (e.g., UTI) for a long-term resolution.

decoys.label: Recommend a diet high in spicy foods for someone experiencing burning during urination
decoys.why: Spicy foods can irritate the urinary tract and worsen symptoms of a UTI. A bland diet may be more appropriate to help alleviate discomfort.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

009.title: Abdominal Pain
009.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
009.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
009.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

decoys.label: Administer non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) without obtaining a detailed patient history or considering possible contraindications
decoys.why: NSAIDs should not be administered without evaluating the patient's medical history, as they may cause serious side effects in certain individuals, such as those with a history of stomach ulcers, kidney disease, or allergies.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without performing necessary laboratory tests for bacteria or considering possible causes other than bacterial
decoys.why: Antibiotics are only effective against bacterial infections. In some cases of abdominal pain, the cause may be viral or non-infectious. Therefore, it is important to perform laboratory tests before prescribing antibiotics.

decoys.label: Order an immediate CT scan without first obtaining a complete medical history and performing a physical examination
decoys.why: A CT scan should be ordered judiciously as it exposes the patient to radiation. Performing a complete medical history and physical examination helps to determine whether a CT scan is necessary, and if so, which specific areas need to be imaged.

decoys.label: Refer the patient for surgery without first attempting non-surgical treatments or obtaining additional diagnostic information
decoys.why: Surgical intervention should be a last resort in managing abdominal pain. Non-surgical treatments such as medication, diet modifications, and fluid management should be attempted first. Additionally, further diagnostic tests may be necessary to confirm the necessity of surgery.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. 010.title: Pain in Legs
exam: Pain in Legs — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
010.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
010.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: A
interventions.why: Brown infections on the dermis, likely due to a systemic issue such as diabetes. Topical treatments like creams or ointments may not be effective; systemic antibiotics may be required.

interventions.label: D
interventions.why: Cases of brown lesions in the genital area, especially if accompanied by pruritus and discomfort, could indicate an STI such as chlamydia or gonorrhea. Treatment would depend on test results but often involves antibiotics.

interventions.label: B
interventions.why: The diagnosis is not supported by the image provided. The patient's history, physical examination, and current treatment regimen do not suggest diabetes as the cause of the skin lesions.

interventions.label: C
interventions.why: Given the presence of brown lesions in the genital area and associated symptoms, chlamydia or gonorrhea are more likely causes. Swabs for STIs would be an appropriate initial diagnostic test.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics
decoys.why: Pain in legs is not typically caused by bacterial infection, which antibiotics are designed to treat. The correct diagnosis for the given symptoms is unknown.

decoys.label: Recommend surgery without further investigation
decoys.why: It's essential to identify the specific condition causing the leg pain before recommending any surgical interventions. Without a proper diagnosis, surgery could be inappropriate or unnecessary.

decoys.label: Prescribe strong analgesics without assessing their necessity
decoys.why: While pain relief is crucial for managing leg pain, prescribing strong analgesics should be done with caution. First, the severity of the pain and patient's medical history need to be evaluated to ensure that stronger medications are indeed necessary.

decoys.label: Ignore symptoms and ask the patient to return if the condition worsens
decoys.why: Ignoring symptoms or delaying further investigation can lead to delayed diagnosis and potentially poorer treatment outcomes. It is essential to evaluate the cause of leg pain promptly, even if it seems minor at first.

011.title: Abdominal Pain
011.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
011.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
011.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Perform complete physical examination, with special focus on abdominal exam
Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Electrolytes, Liver Function Tests (LFTs), Amylase, Lipase
Order imaging studies: Abdominal ultrasound, CT scan (consider IV contrast)
Consult Surgery for possible exploratory laparotomy if findings are suggestive of acute surgical abdomen
Oxygen
interventions.why: The case presents with abdominal pain which could be life-threatening. The patient may be experiencing distress or AMS, hence immediate oxygen is necessary.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access is essential for administering fluids and medications. Glucose check is important as hypoglycemia can present with abdominal pain.

decoys.label: Order a MRI scan instead of an abdominal ultrasound or CT scan
decoys.why: In acute abdomen, ultrasound and CT scans are preferred due to their speed and ability to quickly detect findings such as free air, free fluid, or obstruction that may indicate a surgical emergency. MRI is less time-sensitive and may not provide the necessary information in an emergency setting.

decoys.label: Order antibiotics without confirmatory culture results
decoys.why: Antibiotics should only be given after obtaining cultures when indicated, such as in cases of suspected peritonitis or appendicitis. Starting antibiotics without culture results may lead to unnecessary use and potential development of antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Order a head CT scan instead of focusing on abdominal exam and imaging
decoys.why: A head CT scan is not indicated in the initial workup for acute abdomen. The symptoms are localized to the abdomen, so it's essential to focus on a thorough physical examination and relevant imaging studies of the abdomen first.

decoys.label: Order a lactate test instead of obtaining vital signs
decoys.why: While lactate levels can be helpful in diagnosing severe infections, obtaining vital signs is crucial for initial assessment and triage. Vital signs provide important information about the patient's hemodynamic status, respiratory function, and level of consciousness.

012.title: Burning During Urination
012.diagnosis: Possible causes include Urinary Tract Infection (UTI), Genital Herpes, STDs, or Kidney Stones.
012.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 89 beat/min, Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
012.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
012.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Obtain a urinalysis and urine culture
interventions.why: These tests help identify the presence of bacteria, indicating a UTI. They also allow for antibiotic sensitivity testing.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing burning during urination, a potential symptom of a urinary tract infection (UTI). However, the symptoms could also indicate other serious conditions such as kidney stones or a sexually transmitted disease. To stabilize the patient immediately, it's crucial to ensure adequate oxygenation and establish IV access for fluid resuscitation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary to allow rapid administration of fluids in case of hypovolemia or sepsis, both potential complications of UTI.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without obtaining urinalysis and urine culture
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be prescribed without first confirming a UTI diagnosis through a urinalysis and urine culture to prevent the unnecessary use of antibiotics, which can lead to antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Prescribe antiviral medication for a UTI
decoys.why: Antiviral medication is used to treat viral infections such as genital herpes or STDs, not bacterial infections like UTIs. Antibiotics should be prescribed instead.

decoys.label: Order an X-ray for suspected Kidney Stones without first performing a urinalysis and urine culture
decoys.why: A urinalysis and urine culture can help confirm the presence of bacteria, which may suggest a UTI rather than kidney stones. If a UTI is present, it should be treated before proceeding with an X-ray to diagnose possible kidney stones.

decoys.label: Prescribe oxygen for burning during urination
decoys.why: Oxygen therapy is not indicated for burning during urination. This symptom may suggest a UTI, STD, or kidney stones, and a urinalysis and urine culture should be performed to determine the underlying cause.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

013.title: Abdominal Pain
013.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
013.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
013.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: CBC, metabolic panel, lipase
Obtain imaging studies: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (if renal function is normal), ultrasound if contraindicated for CT
Consult general surgery for possible exploratory laparotomy or other interventions as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: To ensure adequate oxygenation in cases of abdominal pain which can be a sign of various emergencies

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: To provide immediate stabilization and start critical investigations

decoys.label: Order an X-ray of the chest
decoys.why: An X-ray of the chest is not typically indicated for abdominal pain, unless there is suspicion of a concurrent thoracic pathology. However, in acute abdomen, a CT scan of the abdomen/pelvis is more appropriate.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without first obtaining lab results or imaging studies
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be administered before identifying the etiology of acute abdomen. Appropriate laboratory tests, such as CBC, metabolic panel, and lipase, and imaging studies need to be performed first.

decoys.label: Order a liver function test (LFT) instead of a metabolic panel
decoys.why: A liver function test is not sufficient for diagnosing acute abdomen. A metabolic panel provides more comprehensive information about the patient's electrolyte, glucose and kidney function levels.

decoys.label: Administer antacids instead of analgesics
decoys.why: Antacids are typically used for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) or peptic ulcer disease, not for abdominal pain associated with acute abdomen. Analgesics are more appropriate to manage the pain caused by possible intra-abdominal pathologies.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. 014.title: Facial and Upper Extremity Edema
014.diagnosis: Lymphedema or venous congestion (edematous disorders), Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or Pulmonary embolism (PE) as differential diagnoses
exam: Facial and Upper Extremity Edema — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
014.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
014.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, infection, or other systemic conditions that may be causing the edema

interventions.label: Perform D-dimer test
interventions.why: As a quick and non-invasive screening test for DVT/PE, positive results warrant further imaging investigation

interventions.label: Obtain duplex ultrasound of the lower extremities for DVT evaluation
interventions.why: To confirm or rule out DVT if clinical suspicion is high or D-dimer test is positive

interventions.label: Consult with a vascular surgeon or internist for further assessment and management
Initiate supportive care: elevation, compression bandages, and diuretics as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is exhibiting facial and upper extremity edema which may indicate severe distress or acute medical emergency. Immediate administration of oxygen is essential to maintain adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for prompt delivery of fluids, medications, and blood products if needed in this critical situation.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to those described in the case. A quick glucose check can help determine if the patient's condition is worsened by low blood sugar levels.

decoys.label: Oxygen
decoys.why: Oxygen therapy is not typically indicated for Facial and Upper Extremity Edema related to Lymphedema, Venous Congestion or Deep Vein Thrombosis. It may be used if there's underlying respiratory distress or hypoxemia, but it's not a standard treatment for these conditions.

decoys.label: IV access ×2
decoys.why: Intravenous (IV) access is usually not required for Facial and Upper Extremity Edema related to Lymphedema, Venous Congestion or Deep Vein Thrombosis unless there's a need for medication administration. However, it could be necessary if the patient shows signs of sepsis or other systemic infections.

decoys.label: Consult with a cardiologist
decoys.why: While a cardiologist may provide valuable input regarding the risk of Pulmonary Embolism, the primary specialist for Lymphedema and Venous Congestion is usually an internist or a lymphologist. For Deep Vein Thrombosis, vascular surgeon or an interventional radiologist would be more appropriate.

decoys.label: CT scan of the face
decoys.why: A CT scan of the face is not necessary for the diagnosis and management of Facial and Upper Extremity Edema related to Lymphedema, Venous Congestion or Deep Vein Thrombosis. It may be useful in the context of trauma, tumor evaluation, or other facial disorders.

015.title: Chest Pain
015.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac causes are most likely considering the chief complaint of Chest Pain.
015.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
015.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
015.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: EKG - to evaluate for signs of acute ischemia
Troponin labs - to assess for myocardial damage
Oxygen therapy if patient is dyspneic or hypoxic
Morphine for analgesia and potential vasodilation
ASA (acetylsalicylic acid) - to reduce the risk of thrombosis
Beta-blockers (if not contraindicated) - for rate control and reduction in oxygen demand
Consult Cardiology or Cardiac Care Unit (CCU) - for further management and potential interventions such as PCI or CABG
monitor
interventions.why: To keep track of the patient's vital signs

interventions.label: EKG
interventions.why: To evaluate for possible ischemia or arrhythmias

interventions.label: troponins x serial
interventions.why: To monitor for myocardial injury and diagnose NSTEMI/ STEMI

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are not typically indicated for chest pain caused by Acute Coronary Syndrome. They are used to treat allergies, not cardiac conditions.

decoys.label: Insulin
decoys.why: Unless there is a comorbidity of diabetes, insulin is not typically ordered for a patient presenting with chest pain and suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome. Insulin is used to manage blood sugar levels in diabetic patients.

decoys.label: NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Naproxen etc.)
decoys.why: While NSAIDs may provide some pain relief, they are generally contraindicated in acute coronary syndrome as they can increase the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and mask symptoms of an ongoing myocardial infarction.

decoys.label: Antiarrhythmics (such as Lidocaine)
decoys.why: In the initial management of Acute Coronary Syndrome, antiarrhythmics are usually not the first line of treatment. They may be used in specific situations where there is an ongoing life-threatening arrhythmia, but they do not address the underlying cause, which is often thrombosis or plaque rupture.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

016.title: Cough
016.diagnosis: Work-up for acute or chronic cough may include pneumonia, asthma, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), lung cancer, heart failure, or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
exam: Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
016.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
016.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order arterial blood gases (ABGs) if hypoxia or respiratory distress is present
Consider ordering imaging studies like a high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest, especially if persistent symptoms
Consult Pulmonology if symptoms persist or worsen
order1
interventions.why: Some reason for why order1 should be avoided or considered.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing a cough, which could indicate respiratory distress. Therefore, oxygen is necessary to help the patient breathe.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for administering medications and fluids if needed.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: Nasal cannula is a common method of delivering oxygen in cases of respiratory distress.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without performing a culture
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be prescribed without first determining the cause of cough, as many causes (e.g., viral infections) do not respond to antibiotics.

decoys.label: Prescribe proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) without diagnostic workup
decoys.why: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) should be diagnosed through tests like endoscopy, pH monitoring, or esophageal manometry before initiating long-term treatment with PPIs.

decoys.label: Prescribe corticosteroids without diagnostic workup
decoys.why: Corticosteroids are not the first-line treatment for an acute or chronic cough. They should be reserved for specific conditions like asthma, where a proper diagnosis is established.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the abdomen instead of chest
decoys.why: A CT scan of the abdomen would not aid in diagnosing pulmonary conditions such as pneumonia, lung cancer, heart failure, or COPD. A chest CT is more appropriate for these diagnoses.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

017.title: Weight Loss and Night Sweats
017.diagnosis: Malignancy (e.g., lymphoma, leukemia, or solid tumor), Infection, Autoimmune disorders
exam: Weight Loss and Night Sweats — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
017.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
017.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), Chemistry panel, Tumor markers, LDH, HIV, Hepatitis B and C serology, ESR, CRP
Imaging studies: CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing weight loss and night sweats, which can be non-specific symptoms of several conditions. However, the presence of these symptoms might indicate a metabolic or infectious process. Oxygen is essential to stabilize the patient upon presentation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and drawing blood for lab tests. Having two large-bore IVs ensures rapid and efficient administration of lifesaving interventions.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: A metabolic panel (CMP/BMP) provides essential information about the patient's electrolyte levels, renal function, and liver function. This information can help guide further evaluation and management.

decoys.label: High-fat diet prescription
decoys.why: A high-fat diet may not be appropriate for a patient experiencing weight loss due to underlying malignancy, infection, or autoimmune disorders as it could potentially exacerbate their condition.

decoys.label: Diuretics
decoys.why: Diuretics might be inappropriate for a patient experiencing night sweats due to potential fluid and electrolyte imbalances caused by the underlying condition (malignancy, infection, or autoimmune disorders), which could worsen if diuretics are administered.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs might be contraindicated in patients with suspected malignancy, infection, or autoimmune disorders as they could potentially mask symptoms or interact with other medications.

decoys.label: Thyroid function tests (TFTs)
decoys.why: While thyroid dysfunction can cause weight loss and night sweats, TFTs are not typically the first-line diagnostic test for malignancy, infection, or autoimmune disorders. Initial investigations should focus on identifying the underlying condition.

018.title: Constipation and Malaise
018.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for constipation and malaise could include infectious colitis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), or an organic bowel disorder. However, due to the unknown diagnosis, a broad approach should be taken.
exam: Constipation and Malaise — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
018.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
018.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain stool sample for culture, ova, parasites, and C. difficile toxin
Consider imaging (abdominal X-ray or CT scan) if no response to initial treatment
CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

decoys.label: Laxatives for constipation
decoys.why: While laxatives can help relieve symptoms of constipation, they do not address the underlying cause. A thorough investigation is necessary to identify and treat any potential infections or organic bowel disorders.

decoys.label: Antacids for malaise
decoys.why: Antacids are used to neutralize stomach acid, primarily for conditions like heartburn and acid reflux. They do not treat the symptoms of constipation or malaise, which may be indicative of a gastrointestinal infection or disorder.

decoys.label: Probiotics without identifying C. difficile
decoys.why: Probiotics can help restore the gut microbiota, but in cases where C. difficile infection is suspected, it's important to first perform a diagnostic test for C. difficile toxin before administering probiotics.

decoys.label: Barium enema for evaluation
decoys.why: While barium enemas can help visualize the colon, they are not typically used as a first-line diagnostic tool in cases of acute constipation or malaise. An abdominal X-ray or CT scan may be more appropriate initially due to their ability to detect a wider range of conditions.

easy.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

019.title: Abdominal Pain
019.diagnosis: Abdominal pain of unknown etiology, further workup needed for definitive diagnosis.
019.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
019.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
019.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), Basic metabolic panel, Lipase, Amylase
Imaging: Abdominal ultrasound or CT scan (depending on the clinical context and patient's condition)
Oxygen
interventions.why: For abdominal pain, oxygen should be administered to maintain adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for fluid resuscitation if the patient becomes unstable.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: Electrolytes and renal/liver function tests are important in evaluating abdominal pain.

interventions.label: UA ± culture
interventions.why: Urinalysis can help diagnose pyelonephritis, hematuria, or proteinuria. A urine culture should be obtained if infection is suspected.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without additional information or culture results
decoys.why: Abdominal pain of unknown etiology does not warrant antibiotics as a first line treatment. A definitive diagnosis is needed before starting antibiotics.

decoys.label: NSAIDs for abdominal pain without considering potential causes like appendicitis or cholecystitis
decoys.why: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) can mask symptoms of serious conditions like appendicitis or cholecystitis. A definitive diagnosis is needed before administering NSAIDs.

decoys.label: Gastrointestinal motility agent without considering possible obstruction
decoys.why: Administration of gastrointestinal motility agents can be harmful in cases of potential obstructions, such as bowel obstruction or volvulus. A definitive diagnosis is needed before administering these medications.

decoys.label: Neuroleptic medication for abdominal pain
decoys.why: Neuroleptic medications are not indicated for abdominal pain management in the absence of psychiatric symptoms. A definitive diagnosis is needed before administering neuroleptics.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. 020.title: Palpitations
exam: Palpitations — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
020.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
020.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain a 12-lead EKG
interventions.why: To identify any abnormalities in the heart's electrical activity

interventions.label: Order cardiac monitoring (telemetry or continuous EKG) if not already on it
Consult Cardiology for further management and interpretation of results
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing palpitations, which can be a symptom of various cardiac conditions. Oxygen is essential to maintain adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for administering medications and fluids if necessary. In emergencies like this, having large-bore IVs ensures quick administration.

interventions.label: EKG (Electrocardiogram)
interventions.why: An EKG can help diagnose various cardiac conditions that may be causing the palpitations.

decoys.label: Prescribe anti-arrhythmic medication without prior cardiac evaluation or consultation
decoys.why: This order is premature and inappropriate as the cause of palpitations is undetermined, and further evaluation (such as an EKG) and cardiology consultation are necessary before starting any anti-arrhythmic medication.

decoys.label: Recommend immediate cardiac catheterization for diagnosis
decoys.why: Cardiac catheterization is an invasive diagnostic procedure reserved for specific situations, such as when other tests are inconclusive and there is a high clinical suspicion of significant coronary artery disease or congenital heart defects. It's not the first-line test for undetermined cardiac arrhythmia.

decoys.label: Administer beta blockers without consultation
decoys.why: Beta blockers are a type of anti-arrhythmic medication, and their use should be guided by a cardiologist. The cause of palpitations is undetermined, so it's crucial to obtain further evaluation before starting any medications.

decoys.label: Refer the patient to a neurologist for an MRI scan
decoys.why: While palpitations can sometimes be caused by neurological conditions, a cardiac etiology is more likely in the context of undetermined cardiac arrhythmia. An EKG and cardiology consultation are necessary to evaluate the heart before referring for a neurological workup.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
standard.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

021.title: Abdominal Pain
021.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
021.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
021.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory workup: Complete blood count, electrolytes, liver function tests, amylase, lipase
Imaging: Abdominal ultrasound or CT scan with intravenous contrast
Symptomatic treatment: Analgesia, fluid replacement (if dehydrated)
Disposition: Hospital admission for observation and further evaluation
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with abdominal pain, which could be a sign of various serious conditions. Immediate oxygen administration is essential to ensure adequate oxygenation and support respiratory function.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and other lifesaving interventions as necessary. Having two large-bore IVs ensures rapid access in case of emergencies or complications.

interventions.label: Place the patient: in the appropriate location (ER → ICU vs floor)
interventions.why: Depending on the severity and potential urgency of the patient's condition, they may require immediate transfer to an intensive care unit. Initial placement in an appropriate location allows for timely management and monitoring.

decoys.label: Blood type and crossmatch
decoys.why: This is typically not necessary in the acute abdomen workup unless there is a known need for immediate blood transfusion, which is not yet established in this case.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without a specific diagnosis
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be prescribed based on a suspected or confirmed infection. In an undetermined acute abdomen, a specific infection may not have been identified yet.

decoys.label: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
decoys.why: MRI is less commonly used in the acute setting due to its longer acquisition time compared to CT scan. An ultrasound or CT scan with intravenous contrast is more appropriate for an acute abdomen.

decoys.label: Neurosurgery consult
decoys.why: A neurosurgical consult would not be indicated for acute abdominal pain without signs or symptoms of central nervous system involvement.

022.title: Found Unconscious
022.diagnosis: Acute Brain Injury (ABI) or Cardiopulmonary Arrest
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
022.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Vital signs and airway assessment
interventions.why: Assess patient's vital signs and airway status to determine the urgency of intervention

interventions.label: Order blood glucose and basic metabolic panel
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause altered mental status. Basic metabolic panel aids in identifying electrolyte imbalances that may have contributed to the patient's condition

interventions.label: Send STAT head CT to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage or trauma
Administer high-flow oxygen
interventions.why: High-flow oxygen supplementation can help maintain adequate oxygenation in patients with unknown cause of unconsciousness

interventions.label: Initiate Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) measures if necessary
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is unconscious, which could be due to lack of oxygen. Giving oxygen immediately can help prevent further complications.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering necessary fluids, medications, and blood products.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia can cause unconsciousness. Checking the glucose level can help determine the appropriate treatment.

decoys.label: Administer digitalis
decoys.why: Digitalis is a cardiac glycoside used for treating heart failure and atrial fibrillation, not appropriate for Cardiopulmonary Arrest

decoys.label: Order full body X-ray
decoys.why: For Acute Brain Injury or Cardiopulmonary Arrest, a head CT is more relevant to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage or trauma, not a full body X-ray

decoys.label: Order antiplatelet therapy
decoys.why: Antiplatelet therapy such as aspirin is used in the treatment of acute coronary syndrome, not for Acute Brain Injury or Cardiopulmonary Arrest

decoys.label: Order a stress test
decoys.why: A stress test is used to evaluate cardiac function and exercise capacity, not appropriate for Cardiopulmonary Arrest

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes.

023.title: Shortness of Breath
023.diagnosis: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) or Pulmonary Embolism (PE) are potential causes for shortness of breath in undiagnosed cases.
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
023.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
023.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Arterial blood gas (ABG), complete blood count (CBC), coagulation panel, D-dimer
Imaging: Chest X-ray, Computed Tomography Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) or Ventilation/Perfusion Scan (V/Q scan)
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This order includes electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests which are essential for various respiratory and metabolic disorders.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: A urinalysis is useful in diagnosing several conditions that could cause shortness of breath, such as pulmonary edema, pneumonia, or kidney disease.

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are not typically used for treating Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) or Pulmonary Embolism (PE). They are more commonly used for allergies and hay fever.

decoys.label: Bronchodilators
decoys.why: While bronchodilators can help with certain respiratory conditions like asthma, they may not be effective in treating ARDS or PE. These medications are designed to relax and open up narrowed airways, which is not the primary issue in these conditions.

decoys.label: Diuretics
decoys.why: Diuretics may not be appropriate for treating ARDS or PE. Diuretics are used to remove excess fluid from the body, which is usually not the primary issue in these conditions. In fact, excessive diuresis could lead to hypovolemia and worsen the patient's condition.

decoys.label: NSAIDs
decoys.why: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) may not be beneficial for treating ARDS or PE. These medications work by reducing hormones that cause inflammation and pain in the body, which is not the main issue in these conditions.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

024.title: Burning During Urination
024.diagnosis: Cystitis or Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) is the most likely diagnosis for this chief complaint in a CCS exam.
024.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 89 beat/min, Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
024.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
024.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Obtain urine sample for culture and sensitivity
interventions.why: To identify the causative organism and determine appropriate antibiotic therapy.

interventions.label: Prescribe broad-spectrum antibiotics pending culture results

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without obtaining a urine sample
decoys.why: This order is premature as a urine sample is required for confirmation of UTI and to determine the appropriate antibiotic.

decoys.label: Prescribe over-the-counter pain relievers for burning during urination
decoys.why: While pain relief may provide temporary comfort, it does not address the underlying infection causing the symptom.

decoys.label: Recommend home remedies such as cranberry juice or baking soda instead of prescribing antibiotics
decoys.why: While these may help alleviate symptoms, they do not treat the bacterial infection and could delay appropriate treatment.

decoys.label: Prescribe antifungal medication for burning during urination
decoys.why: Burning during urination is typically a symptom of a UTI, not a fungal infection. Antifungals are ineffective against bacterial infections.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

025.title: Generalized Pain
025.diagnosis: Non-specific generalized pain may suggest various underlying conditions such as musculoskeletal disorders, infection, neoplasm, autoimmune diseases, or metabolic disorders. Initial evaluation should aim to narrow the diagnosis.
exam: Generalized Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
025.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
025.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including onset, location, severity, duration, aggravating and alleviating factors, associated symptoms, past medical history, medications, and family history.
Perform a comprehensive physical examination focusing on the musculoskeletal system, neurological system, and general appearance, vital signs, and skin.

Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), Basic Metabolic Panel, Liver Function Tests, Rheumatoid Factor, Antinuclear Antibodies, and HIV screening.
Order imaging studies: X-rays, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) or Computed Tomography (CT) based on the findings from history, physical exam, and laboratory tests.
Consultations: refer to a rheumatologist, orthopedist, neurologist, or oncologist as needed, depending on the results of initial workup.
Oxygen
interventions.why: Generalized Pain could indicate a variety of life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering medications and fluids, which may be required based on the patient's condition.

decoys.label: Oxygen, IV access x2
decoys.why: Non-specific generalized pain is not typically associated with respiratory or intravenous needs unless there's an underlying condition causing both symptoms. In this context, oxygen and IV access are not clinically relevant for initial evaluation of non-specific generalized pain.

decoys.label: NSAIDs
decoys.why: While Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) can be used to manage pain symptoms, they should not be the initial course of action without a proper diagnosis. The underlying cause of non-specific generalized pain needs to be identified before starting treatment with NSAIDs.

decoys.label: Morphine
decoys.why: Morphine is a potent opioid analgesic and should only be used for severe, acute pain. In the context of non-specific generalized pain, morphine may not be an appropriate initial treatment due to its potential side effects and addiction risks. A proper diagnosis and risk assessment are necessary before prescribing morphine.

decoys.label: Antibiotics
decoys.why: Non-specific generalized pain is not necessarily indicative of an infection, so antibiotics may not be the best initial treatment. A thorough history, physical examination, and laboratory tests are needed to identify any bacterial infections before starting antibiotic therapy.

easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

026.title: Shortness of Breath
026.diagnosis: Acute Pulmonary Embolism or Pneumonia (most likely diagnosis based on Shortness of Breath)
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
026.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
026.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Order a D-dimer test and/or pulmonary angiography for PE suspicion
Order sputum culture, chest CT scan for pneumonia suspicion
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with shortness of breath, which is a symptom requiring immediate oxygen supplementation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large bore IV access is necessary for rapid administration of fluids and medications in the event of an emergency.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the abdomen
decoys.why: A CT scan of the abdomen is not relevant for evaluating shortness of breath due to acute pulmonary embolism or pneumonia.

decoys.label: Order an EKG (ECG) instead of arterial blood gases
decoys.why: Although an EKG can provide valuable information, it may not be sufficient for assessing respiratory issues such as acute pulmonary embolism or pneumonia. Arterial blood gases are more appropriate for evaluating gas exchange and acid-base balance.

decoys.label: Prescribe aspirin as primary treatment for suspected PE
decoys.why: Aspirin is not the first-line therapy for acute pulmonary embolism. Anticoagulants like heparin are usually administered, followed by oral anticoagulation with warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC).

decoys.label: Order a chest X-ray every hour
decoys.why: Repeating a chest X-ray excessively may not provide additional clinical benefit and can potentially expose the patient to unnecessary radiation. The frequency of imaging should be guided by clinical response, hemodynamic stability, and other relevant factors.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

027.title: Fatigue
027.diagnosis: A comprehensive workup is necessary due to the unknown cause of fatigue.
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
027.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
027.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC) and metabolic panel
interventions.why: These tests are useful in identifying anemia, electrolyte imbalances, or kidney dysfunction that could cause fatigue.

interventions.label: Consider additional testing based on initial findings such as thyroid function tests, vitamin B12/Folate levels, and comprehensive metabolic panel with liver enzymes
Oxygen
interventions.why: Patient presents with fatigue, which could be a symptom of multiple conditions including hypoglycemia. Oxygen is crucial to support patient's respiration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer necessary fluids and medications promptly.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Fatigue can also be a symptom of low blood sugar.

decoys.label: Prescribe a stimulant medication for fatigue
decoys.why: Stimulants are not indicated for treating the underlying cause of fatigue, which may be due to various conditions including anemia, thyroid dysfunction, vitamin deficiencies, or other metabolic disorders.

decoys.label: Request a CT scan of the brain
decoys.why: A CT scan is not typically the first line of investigation for unexplained fatigue. While it can help rule out certain conditions like brain tumors or infections, it's less likely to be the cause of generalized fatigue.

decoys.label: Order a sleep study
decoys.why: While sleep disorders can contribute to feelings of fatigue, a comprehensive workup for unexplained fatigue should initially focus on metabolic and other systemic causes before investigating sleep disorders.

decoys.label: Recommend excessive exercise for increasing energy levels
decoys.why: Exercise can help improve energy levels in some cases, but excessive exercise can lead to fatigue due to overexertion. A more moderate exercise regimen and appropriate rest should be recommended.

028.title: Pelvic Pain
028.diagnosis: Possible Gynecologic or Genitourinary Disorder, Abdominal Pain of Unknown Origin (APOO), Ovarian Torsion (in women of reproductive age), Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID), Ectopic Pregnancy, Appendicitis (if right lower quadrant pain is present), or other abdominal pathologies
028.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: complaining of pelvic pain for the last month.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
028.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
028.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Order urine pregnancy test in women of childbearing age
Order ultrasound for pelvis and/or abdomen
Consider lab tests for sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) if PID is suspected
Consult Obstetrics & Gynecology or General Surgery as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing pelvic pain, which can be a symptom of various conditions. However, initial priority should always be given to maintaining oxygenation and stabilizing the patient's condition.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids and medications quickly in case of emergency.

decoys.label: Order an X-ray of the skull
decoys.why: Pelvic pain is not related to the skull, making this order irrelevant for the given context.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without obtaining any diagnostic tests
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be prescribed only after a proper diagnosis, and in this case, multiple diagnoses are possible. Ordering tests to confirm the diagnosis is crucial before starting treatment.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the chest
decoys.why: The symptoms presented do not indicate any pulmonary or cardiac issues that would require a chest CT, making this order irrelevant for the given context.

decoys.label: Prescribe painkillers without addressing the underlying cause of pelvic pain
decoys.why: Painkillers alone may temporarily alleviate symptoms but do not address the root cause. Proper diagnosis and treatment should be sought before administering pain medication.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Pelvic pain History of present Illness: This is a 20-year-old previously healthy female presenting to your office complaining of pelvic pain for the last month. She rates the pain as a constant 7/10 sharp pain which worsens when she moves. She was encouraged by her boyfriend to come to you because she is having extreme pain during sex. Ibuprofen "takes the edge off." The pain is severe enough that she reports having difficulty sleeping. Her pain worsens during her menstruation and that the pain is "unbearable." She admits that she has had unprotected sex with her boyfriend a few times, but reports that she is taking an oral contraceptive. She denies multiple sexual partners in the past. She has had a few episodes of vomiting and still feels nauseous. No fevers or chills. She also reports pain when having a bowel movement. She denies any vaginal discharge. Past Medical History: Current medications: Oral Contraceptive Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She has never been to a gynecologist. Family History: The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer in her 50s. Her father had a 'heart attack' in his 40s. Marital/Family: The patient is the oldest of 3 children. Both of her siblings are healthy without medical problems. Personal habits: She admits to smoking 2 cigarettes a day to help lose weight. She also admits to smoking marijuana occasionally at parties. She also drinks alcohol once a week with friends. Occupation/educational: She is a college student and currently studying Art History and would like to be a teacher. Recreational: She enjoys to paint and train dogs. hard.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Pelvic pain History of present Illness: This is a 20-year-old previously healthy female presenting to your office complaining of pelvic pain for the last month. She rates the pain as a constant 7/10 sharp pain which worsens when she moves. She was encouraged by her boyfriend to come to you because she is having extreme pain during sex. Ibuprofen "takes the edge off." The pain is severe enough that she reports having difficulty sleeping. Her pain worsens during her menstruation and that the pain is "unbearable." She admits that she has had unprotected sex with her boyfriend a few times, but reports that she is taking an oral contraceptive. She denies multiple sexual partners in the past. She has had a few episodes of vomiting and still feels nauseous. No fevers or chills. She also reports pain when having a bowel movement. She denies any vaginal discharge. Past Medical History: Current medications: Oral Contraceptive Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She has never been to a gynecologist. Family History: The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer in her 50s. Her father had a 'heart attack' in his 40s. Marital/Family: The patient is the oldest of 3 children. Both of her siblings are healthy without medical problems. Personal habits: She admits to smoking 2 cigarettes a day to help lose weight. She also admits to smoking marijuana occasionally at parties. She also drinks alcohol once a week with friends. Occupation/educational: She is a college student and currently studying Art History and would like to be a teacher. Recreational: She enjoys to paint and train dogs. 029.title: Vaginal Bleeding
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
029.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
029.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Lithium
interventions.why: Lithium is commonly used in treating chronic kidney disease, especially in managing nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

interventions.label: Frusemide
interventions.why: Frusemide may help manage volume overload in patients with CKD by reducing sodium and water retention.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing vaginal bleeding, and immediate oxygen support is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: To administer fluids and medications as needed in case of severe bleeding or other complications.

decoys.label: Aspirin
decoys.why: Aspirin is not typically used in the management of chronic kidney disease (CKD) due to its potential nephrotoxicity and interaction with medications like lithium, increasing the risk of renal damage.

decoys.label: Lithium
decoys.why: Lithium is a known cause of chronic kidney disease, and it should be avoided in patients already diagnosed with CKD. Continuing lithium therapy may worsen the patient's condition.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can contribute to renal insufficiency in patients with CKD by reducing glomerular filtration rate and increasing blood pressure. They should be used cautiously or avoided altogether.

decoys.label: Iron supplements
decoys.why: Although iron deficiency can potentially exacerbate vaginal bleeding, administering iron supplements to a patient with CKD may increase the risk of developing anemia and worsening renal function.

easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

030.title: Pain in Right Arm
030.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or Pericarditis are common differentials for right arm pain, however further evaluation is necessary
exam: Pain in Right Arm — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
030.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
030.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain ECG to assess for acute coronary syndrome and other cardiac abnormalities.
Order cardiac enzymes (Troponin, CK-MB) to evaluate for myocardial injury in ACS.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing pain in the right arm which could potentially be indicative of a cardiac event, and oxygen administration is an essential first step in stabilizing the patient.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Administering intravenous fluids quickly is crucial for addressing any potential electrolyte imbalances or other fluid-related issues that may arise.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: This order is provided as a safe and non-invasive method to deliver oxygen, which should be done immediately upon the patient's arrival.

decoys.label: Order a MRI for right arm pain
decoys.why: MRI is not typically the first-line imaging modality for acute chest pain. ECG and cardiac enzymes are more commonly used to evaluate for Acute Coronary Syndrome or Pericarditis.

decoys.label: Prescribe aspirin for right arm pain
decoys.why: Aspirin is not appropriate as an initial treatment for acute chest pain without a specific indication. It should be given only after a definitive diagnosis of Acute Coronary Syndrome has been made.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the right arm
decoys.why: CT scan is not usually used to diagnose cardiac conditions such as ACS or Pericarditis. ECG and cardiac enzymes are more appropriate for this purpose.

decoys.label: Prescribe ibuprofen for right arm pain
decoys.why: Ibuprofen is an anti-inflammatory medication that may provide relief for muscle or joint pain, but it is not appropriate as the primary treatment for acute chest pain due to potential masking of symptoms and risks in cases of ACS.

031.title: Dizziness and Fatigue
031.diagnosis: Might be related to Cardiovascular, Neurological, or Endocrine disorders, but without definitive diagnosis
exam: Dizziness and Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
031.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain a complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), coagulation profile, and comprehensive metabolic panel.
interventions.why: To evaluate possible causes of dizziness and fatigue, including anemia, electrolyte imbalances, and endocrine disorders.

interventions.label: Brain imaging such as MRI or CT scan if neurological symptoms are severe or persistent.
24-hour Holter monitor for further evaluation of potential arrhythmias.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing dizziness and fatigue, which could be signs of hypoxia. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering fluids, medications, and drawing blood samples.

interventions.label: Follow-up Actions (Next 30 Seconds)
interventions.why: Universal lab tests such as CBC, CMP, UA, and EKG will provide valuable information about the patient's condition.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This order includes electrolytes, renal, and liver status which can help diagnose various conditions causing dizziness and fatigue.

decoys.label: Prescribe antihistamines for dizziness
decoys.why: Antihistamines are typically used to treat allergies, not dizziness. The cause of the patient's dizziness might be related to cardiovascular, neurological, or endocrine disorders, and further diagnostic tests are needed.

decoys.label: Prescribe stimulants for fatigue
decoys.why: Stimulants are typically used to treat conditions such as ADHD, not fatigue. The cause of the patient's fatigue might be related to cardiovascular, neurological, or endocrine disorders, and further diagnostic tests are needed.

decoys.label: Recommend a glucose tolerance test for dizziness
decoys.why: While dizziness can be a symptom of hypoglycemia, it is not the primary test for diagnosing dizziness. A complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), and electrocardiogram (ECG) are more appropriate initial tests.

decoys.label: Recommend a thyroid function test for fatigue
decoys.why: While fatigue can be a symptom of thyroid disorders, it is not the primary test for diagnosing fatigue. A complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), and electrocardiogram (ECG) are more appropriate initial tests.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

032.title: Abdominal Pain
032.diagnosis: Acute Abdomen (e.g., appendicitis, cholecystitis, mesenteric ischemia)
032.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
032.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
032.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and perform a physical exam
interventions.why: To quickly assess the patient's overall condition

interventions.label: Order abdominal x-ray (AP and lateral views)
interventions.why: To help identify free air under the diaphragm, possible calcifications or masses

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, leukocytosis, or thrombocytopenia

interventions.label: Order electrolyte panel, liver function tests (LFTs), amylase, and lipase
interventions.why: To help identify pancreatitis or other potential causes of abdominal pain

interventions.label: Consider ordering urinalysis
interventions.why: To evaluate for potential kidney involvement or infection

interventions.label: Consult General Surgery for possible surgical intervention
interventions.why: If the physical exam and initial diagnostic results suggest a surgical emergency

interventions.label: order1
interventions.why: Reason for correct order, if any

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

decoys.label: Order CT scan without first performing an abdominal ultrasound
decoys.why: An abdominal ultrasound is often the initial imaging modality for acute abdomen due to its non-invasive nature and ability to visualize the appendix, gallbladder, and abdominal aorta. A CT scan should be reserved as a secondary option if ultrasound findings are inconclusive or if there is clinical suspicion of other conditions like mesenteric ischemia.

decoys.label: Order only amylase and lipase without considering CBC, electrolyte panel, LFTs, or urinalysis
decoys.why: In acute abdomen, pancreatitis may coexist with other conditions like hepatobiliary disease or kidney injury. Therefore, a complete diagnostic workup including CBC, electrolytes, LFTs, and urinalysis is necessary to evaluate for potential complications.

decoys.label: Prescribe pain medication without first obtaining vital signs and performing a physical exam
decoys.why: A thorough evaluation of the patient's condition through vital sign monitoring and a physical examination is crucial for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and appropriate management of acute abdomen. Pain medications should only be prescribed after this initial assessment.

decoys.label: Order an abdominal MRI instead of an abdominal x-ray
decoys.why: An abdominal X-ray is often the first imaging modality used in acute abdomen due to its speed, availability, and ability to detect free air, calcifications, and dilated bowel loops. An MRI may be considered if the diagnosis remains unclear after initial workup, but it requires more time, is more expensive, and has lower availability compared to an x-ray.
