033.title: Altered Mental Status
033.diagnosis: Altered Mental Status - Possible CNS infection, metabolic disorder, or toxic-metabolic encephalopathy
033.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
033.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis (if patient is stable and no contraindications)
Maintain airway, administer oxygen as needed, and closely monitor vital signs
Oxygen
interventions.why: Altered Mental Status could be due to hypoxia, so providing oxygen is essential.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Immediate stabilization and assessment of vital signs is crucial in managing patients with altered mental status.

decoys.label: Administer antibiotics without culture or sensitivity
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be given without obtaining cultures first to identify the specific pathogen causing an infection, and then performing sensitivity testing to determine the appropriate antibiotic.

decoys.label: Order magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) as initial imaging modality
decoys.why: Computed tomography (CT) is often the initial imaging test of choice for patients with altered mental status due to its speed and ability to detect acute intracranial abnormalities such as hemorrhage or mass lesions.

decoys.label: Prescribe insulin without blood glucose measurement
decoys.why: Insulin should not be administered without first measuring the patient's blood glucose level to determine if hypoglycemia is present, as this could lead to further lowering of blood sugar levels and potentially worsen the altered mental status.

decoys.label: Order electroencephalogram (EEG) for diagnosis in the acute phase
decoys.why: An EEG is typically used to evaluate seizure activity or to diagnose certain types of encephalopathy, but it is not typically used as an initial diagnostic test in the acute phase of altered mental status. The initial workup should focus on identifying potential causes such as infection, metabolic disorders, and toxic-metabolic encephalopathy.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. standard.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. I has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. I used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. I now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, I got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. I was found on the floor the next morning by family members. I was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. I is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. I has no sibl

034.title: Headache
034.diagnosis: Unknown Headache: Migraine, Tension-type headache, Cluster headache, or other secondary causes
034.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
034.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
034.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including symptoms, triggers, and family history
Perform a physical examination focusing on neurological assessment
Order laboratory tests (CBC, ESR, glucose, electrolytes, liver function tests) to rule out secondary causes if clinical suspicion arises
Consider imaging (CT or MRI) if red flags are present (e.g., sudden onset, severe headache, focal neurological deficits)
Refer patient to a neurologist for further evaluation and management if necessary
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This order helps to evaluate the patient's renal and liver function, electrolyte levels which can be useful in diagnosing various conditions causing headaches such as metabolic imbalances, renal diseases, or hepatic diseases.

decoys.label: Prescribe immediate pain relief medication (e.g., opioids) for unknown headache
decoys.why: Initial management should focus on identifying the underlying cause of the headache before administering pain relief medications, especially opioids which have potential for addiction and side effects.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antidepressant without a thorough evaluation
decoys.why: Antidepressants are used in migraine prophylaxis but not as a first-line treatment for unknown headaches. A detailed history, physical examination, and further investigations are necessary to establish the diagnosis.

decoys.label: Order CT scan without considering MRI
decoys.why: Both CT and MRI have their roles in imaging for headache evaluation. The choice between them depends on various factors such as age, presentation, and specific diagnostic questions. Over-reliance on one modality could lead to missed diagnoses.

decoys.label: Recommend dietary restrictions without a proper diagnosis
decoys.why: Dietary triggers are often associated with migraines but not all headaches. A thorough evaluation is essential to identify the type of headache and determine if dietary modifications would be beneficial.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 035.title: Seizure
035.diagnosis: Status Epilepticus or Structural Brain Abnormality (pending further workup)
exam: Seizure — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Neurologic exam focused on mental status and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
035.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain stat head CT to rule out structural brain abnormalities or acute process
Administration of benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam) for seizure control
Consult Neurology for further management, workup, and long-term treatment options
Monitor vital signs, oxygenation, and neurological status
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide supplemental oxygen if the patient is having difficulty breathing

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: For administration of fluids, medications, and blood products as needed

interventions.label: monitor
interventions.why: To continuously monitor the patient's vital signs

interventions.label: pulse ox
interventions.why: To assess the patient's oxygen saturation level

interventions.label: CBC
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, infection, or other hematologic abnormalities

interventions.label: CMP
interventions.why: To evaluate renal and liver function, as well as electrolyte levels

interventions.label: glucose
interventions.why: To check for hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia which can trigger seizures

interventions.label: tox screen
interventions.why: To rule out toxicology as a cause of the seizure

decoys.label: Order immediate surgery for Status Epilepticus
decoys.why: Status Epilepticus is a condition characterized by continuous seizure activity, but it does not require surgical intervention. The correct approach would be to rule out structural brain abnormalities or acute processes first, followed by seizure control with benzodiazepines.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antidepressant for seizure control
decoys.why: Antidepressants are not indicated for the treatment of seizures. The correct approach would be to administer benzodiazepines or other anticonvulsants for seizure control.

decoys.label: Order a full body MRI instead of a head CT
decoys.why: Although a structural brain abnormality may exist, the initial step in the workup for Status Epilepticus is to obtain a stat head CT. A full body MRI is not necessary at this stage and can be time-consuming.

decoys.label: Administer digitalis (a cardiac medication) for seizure control
decoys.why: Digitalis is a cardiac glycoside used to treat heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and other arrhythmias. It should not be administered for seizure control. The correct approach would be to use benzodiazepines or other anticonvulsants.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

036.title: Headache
036.diagnosis: Primary Headache Disorders (such as Migraine, Tension-type headache, Cluster headache) or Secondary Headaches (due to intracranial or extracranial conditions) should be considered
036.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
036.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
036.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history and perform a physical examination
Order routine laboratory tests: CBC, Electrolytes, Glucose, Liver function tests, Coagulation profile (if there is any indication of bleeding or bruising)
Imaging studies (CT or MRI) if the headache is severe, persistent, or associated with neurological symptoms
Refer to a Neurologist for further evaluation and management if necessary
Oxygen
interventions.why: To ensure adequate oxygenation in case of hypoxia or compromised respiration.

interventions.label: Order
interventions.why: To initiate the autopilot protocol with essential orders for initial assessment and stabilization.

interventions.label: Never forget
interventions.why: Pregnancy test should always be considered in women of childbearing age.

decoys.label: Order CT scan as the initial diagnostic test
decoys.why: CT scan should not be the first line of investigation for primary headache disorders, it should be reserved for secondary headaches or when neurological symptoms are present.

decoys.label: Prescribe immediate pain medication without obtaining a detailed history and performing a physical examination
decoys.why: It is important to obtain a thorough history and perform a physical examination before prescribing any medication, as it helps to determine the underlying cause of the headache.

decoys.label: Order a lumbar puncture (LP) for all patients presenting with headaches
decoys.why: LP is not necessary for primary headache disorders and should be reserved for patients suspected of having meningitis or other conditions that may require spinal fluid analysis.

decoys.label: Recommend a dietary change as the sole treatment for migraines
decoys.why: While dietary changes can help manage symptoms in some cases, they are not sufficient as the only treatment for migraines. Medications and other interventions may also be necessary.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 037.title: Fatigue
037.diagnosis: Unknown fatigue could be caused by a wide range of conditions including metabolic disorders, infectious diseases, autoimmune disorders, neurological disorders, and psychological issues. Initial workup should focus on common causes.
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
037.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
037.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, ESR/CRP, HIV, Hepatitis panels, Lyme disease test, thyroid function tests
Imaging studies (if clinically indicated): Chest X-ray, MRI/CT if neurological symptoms present
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide oxygen to the patient, essential for maintaining life

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To ensure adequate intravenous access for administration of fluids and medications

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To assess blood glucose level, which can be a cause of fatigue

decoys.label: Blood type test
decoys.why: While blood type is relevant for transfusions and some medical procedures, it does not provide information about the cause of fatigue.

decoys.label: CT scan of the abdomen without clinical indication
decoys.why: Given that the initial workup should focus on common causes of fatigue and there are no abdominal symptoms, a CT scan of the abdomen is not clinically indicated at this stage.

decoys.label: Lumbar puncture without neurological symptoms
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture (spinal tap) is typically used to diagnose certain central nervous system infections or disorders. If there are no neurological symptoms, it may not be indicated.

decoys.label: Genetic testing without a known family history or suspicion of a genetic disorder
decoys.why: Genetic testing can help diagnose certain conditions, but it is usually indicated when there is a strong familial history or clinical suspicion of a specific genetic disorder. In the case of unknown fatigue, initial workup should focus on common and more easily detectable causes.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

038.title: Yellowing of Eyes
exam: Yellowing of Eyes — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
038.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
038.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Lab work: CBC, Liver function tests (LFTs), Coagulation profile, Reticulocyte count, Bilirubin levels
Imaging: Abdominal ultrasound or CT scan

decoys.label: Diuretics
decoys.why: Diuretics may worsen dehydration and electrolyte imbalance in patients with jaundice, as the liver's ability to regulate fluid and electrolytes is impaired.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without culture and sensitivity
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not typically indicated for jaundice of unknown etiology, as it is often a symptom of various underlying conditions, not an infection. Culture and sensitivity testing should be performed to guide antibiotic selection if an infection is suspected.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can exacerbate liver damage in patients with jaundice, as they can increase the risk of kidney and liver problems by impairing blood flow to these organs.

decoys.label: Oral contraceptives
decoys.why: Oral contraceptives may increase estrogen levels, which can contribute to cholestasis (a condition causing jaundice) and further liver damage. They should be temporarily discontinued in patients presenting with jaundice.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

039.title: Epigastric pain
039.diagnosis: Acute abdomen or gastrointestinal disorder (based on epigastric pain)
exam: Epigastric pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.

039.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory workup: Complete blood count (CBC), Basic metabolic panel (BMP), Lipase, Amylase
Imaging: Abdominal ultrasound or CT scan if ultrasound is inconclusive
Oxygen
interventions.why: Epigastric pain could be a symptom of various conditions, some of which might require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer fluids and medications as needed for stabilization.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to epigastric pain.

decoys.label: NSAIDs (Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can cause gastrointestinal irritation and ulcers, which may exacerbate the symptoms of acute abdomen or gastrointestinal disorder.

decoys.label: Antacids
decoys.why: Antacids are typically used for heartburn or acid reflux, not for acute abdomen or gastrointestinal disorders. The symptoms may be relieved by antacids temporarily, but the underlying cause remains unaddressed.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without culture and sensitivity
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be started empirically for acute abdomen or gastrointestinal disorders without first determining the specific causative organism through culture and sensitivity testing. This ensures that appropriate antibiotics are used, minimizing the risk of resistance and adverse effects.

decoys.label: Digital rectal examination (DRE)
decoys.why: A DRE is not typically indicated for epigastric pain unless there is a suspected associated condition such as prostate enlargement or rectal pathology. The primary focus should be on investigating the abdominal symptoms, hence an ultrasound or CT scan is more appropriate.

040.title: Chest Pain
040.diagnosis: Chest pain of unknown etiology, potential causes include acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, pneumonia, or esophageal disorders.
040.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
040.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
040.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), cardiac enzymes (Troponin I/T, CK-MB), D-dimer
Order chest X-ray and possibly computed tomography angiography (CTA) or echocardiography depending on initial results and clinical suspicion
Consult Cardiology for further management and possible stress testing if indicated
Continuously monitor vital signs, oxygen saturation, and ECG monitoring
Administer sublingual nitrate if patient has persistent chest pain and no contraindications
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing chest pain, which could be a sign of a life-threatening condition. Therefore, it's crucial to provide oxygen immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer necessary fluids and medications quickly in case of an emergency.

decoys.label: Order magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
decoys.why: An MRI is not the first-line imaging for chest pain of unknown etiology. It's typically used when CT angiography or echocardiography results are inconclusive.

decoys.label: Prescribe aspirin
decoys.why: Aspirin is not typically prescribed as a first-line treatment for chest pain of unknown etiology. It may be considered in certain situations, but a proper diagnosis and other treatments should be established first.

decoys.label: Administer morphine for pain relief
decoys.why: Morphine can mask symptoms and potentially delay diagnosis in cases of acute coronary syndrome or pulmonary embolism. Pain management should be approached carefully, considering the potential severity of the underlying condition.

decoys.label: Order arterial blood gas (ABG)
decoys.why: While ABG can provide valuable information about respiratory status, it's not typically ordered for chest pain of unknown etiology as a first-line test. Initial tests would focus more on cardiac and pulmonary evaluations.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

exam: Weakness — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.

interventions.label: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS)
interventions.why: This is the correct diagnosis, as indicated by the patient's symptoms and lab results.

interventions.label: MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

decoys.label: Unnecessary blood transfusion
decoys.why: In the acute phase of HUS, aggressive blood transfusions should be avoided due to the risk of worsening hemolysis and thrombocytopenia.

decoys.label: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can exacerbate renal dysfunction in patients with HUS, so they should be avoided.

decoys.label: Unnecessary dialysis initiation
decoys.why: Dialysis is generally indicated for refractory hypertension, fluid overload, or anuria (no urine output) in HUS. Early initiation of dialysis may not be necessary as many patients recover renal function spontaneously.

decoys.label: Surgical intervention for anemia
decoys.why: Anemia is a common complication of HUS, but surgical interventions such as blood transfusions or red blood cell (RBC) transfusion should be avoided in the acute phase due to the risk of worsening hemolysis and thrombocytopenia. Instead, supportive care and management of underlying causes are important.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including mechanism of injury, location and characteristics of pain, and associated symptoms.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with knee pain, but symptoms such as chest pain, fever, or trauma may indicate underlying conditions requiring immediate attention. Oxygen is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: X-Ray
interventions.why: A chest X-ray is a common initial imaging study ordered for patients presenting with symptoms like chest pain or fever.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan instead of X-rays
decoys.why: CT scans are usually not the first choice for acute knee pain as they expose patients to higher radiation doses and may not be necessary if X-rays show no abnormalities

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without a bacterial infection diagnosis
decoys.why: Acute knee pain is typically not caused by a bacterial infection, and prescribing antibiotics without a confirmed diagnosis could lead to unnecessary side effects and potential development of antibiotic resistance

decoys.label: Recommend knee surgery for acute knee pain without further investigation
decoys.why: Surgery should be considered only after thorough evaluation, diagnostic tests, and non-surgical treatments have been exhausted. Acute knee pain can often be managed with conservative measures.

decoys.label: Order a blood test to evaluate for acute knee pain
decoys.why: Blood tests are not typically used in the initial evaluation of acute knee pain as they do not provide information about the structures within the knee joint. X-rays and MRI are more commonly used for this purpose.

interventions.label: Obtain stool culture and sensitivity
interventions.why: To identify the causative organism for appropriate antibiotic therapy.

interventions.label: Obtain stool ova and parasites examination

decoys.label: Antibiotics without culture and sensitivity
decoys.why: Administration of antibiotics should be based on the results of stool culture and sensitivity to ensure appropriate treatment and avoid antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Barium enema
decoys.why: Barium enema is not typically used in the diagnosis of infectious diarrhea. It's usually used for diagnostic imaging of the large bowel, which is not the primary focus when dealing with infectious diarrhea.

decoys.label: Anti-motility agents (e.g., Loperamide)
decoys.why: While anti-motility agents can help manage symptoms of diarrhea, they are generally not recommended for infectious diarrhea because they may prolong the shedding of pathogens and worsen the disease.

decoys.label: Prednisone
decoys.why: Prednisone is a corticosteroid used to reduce inflammation in various conditions. It's not typically used for infectious diarrhea as it may mask symptoms without addressing the underlying infection and could potentially worsen the disease.

interventions.label: Order 1
interventions.why: Reason for correctness/relevance to DKA diagnosis and treatment

interventions.label: Order 2
interventions.why: Reason for correctness/relevance to DKA diagnosis and treatment

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is unresponsive, indicating potential respiratory distress. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To ensure adequate fluid resuscitation and to draw blood for labs.

decoys.label: Insulin without initial correction of fluid and electrolyte imbalance
decoys.why: In DKA, insulin should be administered after correcting the patient's dehydration, acid-base balance, and electrolyte levels to avoid hypoglycemia and complications.

decoys.label: Bicarbonate without initial assessment of pH level
decoys.why: Bicarbonate should only be administered when the blood pH is less than 7.0 to 7.1, as its indiscriminate use can lead to metabolic alkalosis and worsen DKA.

decoys.label: Potassium replacement without monitoring serum potassium levels
decoys.why: Potassium should be administered carefully in DKA, as the body may have abnormal potassium levels due to cellular shifts. Serum potassium levels should be monitored and replaced only if necessary.

decoys.label: Fluid replacement with a solution containing high glucose concentration
decoys.why: Fluid replacements in DKA should contain low concentrations of glucose to prevent further hyperglycemia and osmotic diuresis. Isotonic crystalloids, such as 0.9% sodium chloride or lactated Ringer's solution, are typically used.

exam: Weakness in Extremities — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.

interventions.label: Obtain immediate neurology consult for further evaluation and management
Laboratory workup: CBC, ESR, CRP, glucose, electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, thyroid function tests, Vitamin B12/Folate levels, Lyme disease serology, HIV antibodies
Imaging: MRI with and without contrast of the brain and spinal cord
Treat symptomatically: provide supportive care such as hydration, physical therapy, and pain management

decoys.label: Prescribe immunosuppressant medication (e.g. corticosteroids) without neurology consultation
decoys.why: Immunosuppressants can be harmful in certain neurological conditions like Guillain-Barre Syndrome and Myasthenia Gravis, and their use should be guided by a neurologist.

decoys.label: Prescribe anticoagulants (e.g. heparin) without ruling out underlying autoimmune or metabolic disorders
decoys.why: Anticoagulants may not be beneficial in cases of peripheral neuropathy or myopathy caused by autoimmune or metabolic disorders, and their use should be determined after a thorough evaluation.

decoys.label: Order only a lumbar puncture without an MRI
decoys.why: A lumbar puncture alone may not provide enough information about the underlying cause of peripheral neuropathy or myopathy, and an MRI is needed to rule out conditions like multiple sclerosis.

decoys.label: Prescribe symptomatic treatment (e.g. pain management) without addressing the underlying cause
decoys.why: Treating only symptoms without finding and treating the underlying cause may not provide long-term relief and can lead to complications.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
standard.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

047.title: Chest Pain
047.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
047.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
047.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain could indicate a variety of conditions, including cardiac issues that may require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer necessary fluids and medications quickly.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to chest pain.

decoys.label: Obtain a CT scan of the abdomen
decoys.why: This order is not relevant to acute coronary syndrome as it does not directly address cardiac issues.

decoys.label: Prescribe aspirin for stomach ulcer
decoys.why: While aspirin can help with some symptoms of a stomach ulcer, it is also an antiplatelet agent used in the management of ACS. However, in this context, it is being prescribed inappropriately.

decoys.label: Administer digoxin
decoys.why: Digoxin is typically used for heart failure or atrial fibrillation, not acute coronary syndrome.

decoys.label: Order a stress test
decoys.why: A stress test is usually used to assess exercise tolerance and cardiac function. An ECG and cardiac enzymes are more appropriate in the initial management of ACS.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

048.title: Cough
048.diagnosis: Possible underlying respiratory infection or pulmonary disorder
exam: Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
048.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
048.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps in identifying possible pneumonia, mass, or consolidation

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel (BMP)
interventions.why: Assesses for signs of infection, anemia, or other systemic issues

interventions.label: Order sputum culture if expectorated sputum is present
interventions.why: Helps identify specific bacterial pathogens responsible for the respiratory symptoms

interventions.label: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically, pending culture results
interventions.why: Provides initial treatment while waiting for culture results

interventions.label: Consult Pulmonology for further evaluation and management
interventions.why: Ensures comprehensive management of potential underlying pulmonary conditions

interventions.label: If fever or signs of pneumonia are present, consider ordering a chest CT scan
interventions.why: Helps in identifying complications such as abscesses, pleural effusions, or lung cancer

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Cough may indicate respiratory distress, thus oxygen is crucial.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Vital for administration of fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

decoys.label: Obtain urine culture
decoys.why: Urine culture is not relevant for a cough or possible underlying respiratory infection.

decoys.label: Start corticosteroids
decoys.why: Corticosteroids are typically used for conditions such as asthma exacerbations or allergic reactions, and not for a cough due to suspected pulmonary disorder without a specific indication.

decoys.label: Order thyroid function tests (TFTs)
decoys.why: Thyroid function tests are not typically indicated in the context of a cough caused by a respiratory infection or pulmonary disorder, as they are more related to metabolic disorders and endocrine diseases.

decoys.label: Order HIV test
decoys.why: While HIV can present with respiratory symptoms, it is not the primary consideration in a patient presenting with a cough without other risk factors or symptoms suggestive of HIV infection. Other tests should be ordered first to rule out common causes of cough.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

049.title: Headache
049.diagnosis: Primary Headache (Tension-type or Migraine) until further diagnostic evaluation
049.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
049.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
049.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including family history of headaches, trigger factors, and associated symptoms
Perform a physical examination focusing on the neurological system
Order appropriate laboratory tests (CBC, ESR, glucose, electrolytes, thyroid function tests) to rule out secondary causes if necessary
Consider imaging studies such as CT or MRI of the brain if symptoms persist, worsen, or are atypical
If no secondary cause is identified and headaches meet criteria for migraine or tension-type headache, initiate appropriate treatment (e.g., analgesics, antiemetics, antimigraine drugs)
Refer the patient to a neurologist for further evaluation and management if necessary
Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen is essential for any patient presenting with headache to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Time-sensitive interventions such as oxygen and IV access are crucial in the initial management of a patient presenting with headache.

decoys.label: Order antibiotics
decoys.why: Headaches are not typically caused by bacterial infections, so antibiotics would not be appropriate.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan immediately upon presentation without further evaluation
decoys.why: CT scans should be ordered after other diagnostic evaluations and if symptoms persist, worsen, or are atypical to rule out secondary causes like brain tumor or stroke.

decoys.label: Prescribe blood thinners
decoys.why: Blood thinners are not typically used for primary headaches such as tension-type or migraine, and could potentially be harmful if there is no underlying condition like a blood clot.

decoys.label: Order an electroencephalogram (EEG)
decoys.why: An EEG is used to diagnose seizure disorders, not primary headaches. It may be ordered later if the patient's symptoms suggest a seizure disorder.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 050.title: Headache
050.diagnosis: Primary Headache (e.g., Migraine, Tension-type headache), Secondary Headache (e.g., Intracranial hemorrhage, meningitis) should be considered
050.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
050.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
050.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including onset, duration, location, severity, and any associated symptoms
interventions.why: To gather necessary information to guide further diagnostic workup

interventions.label: Physical examination focusing on neurological assessment
interventions.why: To assess for signs of intracranial pathology or secondary headaches

interventions.label: Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Electrolytes, Glucose, Liver Function Tests (LFTs), and Coagulation studies
interventions.why: To rule out systemic causes of headache such as infections, metabolic disorders, or coagulopathies

interventions.label: Imaging studies: Non-contrast CT scan of the brain if there are signs or symptoms suggesting intracranial pathology. If CT is normal or inconclusive, consider MRI with and without contrast
interventions.why: To assess for intracranial abnormalities or structural lesions

interventions.label: Consider referral to a neurologist for further evaluation and management
interventions.why: For specialized evaluation and management of the patient's headache

interventions.label: Treat patient symptomatically with analgesics (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) and anti-emetics if necessary
interventions.why: To provide relief from symptoms while awaiting diagnostic results

interventions.label: order1
interventions.why: Reason for the correct order is not visible in the image provided.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is presenting with a headache, which could be associated with severe distress or altered mental status. Therefore, oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: It is essential to secure large-bore IV access for fluid resuscitation, administration of medications, and monitoring the patient's condition.

interventions.label: universal labs + imaging
interventions.why: In this case, a broad approach is necessary to evaluate potential causes for the headache. Universal labs (CBC, CMP, UA) and an initial imaging study such as a CT head non-contrast STAT would be appropriate.

decoys.label: Oxygen
decoys.why: Oxygen therapy is not typically indicated for primary headaches such as migraines or tension-type headaches, unless there are comorbidities like hypoxia or hypercarbia. In cases of secondary headache, oxygen may be used if there's suspected cerebrovascular insufficiency.

decoys.label: Antiplatelet medication
decoys.why: Antiplatelet agents like aspirin are not the first-line treatment for primary headaches. They may be considered in specific cases of migraine with aura, but typically not for tension-type headache or other forms of primary headache.

decoys.label: Steroids
decoys.why: Steroids are not routinely used for the acute treatment of primary headaches. They may be considered in certain cases such as cluster headaches, but their use should be guided by a neurologist.

decoys.label: Anticonvulsants
decoys.why: Anticonvulsants are not typically used for the acute treatment of primary headaches. They may be considered for the prophylaxis of certain types of headache, such as trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias, but their use should be guided by a neurologist.

051.title: Altered Mental Status
051.diagnosis: Possible diagnoses may include acute intoxication, encephalopathy, infection, metabolic disorders, or central nervous system (CNS) trauma.
051.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
051.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), toxicology screen, and coagulation studies
Imaging: Non-contrast head CT to rule out acute intracranial pathologies
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is exhibiting altered mental status, which may indicate hypoxia. Therefore, oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: In any critical situation, it's essential to address life-threatening issues promptly. Oxygen, IV access, and a quick glucose check are crucial initial steps.

decoys.label: Antipsychotic medication
decoys.why: Antipsychotics are typically not indicated for altered mental status without a specific psychosis diagnosis. Initial management should focus on identifying and treating the underlying cause.

decoys.label: Neurological examination
decoys.why: While a neurological examination is important, it should be performed after time-sensitive interventions such as oxygen, IV access, and glucose check have been administered. This order may delay necessary lifesaving measures.

decoys.label: CT scan of the abdomen
decoys.why: A CT scan of the abdomen would not be relevant for an altered mental status unless there is a suspicion of an abdominal cause or complication. The initial focus should be on identifying and treating potential intracranial causes.

decoys.label: Narcan (opioid antagonist)
decoys.why: While Narcan could be useful if opiate intoxication is suspected, it's not the first-line treatment for altered mental status. The initial focus should be on identifying and treating the underlying cause, and administering necessary supportive care such as oxygen, IV access, glucose check, and laboratory tests.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 052.title: Chest Pain
052.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses for a patient presenting with Chest Pain of unknown cause.
052.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
052.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
052.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order blood tests including cardiac enzymes and troponin
Administer oxygen if patient is short of breath or oxygen saturation is low
Consider obtaining a chest X-ray for further imaging
Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is essential to stabilize the patient immediately.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: As stated in 'Life-Saving Interventions That Must Be Ordered Immediately', critical principle for Step 3 CCS is that if you delay stabilization while ordering labs, your score plummets even with a correct diagnosis.

decoys.label: Administer aspirin
decoys.why: While aspirin is sometimes used in the treatment of acute coronary syndromes, it should not be administered as a first-line treatment without proper assessment and consultation with a healthcare professional.

decoys.label: Prescribe beta blockers
decoys.why: Beta blockers are often used in the management of chronic stable angina or hypertension, not as a first-line treatment for acute coronary syndromes. They can be harmful if given too soon after the onset of symptoms.

decoys.label: Order a computed tomography (CT) scan immediately
decoys.why: A CT scan is not typically used as an initial diagnostic test for acute coronary syndromes. An electrocardiogram (ECG) and cardiac enzyme tests are usually performed first.

decoys.label: Perform a stress test
decoys.why: A stress test is used to evaluate the severity of coronary artery disease, not as a primary diagnostic tool for acute coronary syndromes. The focus in these cases should be on diagnosing and stabilizing the condition.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

standard.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
standard.clinicalTip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
standard.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

053.title: Shortness of Breath
053.diagnosis: Pulmonary Edema or Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema (most likely diagnosis in a patient presenting with Shortness of Breath)
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
053.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
053.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Shortness of breath is a symptom that necessitates immediate oxygen administration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: The case describes shortness of breath, which may indicate an acute condition. Large-bore IV access ensures quick fluid resuscitation if needed.

decoys.label: Diuretics
decoys.why: Initially, diuretics may not be the most appropriate treatment for acute shortness of breath due to pulmonary edema or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The primary goal is to quickly relieve symptoms and improve oxygenation, which can be achieved through oxygen therapy, IV fluids, and supportive measures such as positioning the patient upright. Diuretics may be considered later if there is evidence of fluid overload.

decoys.label: Antihypertensives
decoys.why: Antihypertensives are typically not the first line of treatment for shortness of breath due to pulmonary edema or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The main priority is to reduce the volume overload on the heart and lungs, improve oxygenation, and stabilize hemodynamic status. Antihypertensives may be necessary if the patient has hypertension complicating their condition, but they should not be given as the initial treatment for dyspnea.

decoys.label: Nebulized beta-2 agonists
decoys.why: Nebulized beta-2 agonists are typically used for bronchodilation in asthma and COPD, not for pulmonary edema or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. In these conditions, the underlying issue is fluid overload, which requires a different approach focused on diuretics, heart rate control, and oxygen therapy.

decoys.label: Digitalis
decoys.why: Although digitalis (digoxin) may be useful in the treatment of heart failure with a slow ventricular response, it is not typically the first-line treatment for acute shortness of breath due to pulmonary edema or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. In these cases, the primary goal is to quickly relieve symptoms and improve oxygenation, which can be achieved through oxygen therapy, IV fluids, and supportive measures such as positioning the patient upright. Digoxin may be considered later if there is evidence of a slow ventricular response complicating the patient's condition.

054.title: Yellow Baby
exam: Yellow Baby — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
054.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
054.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Coagulation studies, Liver function tests, Electrolytes, Glucose, Bilirubin levels (total and direct), Aminoaciduria screen, Urinalysis, Blood Type and Rh
Empiric Antibiotics IV
interventions.why: Do not delay for LP if unstable or petechial rash.

interventions.label: IV Fluid Bolus
interventions.why: Septic shock physiology common in toxic presentations.

interventions.label: Blood Cultures
interventions.why: Before antibiotics when possible — not if delaying care.

decoys.label: Unnecessary Red Blood Cell Transfusion
decoys.why: Galactosemia and Bilirubin metabolism disorders do not typically cause anemia requiring blood transfusions.

decoys.label: Inappropriate Feeding with Soy-based Formula
decoys.why: Soy-based formula contains galactose, which can exacerbate symptoms of Galactosemia.

decoys.label: Prescription for an Oral Galactose Supplement
decoys.why: Galactose is contraindicated in patients with Galactosemia due to its toxic effects on the body.

decoys.label: Inadequate Phototherapy
decoys.why: Patients with high levels of bilirubin, particularly those with Bilirubin metabolism disorders, often require phototherapy. However, in this case, the patient is diagnosed with Galactosemia, not a specific Bilirubin metabolism disorder.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

055.title: Shortness of Breath
055.diagnosis: Pulmonary Edema or Congestive Heart Failure until proven otherwise
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
055.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
055.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Obtain electrocardiogram (ECG) to assess heart function and rule out acute coronary syndrome
Chest X-ray to evaluate lung fields, cardiac silhouette, and pleural effusions
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with shortness of breath which is a life-threatening symptom. Immediate administration of oxygen is crucial to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access and glucose check are also important for immediate stabilization of the patient.

decoys.label: Administer diuretics without initial evaluation and management of acute shortness of breath
decoys.why: Diuretics are used to manage chronic heart failure symptoms, not as an immediate intervention for acute pulmonary edema or congestive heart failure. Initial management includes oxygen therapy, IV access, glucose check, and evaluation with ECG and chest X-ray.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan instead of a chest X-ray
decoys.why: A chest X-ray is the initial imaging modality for evaluating lung fields, cardiac silhouette, and pleural effusions in patients with acute shortness of breath. A CT scan is more invasive and should be used when additional information beyond a basic evaluation is needed.

decoys.label: Order digitalis without initial assessment
decoys.why: Digitalis is a medication used to treat chronic heart failure symptoms by increasing cardiac contractility. It can cause arrhythmias, which may worsen acute shortness of breath in patients with pulmonary edema or congestive heart failure. Initial management includes oxygen therapy, IV access, glucose check, and evaluation with ECG.

decoys.label: Order a full blood work-up without initial assessment
decoys.why: Initial evaluation of acute shortness of breath should include a focused set of tests such as ECG, chest X-ray, oxygen therapy, IV access, and glucose check. A full blood work-up may be ordered later for further evaluation and management based on the initial assessment results.

easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

056.title: Abdominal Pain
056.diagnosis: Acute abdomen or intra-abdominal pathology (e.g., appendicitis, cholecystitis, mesenteric ischemia)
056.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
056.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
056.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), liver function tests (LFTs), and coagulation studies
interventions.why: To assess for systemic inflammation, electrolyte imbalances, and potential complications such as peritonitis

interventions.label: Obtain abdominal X-ray, ultrasound, or computed tomography (CT) scan as indicated
interventions.why: To identify possible causes of acute abdominal pain and aid in diagnosing the underlying condition

interventions.label: Start intravenous fluid resuscitation
interventions.why: To maintain adequate hydration and blood pressure in the event of potential sepsis or peritonitis

interventions.label: Consider pain management with appropriate analgesics
interventions.why: To manage the patient's symptoms and improve their comfort

interventions.label: Consult general surgery if patient's condition deteriorates or if diagnostic workup is inconclusive
interventions.why: For further evaluation, management, and possible surgical intervention if necessary

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

decoys.label: IV Fluids + NPO
decoys.why: NPO (nothing by mouth) should be ordered after the administration of IV fluids, not before, to prevent fluid and electrolyte imbalances.

decoys.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
decoys.why: Urinalysis (UA) is typically ordered separately due to its unique collection requirements. It should not be combined with other lab tests in a single order.

decoys.label: Consult Cardiology for Acute Abdomen
decoys.why: Cardiologists specialize in heart conditions, while acute abdomen is primarily dealt with by general surgery. This consultation would not be appropriate at this stage.

decoys.label: Order CT Scan before Basic Metabolic Panel (BMP)
decoys.why: A CT scan should not be ordered without first performing a basic metabolic panel to evaluate the patient's electrolyte and kidney function, which can affect the interpretation of CT results.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. 057.title: Abdominal Pain
057.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
057.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
057.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Consider CT scan if symptoms persist or worsen
Consult General Surgery for possible exploratory laparotomy if symptoms suggest peritonitis, or Obstetrics and Gynecology if female of childbearing age with abdominal pain
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide oxygen for the patient experiencing abdominal pain, which may be due to a variety of conditions including acute abdomen or peritonitis.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To ensure adequate IV access in case the patient's condition worsens and requires fluid resuscitation or administration of medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To evaluate for hypoglycemia, which can cause symptoms similar to abdominal pain.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan immediately
decoys.why: A CT scan should be considered if symptoms persist or worsen, not as an initial diagnostic test. Overuse of CT scans can lead to unnecessary radiation exposure.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without a definitive diagnosis
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be used judiciously and only after identifying the causative organism, if possible. Antibiotics may not be effective for many causes of acute abdomen.

decoys.label: Administer a laxative or enema
decoys.why: Laxatives and enemas are not appropriate for managing acute abdominal pain, as they can potentially worsen certain conditions such as appendicitis, intestinal obstruction, or perforation.

decoys.label: Order a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) instead of an ultrasound
decoys.why: An ultrasound is often the first-line imaging modality for acute abdomen due to its speed, cost-effectiveness, and lack of radiation. An MRI may be considered in specific situations where ultrasound is inconclusive or if there is a need to evaluate complex anatomy.

058.title: Shortness of Breath
058.diagnosis: Pulmonary Embolism or Congestive Heart Failure (CHF) are common considerations in a patient presenting with Shortness of Breath of unknown etiology
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
058.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
058.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and perform physical examination
interventions.why: To assess the patient's general condition and to guide further workup

interventions.label: Order electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: To detect heart abnormalities such as arrhythmias, ischemia or infarction that could be contributing to the symptoms

interventions.label: Order chest X-ray
interventions.why: To evaluate for pulmonary edema, consolidation, or pneumonia

interventions.label: Order D-dimer test
interventions.why: As it is a useful initial screening test for venous thromboembolism when clinical suspicion is high and can help guide further testing

interventions.label: If D-dimer is positive, order a ventilation/perfusion scan or computed tomography pulmonary angiogram (CTPA)

If the patient exhibits signs of heart failure such as jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, and crackles on lung auscultation, consider ordering a B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) or N-terminal pro B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP)
Consider echocardiography if heart failure is suspected
interventions.why: To provide further information about cardiac function and structure

interventions.label: If the patient's condition deteriorates, consider intubation and mechanical ventilation
interventions.why: To support ventilation if the patient becomes hypoxic or dyspneic

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Shortness of Breath is the chief complaint, making oxygen a necessary order immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The patient may require further interventions and IV access is essential for administering medications or fluids.

interventions.label: monitor
interventions.why: Vital signs monitoring is crucial to assess the patient's condition and response to initial interventions.

interventions.label: pulse ox
interventions.why: Pulse oximetry provides important information about the patient's oxygen saturation level.

decoys.label: Administer a diuretic without first evaluating the cause of shortness of breath
decoys.why: Diuretics are not an initial treatment for shortness of breath, as the underlying cause needs to be identified. In this context, CHF or pulmonary embolism may require different treatments.

decoys.label: Prescribe a bronchodilator for shortness of breath without first ruling out heart failure
decoys.why: Bronchodilators are used to treat bronchospasm, such as in asthma or COPD. In the case of shortness of breath due to CHF or pulmonary embolism, different treatments are indicated.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the abdomen instead of a chest X-ray
decoys.why: A chest X-ray is crucial in diagnosing conditions like CHF and pulmonary embolism, as it can reveal heart size, lung congestion, or blood clots. An abdominal CT scan would not provide relevant information in this context.

decoys.label: Order a complete metabolic panel (CMP) as an initial test for shortness of breath
decoys.why: A CMP is used to evaluate kidney and liver function, electrolyte levels, and blood glucose. While it may provide valuable information in certain cases, it is not a primary diagnostic tool for conditions like CHF or pulmonary embolism that present with shortness of breath.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.
