059.title: Shortness of Breath
059.diagnosis: Shortness of Breath could be due to various conditions such as Congestive Heart Failure, Pneumonia, COPD exacerbation, Pulmonary Edema, or Asthma. However, since the diagnosis is unknown, a thorough workup is necessary.
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
059.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
059.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: B-type Natriuretic Peptide (BNP) or Brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) and N-terminal pro B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP)
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing shortness of breath, indicating potential respiratory distress. Oxygen is the first life-saving intervention that must be ordered immediately in this case.

decoys.label: Administration of a bronchodilator without assessment or diagnosis of Asthma
decoys.why: Bronchodilators should not be administered without proper evaluation and diagnosis as they can have side effects and may not address the underlying condition causing shortness of breath.

decoys.label: Prescription of a loop diuretic for shortness of breath without investigating heart function
decoys.why: Loop diuretics are used to treat conditions like Congestive Heart Failure, but their use should be guided by appropriate diagnostic tests such as ECG and BNP/NT-proBNP to ensure they are effective and not contraindicated.

decoys.label: Ordering a CBC with no suspicion of infection or anemia
decoys.why: CBC is useful for investigating conditions like infections or anemia, but ordering it without clinical indication may lead to unnecessary cost and potential harm from false positives.

decoys.label: Administering an antipsychotic medication for shortness of breath
decoys.why: Antipsychotics are primarily used for the treatment of psychiatric disorders. They do not have a role in managing respiratory symptoms like shortness of breath, which is more likely to be caused by cardiopulmonary conditions.

060.title: Abdominal Pain
060.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
060.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
060.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Initial resuscitation: IV fluid, oxygen support, pain management
Laboratory tests: CBC, metabolic panel, lipase, amylase
Imaging: Abdominal CT scan (non-contrast initially, contrast if no perforation is suspected)
Diagnosis may require exploratory laparotomy or laparoscopy
interventions.why: If other tests fail to establish a diagnosis, surgical exploration may be required

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Abdominal pain can be a symptom of several life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is crucial to ensure adequate oxygenation and stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Vital signs monitoring, oxygen administration, and establishing IV access are critical initial steps in any emergency situation.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without knowing the specific infection
decoys.why: Broad-spectrum antibiotics should be administered if there's suspected peritonitis or an abscess, but it's crucial to identify the causative organism for appropriate treatment.

decoys.label: Blood transfusion without significant blood loss
decoys.why: Although acute abdomen may cause shock due to hypovolemia, initial resuscitation should focus on IV fluids and oxygen support. Blood transfusion is only indicated when there's evidence of significant blood loss.

decoys.label: Abdominal paracentesis for non-distended abdomen
decoys.why: Paracentesis is used to remove ascites fluid from the peritoneal cavity, but it's not indicated in cases of non-distended abdomen as seen in acute appendicitis or mesenteric ischemia.

decoys.label: Intramuscular (IM) injection for pain management
decoys.why: For severe acute abdominal pain, parenteral administration of analgesics like morphine is recommended to achieve rapid onset. IM injections can be less effective and time-consuming compared to IV or epidural routes.

061.title: Headache
061.diagnosis: Migraine or other secondary causes of headache (such as subarachnoid hemorrhage, meningitis, or temporal arteritis) are possible
061.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
061.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
061.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain immediate imaging (CT or MRI) to rule out any acute intracranial pathology
Order a lumbar puncture for CSF analysis if imaging is normal or inconclusive
order1
interventions.why: The order should be avoided because it is incomplete and lacks specific details such as patient information, treatment indication, and clinical reasoning.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing a headache, which could be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is crucial to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access x2 large-bore
interventions.why: In emergency situations like this, having large-bore IV access ensures rapid administration of fluids and medications.

decoys.label: Over-the-counter painkillers
decoys.why: They may provide temporary relief for migraine symptoms, but they do not address the underlying cause and can mask more serious conditions.

decoys.label: A prescription for a muscle relaxant
decoys.why: A migraine is not typically caused by muscle tension, so a muscle relaxant may not be beneficial in this context. It could also have side effects and interactions with other medications.

decoys.label: Ordering an EEG (electroencephalogram)
decoys.why: An EEG is used to evaluate brain function and diagnose epilepsy or other neurological disorders, not typically for migraine diagnosis. It would be unnecessary in the acute phase of a suspected headache.

decoys.label: A prescription for a serotonin antagonist (such as metoclopramide)
decoys.why: Metoclopramide is a prokinetic agent primarily used to treat gastrointestinal disorders, not migraines. Its use in migraine treatment is off-label and generally not appropriate as an initial approach.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 062.title: Altered Mental Status
062.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
062.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain Stat labs including CBC, Electrolytes, Glucose, BUN, Creatinine, Liver Function Tests, Coagulation studies, Toxicology screen, and Blood cultures.
interventions.why: To identify potential causes of altered mental status such as electrolyte imbalances, metabolic disorders, infections, or drug intoxications.

interventions.label: Immediate CT Head without contrast for acute changes.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with altered mental status, which may indicate hypoxia. Oxygen is essential to address this potential life-threatening condition.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is crucial for administering fluids and medications quickly in an emergency situation like this.

decoys.label: Administer sedatives without initial assessment and documentation of mental status
decoys.why: Sedatives should not be administered without a thorough assessment of the patient's mental status to avoid potential worsening of encephalopathy or delirium.

decoys.label: Prescribe an antipsychotic medication without initial consultation with a psychiatrist
decoys.why: Antipsychotic medications should be prescribed under the supervision of a psychiatrist due to potential side effects and risk of worsening encephalopathy or delirium.

decoys.label: Discharge patient home with no follow-up plan
decoys.why: Patients with acute encephalopathy or delirium require close monitoring and follow-up care to prevent complications and ensure proper management of underlying causes.

decoys.label: Order CT Head with contrast in a patient with suspected renal impairment
decoys.why: CT Head with contrast should be avoided in patients with suspected renal impairment due to the risk of nephrotoxicity and potential worsening of kidney function.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 063.title: Malaise
063.diagnosis: Malaise can be a symptom of various underlying conditions such as infections, autoimmune disorders, malignancies, or metabolic diseases. Until a specific diagnosis is made, it's essential to consider broad-spectrum diagnoses.
exam: Malaise — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
063.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
063.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP)
interventions.why: To evaluate potential underlying conditions such as anemia, infection, or metabolic disturbances

interventions.label: Consider imaging studies such as chest X-ray or CT scan if indicated
interventions.why: To identify possible infections, masses, or other pathologies

interventions.label: Consider infectious workup including HIV testing, Lyme disease serology, and urine/blood cultures
Consult with Internal Medicine specialist, Infectious Disease specialist, and Hematology-Oncology specialist
interventions.why: For further evaluation and potential management of the underlying condition

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing malaise, a symptom that may indicate distress or acute illness. Oxygen administration is crucial for maintaining adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering fluids, medications, and blood products as needed.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without culture results
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be given without a positive culture result, as it can lead to antibiotic resistance and treatment of the wrong organism.

decoys.label: CT scan without indication
decoys.why: CT scans expose patients to radiation and should only be ordered when clinically indicated, considering potential risks and benefits.

decoys.label: Prescribing HIV medications without confirmation of diagnosis
decoys.why: HIV medications should not be prescribed until a definitive diagnosis is made to prevent unnecessary treatment and potential adverse effects.

decoys.label: Referral only to Hematology-Oncology specialist
decoys.why: A malaise can have various causes, not just malignancies or metabolic diseases. Referral to Internal Medicine and Infectious Disease specialists is also important for a comprehensive evaluation.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
standard.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

064.title: Abdominal Pain
064.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
064.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
064.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To assess for anemia, infection, or leukocytosis

interventions.label: Obtain basic metabolic panel (BMP)
interventions.why: To evaluate electrolyte levels and kidney function

interventions.label: Obtain urinalysis
interventions.why: To rule out urinary tract infections

interventions.label: Obtain abdominal X-ray
interventions.why: To assess for free air, bowel obstruction or pneumoperitoneum

interventions.label: Consider obtaining ultrasound or CT scan of the abdomen
interventions.why: For further imaging to help determine the cause of the pain

interventions.label: If fever is present, obtain C-reactive protein (CRP) and white blood cell count (WBC)
Consult with a general surgery specialist if symptoms suggest peritonitis or free air in the abdomen
IV fluids
interventions.why: For hydration due to abdominal pain

decoys.label: Administer non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can mask the symptoms and potentially delay appropriate treatment for acute abdomen.

decoys.label: Prescribe laxatives
decoys.why: Laxatives are not effective for most causes of acute abdominal pain, and may worsen certain conditions like appendicitis or intestinal obstruction.

decoys.label: Prescribe antacids
decoys.why: Antacids can provide temporary relief for symptoms like indigestion or heartburn but are not appropriate for diagnosing or treating acute abdomen.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without consultation
decoys.why: Antibiotics should only be prescribed after a diagnosis is confirmed, and their use can lead to unnecessary side effects and potential antibiotic resistance.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. 065.title: Anxiety
065.diagnosis: Psychiatric Disorder such as Generalized Anxiety Disorder, Panic Disorder, or Adjustment Disorder
exam: Anxiety — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
065.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
065.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a psychiatric consult for comprehensive evaluation and management plan
interventions.why: For accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment plan, it is essential to involve a mental health professional.

interventions.label: Order laboratory tests (CBC, Chemistry panel, Thyroid function tests) to rule out any medical causes of the patient's symptoms
Oxygen
interventions.why: Anxiety could potentially be a sign of underlying distress or AMS, requiring oxygen immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: In case further interventions are needed, it's essential to have adequate IV access.

decoys.label: Prescribe anxiolytics without a comprehensive evaluation
decoys.why: Anxiety disorders require a thorough psychiatric evaluation before starting any medication to ensure an accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment plan.

decoys.label: Order CT scan or MRI for anxiety symptoms
decoys.why: CT scans and MRIs are not typically used to diagnose anxiety disorders. They may be used to rule out physical conditions that can cause or mimic anxiety symptoms, but only after a thorough psychiatric evaluation.

decoys.label: Refer the patient for cognitive behavioral therapy without a psychiatric consultation
decoys.why: Cognitive behavioral therapy is an effective treatment for many anxiety disorders, but it should be part of a comprehensive treatment plan that includes a psychiatric evaluation and management plan.

decoys.label: Prescribe sleeping pills to manage anxiety
decoys.why: Sleeping pills are not typically used as a first-line treatment for anxiety disorders. They may provide temporary relief from insomnia, but they do not address the underlying anxiety and can have side effects.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

066.title: Cough
066.diagnosis: Possible Lower Respiratory Tract Infection (LRTI) or Asthma exacerbation due to chief complaint being cough
exam: Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
066.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
066.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order Complete Blood Count (CBC), Blood Chemistry, and Inflammatory Markers (CRP, ESR)
Inhaled bronchodilators trial for symptomatic relief if patient has a history of asthma or respiratory symptoms suggestive of asthma
Consult Respiratory Medicine if symptoms persist or worsen, or if there is suspicion of pneumonia or other complex lung conditions
Arrange follow-up appointment for re-evaluation and adjustment of treatment plan
Oxygen
interventions.why: Cough can be a sign of respiratory distress, necessitating immediate oxygen delivery. IV access is required for medication administration and fluid resuscitation if necessary.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access provides quick and efficient fluid resuscitation in case of hypovolemia or shock.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: Nasal cannula is typically the initial oxygen delivery method for respiratory distress.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without further testing
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be given without confirmation of a bacterial infection, as a lower respiratory tract infection may be viral in nature.

decoys.label: Nebulized steroids initially
decoys.why: Nebulized steroids are usually used in more severe asthma attacks and not as the first line of treatment. Inhaled bronchodilators should be tried first.

decoys.label: Chest CT scan instead of chest X-ray
decoys.why: A chest CT scan is usually reserved for more complex lung conditions and is not the initial imaging test for a possible LRTI or asthma exacerbation.

decoys.label: Prescribe corticosteroids orally
decoys.why: Oral steroids are usually reserved for more severe cases of asthma, and should not be the first line of treatment. Inhaled bronchodilators should be tried first.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

067.title: Frequency of Urination
exam: Frequency of Urination — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
067.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
067.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

decoys.label: Increase water intake to 3 gallons per day
decoys.why: Excessive water intake can lead to hyponatremia, which may worsen frequency of urination rather than alleviating it.

decoys.label: Prescribe potassium chloride supplements
decoys.why: Potassium chloride supplementation is not typically indicated for frequency of urination without a specific diagnosis. It may lead to hyperkalemia which could be harmful.

decoys.label: Administer non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for frequent urination
decoys.why: NSAIDs can worsen renal function and increase the frequency of urination, making them inappropriate for this condition.

decoys.label: Recommend bedwetting alarms for adult patients
decoys.why: Bedwetting alarms are used to treat nocturnal enuresis in children, not adults. Adult frequent urination may indicate an underlying medical condition that requires further investigation.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
easy.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
standard.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

068.title: Muscle Weakness
068.diagnosis: Neuromuscular Disorder (NMD) - such as Guillain-Barre Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, or Motor Neuron Disease, considering the symptoms of muscle weakness
exam: Muscle Weakness — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
068.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
068.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Lumbar Puncture for CSF Analysis
interventions.why: To evaluate the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) for protein levels and cell counts, which may help in diagnosing Guillain-Barre Syndrome

interventions.label: Electromyography (EMG) and Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS)
interventions.why: To assess the electrical activity of muscles and nerves, which is useful in identifying nerve damage or disorders

interventions.label: Thyroid Function Tests (TFTs) to rule out myxedema
Acetylcholine Receptor Antibody Test to evaluate Myasthenia Gravis
Consult Neurology
interventions.why: For further evaluation and management of the patient's neurological condition

interventions.label: MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

decoys.label: Analgesic for pain management
decoys.why: While muscle weakness can cause discomfort, analgesics are not directly related to the diagnosis and management of Neuromuscular Disorders. The primary focus should be on identifying the underlying condition and treating it.

decoys.label: Vitamin D supplement
decoys.why: Although Vitamin D deficiency can cause muscle weakness, it is not a common feature in Neuromuscular Disorders such as Guillain-Barre Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, or Motor Neuron Disease. Thus, a supplement may not be the most appropriate order at this stage.

decoys.label: Physical therapy for muscle strengthening
decoys.why: While physical therapy can help manage symptoms of muscle weakness, it is not a diagnostic tool and should only be initiated once the underlying Neuromuscular Disorder has been identified and appropriate treatment has begun.

decoys.label: Corticosteroids
decoys.why: Corticosteroids are typically used in conditions such as inflammatory disorders or allergies, not specifically for Neuromuscular Disorders like Guillain-Barre Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, or Motor Neuron Disease. Their use should be guided by the specific diagnosis.

easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

Order a nerve conduction study and electromyography (NCS/EMG) if neurological symptoms are present

interventions.why: To evaluate potential kidney or liver involvement that could be related to the leg pain

070.title: Fever
070.diagnosis: Infectious workup including sepsis, meningitis, or pneumonia are potential considerations due to the fever being of unknown origin.
exam: Fever — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
070.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
070.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Perform complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), and urinalysis
Consider obtaining lumbar puncture (LP) for cerebrospinal fluid analysis in cases where meningitis is suspected
Obtain a chest X-ray or consider computed tomography (CT) scan if pneumonia is suspected
Start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics after obtaining blood cultures
Oxygen
interventions.why: Fever is a symptom that could indicate an infection, and the patient may be in distress. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering fluids, medications, and other lifesaving interventions.

decoys.label: Start antibiotics without obtaining blood cultures
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be started after blood cultures to avoid altering the results and reduce the risk of resistant infections.

decoys.label: Perform a lumbar puncture (LP) without suspecting meningitis
decoys.why: An LP should only be performed if there is clinical suspicion of meningitis to minimize risks associated with the procedure.

decoys.label: Obtain a chest X-ray or CT scan before considering pneumonia
decoys.why: Imaging should be ordered after clinical examination and assessment of respiratory symptoms to guide the diagnosis of pneumonia.

decoys.label: Oxygen therapy without IV access
decoys.why: Oxygen therapy should ideally be administered through an intravenous route (nasal cannula or mask) to ensure proper delivery and patient comfort, especially in cases with suspected sepsis or other severe infection.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

071.title: Diarrhea
exam: Diarrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
071.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
071.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain stool sample for bacterial culture, Clostridium difficile toxin assay, and ova and parasites
Assess hydration status and replace fluids as needed with oral rehydration solution or intravenous fluids in severe cases
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: To check electrolytes, renal function, and liver function. Diarrhea can lead to dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: A urinalysis is often ordered with diarrhea to evaluate for possible infections or other causes.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without performing a stool culture
decoys.why: Antibiotics should not be prescribed until the causative pathogen is identified through stool culture, as it may lead to unnecessary treatment and antibiotic resistance

decoys.label: Prescribe metronidazole for diarrhea without assessing the patient's alcohol consumption
decoys.why: Metronidazole should be used cautiously in patients with a history of alcohol abuse due to the risk of developing a disulphiram-like reaction

decoys.label: Administer bismuth subsalicylate for infectious diarrhea
decoys.why: Bismuth subsalicylate should not be used in cases of infectious diarrhea, as it may mask the presence of Salmonella or Shigella in stool culture

decoys.label: Prescribe loperamide for more than 2 days
decoys.why: Loperamide should not be used for longer than 2 days due to the risk of developing constipation, abdominal pain, and drowsiness

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
easy.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

072.title: Routine Check-Up
exam: Routine Check-Up — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
072.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
072.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Complete Blood Count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate the patient's overall health and detect potential issues with red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.

interventions.label: Chemistry Panel
interventions.why: To assess kidney function, liver function, electrolyte levels, and glucose levels.

interventions.label: Urinalysis
interventions.why: To evaluate kidney function, detect urinary tract infections or other abnormalities.

interventions.label: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: To evaluate the patient's heart rhythm and cardiac health.

interventions.label: Imaging studies (X-ray, CT, MRI as necessary)
interventions.why: To visualize internal organs and structures for potential abnormalities.

interventions.label: Universal Labs (CBC, CMP, UA)
interventions.why: Establishing baseline renal, liver, and electrolyte functions, as well as urinalysis results is essential for a comprehensive workup.

interventions.label: EKG + CXR if indicated
interventions.why: An EKG can help in detecting cardiac abnormalities while a chest X-ray can aid in identifying lung pathologies.

decoys.label: Liver Function Test (LFT)
decoys.why: This test is not part of a routine check-up and is typically ordered when liver disease is suspected. Further diagnostic results are pending.

decoys.label: Thyroid Function Test (TFT)
decoys.why: Similar to LFT, TFT is not usually included in a routine check-up unless there's reason to suspect thyroid disease. Further diagnostic results are pending.

decoys.label: Bone Marrow Aspiration
decoys.why: This invasive procedure is typically performed when there's suspicion of certain conditions like leukemia or anemia, not as part of a routine check-up. Further diagnostic results are pending.

decoys.label: Lumbar Puncture (Spinal Tap)
decoys.why: This procedure is used to collect cerebrospinal fluid for diagnosis and monitoring of certain central nervous system conditions. It's not part of a routine check-up and requires a specific indication. Further diagnostic results are pending.

073.title: Memory Loss
073.diagnosis: Dementia (e.g., Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, frontotemporal dementia)
exam: Family reports progressive memory loss and personality change
Impaired short-term recall; disorientation; gait instability with recent falls
No meningismus; assess for bruising from falls
073.clinical_tip: Separate acute delirium from progressive dementia — always rule out reversible metabolic, infectious, and structural causes first.
073.objective: Stabilize, evaluate reversible causes of cognitive decline, and image when indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory workup: Complete blood count, Chemistry panel, Thyroid function tests, Vitamin B12 and Folate levels
Imaging: MRI brain with and without contrast
Neurology consultation for further evaluation and management
Psychiatry consultation for assessment of behavioral symptoms and psychological support
Oxygen
interventions.why: Memory loss can be a sign of hypoxia or impending respiratory failure.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Stabilizing the patient's airway, breathing, and circulation should be prioritized in emergency situations.

decoys.label: NSAIDs (Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can worsen symptoms of dementia and should be used with caution in patients with Alzheimer's disease or other types of dementia.

decoys.label: Antipsychotics
decoys.why: While antipsychotics may be used to treat behavioral symptoms associated with dementia, they are generally considered second-line treatment and should be prescribed only when other strategies have failed. First-line treatments include non-pharmacological approaches such as therapy and support.

decoys.label: Amphetamines
decoys.why: Amphetamines are stimulants and can worsen agitation, confusion, and hallucinations in patients with dementia. They should not be used for the management of dementia-related symptoms.

decoys.label: Memory enhancement drugs (e.g., Modafinil)
decoys.why: While memory enhancement drugs may improve cognition in healthy individuals, they do not slow the progression of dementia or reverse cognitive decline. Current treatments for dementia focus on managing symptoms rather than curing the underlying disease.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Separate acute delirium from progressive dementia — always rule out reversible metabolic, infectious, and structural causes first.
easy.objective: Stabilize, evaluate reversible causes of cognitive decline, and image when indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Separate acute delirium from progressive dementia — always rule out reversible metabolic, infectious, and structural causes first.
standard.objective: Stabilize, evaluate reversible causes of cognitive decline, and image when indicated.

074.title: Persistent Cough
074.diagnosis: Possible underlying respiratory infection, lung disease, or heart condition based on Persistent Cough
exam: Persistent Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
074.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
074.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain a complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel to evaluate for infection or inflammation.
Order chest X-ray or CT scan of the chest to help identify potential underlying lung pathology.
Oxygen
interventions.why: Persistent cough can be a symptom of various conditions, some of which may require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering necessary fluids, medications, or blood products.

decoys.label: Antibiotic prescription without culture or other evidence of bacterial infection
decoys.why: Persistent cough can be caused by viral infections, allergies, or other non-bacterial causes. Antibiotics are only effective against bacterial infections.

decoys.label: Prescribe a bronchodilator without a diagnosis of asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
decoys.why: Bronchodilators are used to relieve symptoms of asthma and COPD by relaxing the muscles around the airways. Prescribing them without a confirmed diagnosis could lead to unnecessary medication and potential side effects.

decoys.label: Order an electrocardiogram (ECG) as the primary diagnostic test for a persistent cough
decoys.why: While heart conditions can cause symptoms similar to those of a persistent cough, an ECG is not the first-line test for diagnosing lung or respiratory issues. A chest X-ray or CT scan would be more appropriate.

decoys.label: Order a sputum culture without proper preparation and technique
decoys.why: A sputum culture is used to identify the specific bacteria causing an infection. Improper collection techniques can lead to inaccurate results, which could delay appropriate treatment.

075.title: Chest Pain
075.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or Aortic Dissection is a common differential diagnosis for an unknown cause of chest pain.
075.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
075.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
075.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: To evaluate for signs of ischemia or myocardial infarction.

interventions.label: Troponin I/T levels
interventions.why: For the detection and evaluation of myocardial damage.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: To evaluate for alternative causes of chest pain, such as pneumonia or pulmonary embolism.

interventions.label: Consult Cardiology
interventions.why: To discuss further diagnostic tests, potential treatments, and management strategies.

interventions.label: Pain management with aspirin and nitroglycerin
interventions.why: Temporary pain relief and potential benefits in reducing ischemia.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough medical history and perform a physical examination
order2
interventions.why: appropriate medication and dose based on guidelines

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions such as myocardial infarction or pulmonary embolism. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Stabilizing the patient's vital signs and checking for hypoglycemia are critical initial steps in managing chest pain.

decoys.label: Administer morphine for chest pain
decoys.why: Morphine is not the first line of treatment for acute coronary syndrome or aortic dissection. It can mask symptoms and worsen heart failure. Nitroglycerin is typically used for immediate relief of angina pectoris, not morphine.

decoys.label: Perform an MRI scan
decoys.why: An MRI scan may be useful in some cases but it's not the initial diagnostic test for acute coronary syndrome or aortic dissection. The first line of imaging is usually an electrocardiogram (ECG) and chest X-ray.

decoys.label: Administer heparin without consultation from Cardiology
decoys.why: Heparin is an anticoagulant that may be used in some cases of acute coronary syndrome or aortic dissection, but it should not be administered without proper consultation from a cardiologist. Improper use can lead to excessive bleeding.

decoys.label: Prescribe warfarin for immediate treatment
decoys.why: Warfarin is an anticoagulant that may be used in some cases of acute coronary syndrome or aortic dissection, but it should not be prescribed immediately. The initial therapy often includes aspirin and/or heparin, followed by warfarin if indicated after consultation with a cardiologist.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

076.title: Fever and Rash
076.diagnosis: Acute Viral Syndrome (assuming the fever and rash are viral in nature, as bacterial causes usually present with more localized symptoms)
exam: Fever and Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
076.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
076.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain a complete blood count (CBC), liver function tests (LFTs), coagulation profile, urinalysis, and blood cultures
interventions.why: To evaluate for potential complications such as sepsis or disseminated intravascular coagulation

interventions.label: Obtain a respiratory viral panel or polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for common viruses
interventions.why: To help identify the specific causative virus

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing fever and rash, which could be indicative of a systemic infection. Oxygen should be administered to maintain adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The case mentions severe distress or altered mental status (AMS), so it's important to secure IV access for fluid resuscitation and medication administration.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to fever and rash. A quick glucose check is essential in this scenario.

decoys.label: Administer antibiotics
decoys.why: Antibiotics are ineffective against viral infections.

decoys.label: Prescribe corticosteroids
decoys.why: Corticosteroids are not typically used for acute viral syndromes, as they can suppress the immune system and potentially prolong viral shedding.

decoys.label: Order an X-ray of the chest
decoys.why: An X-ray is usually not necessary for diagnosing acute viral syndromes, as they are typically caused by viruses and do not involve bacterial pneumonia or other conditions that would require imaging.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan of the brain
decoys.why: A CT scan is not necessary for diagnosing acute viral syndromes, as they do not typically involve neurological symptoms or conditions requiring brain imaging.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

077.title: Chest Pain
077.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac causes, Pulmonary Embolism, Aortic Dissection are among the differential diagnoses for chest pain of unknown etiology.
077.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
077.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
077.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: EKG - to evaluate for electrocardiographic signs of ischemia
Troponin I or Troponin T labs - for cardiac muscle damage evaluation
Chest X-ray - to assess for pulmonary causes and cardiac silhouette
D-dimer lab - for possible pulmonary embolism
CT Angiography or Echocardiogram - for assessment of coronary arteries, aorta, and potential thrombus
Pulmonary Function Tests (if needed based on chest X-ray results)
Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can indicate several life-threatening conditions, and early oxygen administration is crucial.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access allows for rapid fluid resuscitation if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can present with symptoms similar to chest pain.

decoys.label: Heart Catheterization
decoys.why: It's an invasive procedure that should be performed after initial non-invasive tests have shown indications of significant coronary artery disease. Initial evaluation for ACS typically includes EKG, Troponin I/T, and imaging studies.

decoys.label: Aspirin
decoys.why: While Aspirin can be beneficial in some cases of ACS to reduce platelet activity and prevent further clotting, it is not a definitive diagnostic test for chest pain of unknown etiology.

decoys.label: CT Scan of the Brain
decoys.why: A CT scan of the brain would not be useful in diagnosing cardiac conditions such as ACS, pulmonary embolism, or aortic dissection. These conditions require cardiovascular imaging and lab tests.

decoys.label: Vitamin D Test
decoys.why: A Vitamin D test is not directly related to the diagnosis of ACS, pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, or other causes of chest pain. These conditions require cardiovascular tests and lab work.

078.title: Generalized Weakness
078.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses could include Guillain-Barré Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome, or other neuromuscular disorders.
078.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
078.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
078.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain urgent laboratory workup including CBC, Chemistry panel, LFTs, Coagulation profile, TSH, T4, B12, Folic acid, ESR, CRP, and Rheumatoid factor
Consider ordering a CSF analysis for protein levels if there is suspicion of a demyelinating disorder such as Guillain-Barré Syndrome
Order nerve conduction studies (NCS) and electromyography (EMG) to evaluate the electrical activity of muscles and nerves
Consider ordering a chest X-ray or CT scan if there is suspicion of an underlying infection or malignancy
Consult Neurology for further evaluation and management
MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

decoys.label: Glucose · BMP, ECG + Monitor
decoys.why: These tests are not directly related to the diagnosis of Guillain-Barré Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome or other neuromuscular disorders.

decoys.label: Order Plasma Exchange
decoys.why: Although plasma exchange is a treatment for Guillain-Barré Syndrome, it should not be ordered without proper diagnosis and consultation with a neurologist.

decoys.label: Prescribe Corticosteroids
decoys.why: Corticosteroids are used in the treatment of some neuromuscular disorders such as Myasthenia Gravis, but they may not be appropriate without proper diagnosis and consultation.

decoys.label: Order a MRI scan
decoys.why: An MRI is not typically used for the initial evaluation of Guillain-Barré Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome or other neuromuscular disorders. An MRI may be useful if there is suspicion of an underlying structural problem, but further consultation with a neurologist would be necessary.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. 079.title: Back Pain
079.diagnosis: Degenerative Disc Disease, Spinal Stenosis, or Herniated Disc - Initial Presentation of Back Pain
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
079.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
079.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Order imaging studies (MRI or CT scan) to determine the specific cause and location of back pain
Refer the patient to a Neurologist or Orthopedic Surgeon for further evaluation and management
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: Evaluate electrolytes, renal, and liver function

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Potential spinal cord injury or trauma

decoys.label: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs) without proper evaluation
decoys.why: Initial treatment for degenerative disc disease, spinal stenosis, or herniated disc often involves imaging studies to determine the specific cause and location of back pain before starting medication.

decoys.label: Muscle relaxants
decoys.why: Muscle relaxants may provide temporary relief but do not address the underlying issue causing back pain. Proper evaluation is needed first to determine the specific cause.

decoys.label: Narcotic analgesics for chronic use without proper evaluation
decoys.why: Chronic use of narcotics can lead to dependence and other harmful side effects. The underlying cause of back pain should be determined before considering these medications.

decoys.label: Referral to a Physical Therapist without proper evaluation
decoys.why: While physical therapy can help manage symptoms, it is important to first determine the specific cause of back pain to ensure the appropriate treatment plan is initiated.

080.title: Vomiting
080.diagnosis: Unknown cause of vomiting - consider gastroenteritis, food poisoning, migraine, peptic ulcer disease, or other conditions
exam: Vomiting — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
080.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
080.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory workup: CBC, electrolytes, urinalysis
Obtain imaging (if necessary): Abdominal X-ray, CT scan if symptoms persist or worsen
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing vomiting, which could be a symptom of various conditions. Oxygen should be provided immediately due to the potential for respiratory compromise.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: IV access is crucial for administration of necessary fluids and medications. Two large-bore IVs are ordered to ensure adequate access in case of rapid fluid resuscitation.

interventions.label: universal labs (CBC, CMP, UA)
interventions.why: Universal lab orders help establish a patient's baseline status quickly, allowing for proper diagnosis and management of the condition.

decoys.label: Administer antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are typically used for allergic reactions, not vomiting caused by conditions like gastroenteritis or peptic ulcer disease.

decoys.label: Prescribe painkillers (e.g., Tylenol or Advil) as the primary treatment
decoys.why: While painkillers may help with symptoms, they do not address the underlying cause of vomiting and should be used in conjunction with other treatments.

decoys.label: Recommend a gluten-free diet without proper diagnosis
decoys.why: A gluten-free diet is only indicated for those with celiac disease or gluten sensitivity, not as a general approach for vomiting of unknown cause.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without culture and sensitivity testing
decoys.why: Antibiotics should only be prescribed after identifying the causative pathogen through culture and sensitivity testing to avoid antimicrobial resistance.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
easy.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
standard.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

081.title: Diarrhea
081.diagnosis: Infectious diarrhea (e.g., Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Escherichia coli) or non-infectious causes such as irritable bowel syndrome, lactose intolerance, or celiac disease
exam: Diarrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
081.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
081.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain stool sample for culture and sensitivity
interventions.why: To identify potential pathogens causing diarrhea

interventions.label: Obtain stool ova and parasites examination
interventions.why: For evaluation of possible parasitic infection

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing diarrhea, which can lead to dehydration and electrolyte imbalance. Oxygen is essential for maintaining adequate oxygen saturation.

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: The patient may require fluid resuscitation due to potential dehydration caused by prolonged diarrhea. Large-bore IV access allows for efficient administration of fluids.

interventions.label: Follow-up Actions (Next 30 Seconds)
interventions.why: Universal labs such as CBC, CMP, and UA can help identify any underlying conditions or complications caused by the diarrhea.

decoys.label: Administer antibiotics without stool culture and sensitivity results
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be administered based on the specific pathogen identified from culture and sensitivity results to ensure appropriate treatment.

decoys.label: Prescribe lactase supplements for diagnosis of infectious diarrhea
decoys.why: Lactase supplements are used for lactose intolerance, not for diagnosing or treating infectious diarrhea.

decoys.label: Order a gluten challenge test for the diagnosis of infectious diarrhea
decoys.why: Gluten challenge tests are used for celiac disease diagnosis, not for infectious diarrhea. The correct diagnostic test would be an endoscopy with biopsy.

decoys.label: Prescribe opioids for diarrhea without ruling out infection
decoys.why: Opioids can mask symptoms and potentially worsen the underlying infection. It is important to rule out infectious causes before considering opioid therapy.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
easy.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

082.title: Abdominal Pain
082.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
082.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
082.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Order electrocardiogram (ECG) for baseline cardiac evaluation
Obtain abdominal and pelvic imaging (ultrasound or CT scan, depending on local protocols and patient's condition)
Monitor vital signs, pain level, and physical exam findings
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain which could be a sign of acute abdomen or other life-threatening conditions. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial in such cases.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: This order ensures immediate stabilization of the patient by providing oxygen, establishing IV access, and checking blood glucose level.

decoys.label: Order a CT scan without abdominal and pelvic imaging
decoys.why: A complete evaluation of the abdomen includes both abdominal and pelvic organs. Omitting the pelvic imaging may lead to missed diagnoses.

decoys.label: Prescribe an over-the-counter pain medication as primary treatment
decoys.why: Acute abdomen often requires more aggressive intervention than just pain management. A definitive diagnosis is necessary to guide appropriate treatment.

decoys.label: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics without culture and sensitivity testing
decoys.why: Broad-spectrum antibiotics should not be prescribed without confirming the presence of an infection and determining the specific organism responsible for it.

decoys.label: Discharge the patient with no further evaluation or treatment
decoys.why: Acute abdomen requires prompt evaluation to prevent complications. Dismissing a patient without a diagnosis and appropriate management can lead to serious consequences.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. 083.title: Abdominal Pain
083.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
083.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
083.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain basic labs (CBC, BMP, Coagulation panel)
Order imaging studies (Axial CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast)
CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

decoys.label: Antacids
decoys.why: Antacids are used for symptoms of acid reflux or ulcers, not appropriate for acute abdomen as they do not address the underlying cause

decoys.label: NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen)
decoys.why: NSAIDs can mask symptoms and potentially worsen an acute abdomen by causing gastric ulcers, bleeding or perforation

decoys.label: Laxatives
decoys.why: Laxatives are used for constipation issues, not appropriate for acute abdomen as they do not address the underlying cause

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are used to treat allergies and related symptoms, not appropriate for acute abdomen as they do not address the underlying cause

084.title: Abdominal Pain
084.diagnosis: Abdominal pain of unknown origin, potential causes include appendicitis, cholecystitis, mesenteric ischemia, peptic ulcer disease, pancreatitis, and gastrointestinal bleeding.
084.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
084.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
084.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory studies: CBC, metabolic panel, lipase, amylase
Imaging: Consider CT scan of abdomen and pelvis with contrast if not contraindicated
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain, which may indicate a serious condition requiring immediate oxygen support.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids and potential medications in case of an emergency.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: As the patient's condition is unknown, nasal cannula is a standard initial intervention for providing oxygen support.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: CMP (Comprehensive Metabolic Panel) and BMP (Basic Metabolic Panel) are essential to evaluate renal and liver function, which may be affected by various causes of abdominal pain.

decoys.label: Order a complete blood count (CBC) every hour
decoys.why: Repeatedly ordering a CBC hourly is unnecessary and can lead to radiation exposure from repeated imaging due to the need for new samples.

decoys.label: Administer antibiotics without culture results
decoys.why: Antibiotics should only be administered after positive culture results to avoid antibiotic resistance and unnecessary side effects.

decoys.label: Prescribe a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) for suspected pancreatitis
decoys.why: NSAIDs can worsen pancreatitis by increasing inflammation. They should be avoided in this context and replaced with opioids or other pain medications.

decoys.label: Discontinue all oral intake for suspected gastrointestinal bleeding
decoys.why: In most cases, oral intake can continue unless there is a risk of aspiration or the patient has active signs of bleeding. Discontinuing oral intake unnecessarily can lead to malnutrition and delayed recovery.

085.title: Amenorrhea
085.diagnosis: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) or Hypothalamic amenorrhea are common differential diagnoses for amenorrhea of unknown etiology in reproductive-aged women.
exam: Amenorrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
085.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
085.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Lab work: FSH, LH, prolactin, thyroid function tests, DHEA-S, testosterone, glucose and insulin levels
pregnancy test
interventions.why: This case involves a woman of unknown diagnosis presenting with amenorrhea, which is a common symptom associated with pregnancy. Therefore, a pregnancy test should be ordered as soon as possible.

decoys.label: Blood sugar test instead of glucose and insulin levels
decoys.why: Blood sugar test measures the level of glucose in the blood at a single point in time, while glucose and insulin levels provide a more comprehensive picture of glucose metabolism.

decoys.label: CT scan instead of Transvaginal ultrasound
decoys.why: A CT scan is not typically used for imaging the ovaries in PCOS or hypothalamic amenorrhea. A transvaginal ultrasound is preferred due to its ability to visualize the ovaries more clearly.

decoys.label: MRI instead of Transvaginal ultrasound
decoys.why: An MRI is not typically used for imaging the ovaries in PCOS or hypothalamic amenorrhea. A transvaginal ultrasound is preferred due to its ability to visualize the ovaries more clearly and with less expense and radiation exposure.

decoys.label: Estrogen level test
decoys.why: In amenorrheic women, estrogen levels are often low or absent due to the underlying condition. However, it is not a primary diagnostic tool for PCOS or hypothalamic amenorrhea as these conditions can also present with normal estrogen levels.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

086.title: Blood in Urine
086.diagnosis: Hematuria could be due to various causes such as urinary tract infections, kidney stones, glomerular diseases, or malignancy. A definitive diagnosis will require further evaluation.
exam: Blood in Urine — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.

interventions.label: UA

decoys.label: Administration of antibiotics without a culture and sensitivity test
decoys.why: Antibiotic therapy should be guided by culture and sensitivity results to ensure appropriate treatment, which is not available in this case.

decoys.label: A CT scan of the lungs
decoys.why: CT scan of the lungs is not relevant for evaluation of hematuria. The focus should be on imaging studies of the kidneys.

decoys.label: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) without a definitive diagnosis of kidney stones
decoys.why: ESWL is used for the treatment of kidney stones but should not be performed unless a diagnosis of kidney stones has been confirmed.

decoys.label: Perform a cystoscopy without a preliminary workup or suspicion of bladder cancer
decoys.why: Cystoscopy is an invasive procedure used to diagnose and treat bladder problems. It should be performed only after a thorough evaluation, and with appropriate indications.

087.title: Abdominal Pain
087.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
087.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
087.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: CBC, Metabolic Panel, Lipase
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain, which could be indicative of a number of serious conditions. Oxygen should be administered to ensure adequate oxygenation and prevent hypoxia.

interventions.label: NPO
interventions.why: If surgery or procedure is possible (abdominal pain), the patient should be NPO (nothing by mouth) to prepare for potential procedures.

decoys.label: Ibuprofen
decoys.why: Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that should be avoided in patients with acute abdomen until the cause of the symptoms has been determined, as it may mask symptoms or worsen conditions such as appendicitis or pancreatitis.

decoys.label: Motilium
decoys.why: Motilium is a prokinetic medication used to treat gastrointestinal disorders characterized by slow gastric emptying. It should not be given to patients with acute abdomen, as it could potentially worsen or obscure the symptoms of various underlying conditions such as obstruction or perforation.

decoys.label: Metoclopramide
decoys.why: Metoclopramide is a prokinetic medication used to treat gastrointestinal disorders characterized by slow gastric emptying. It should not be given to patients with acute abdomen, as it could potentially worsen or obscure the symptoms of various underlying conditions such as obstruction or perforation.

decoys.label: Digitalis
decoys.why: Digitalis is a cardiac glycoside used to treat heart failure and atrial fibrillation. It should not be given to patients with acute abdomen, as it has no role in the management of abdominal pain and may not be safe due to potential adverse effects on the gastrointestinal tract.
