088.title: Frequency of Urination
088.diagnosis: Unknown frequency of urination may suggest Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms (LUTS) or Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH) in males, and Urinary Tract Infection (UTI), Overactive Bladder (OAB), Diabetes Mellitus or Chronic Kidney Disease in all patients. However, further evaluation is needed.
exam: Frequency of Urination — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
088.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
088.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Obtain urinalysis to rule out urinary tract infection.
Order a renal function panel to evaluate kidney function and potential diabetes.
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: Electrolytes and renal/liver status are important for a variety of conditions, including kidney problems which can cause frequent urination. Urinalysis ± culture is necessary to identify potential infections that may cause symptoms.

decoys.label: Increase fluid intake without any evaluation
decoys.why: This order is not clinically plausible as it does not address the underlying cause of increased frequency of urination and may worsen symptoms if the condition is due to an infection or other pathology.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics without a urinalysis
decoys.why: Antibiotics should be prescribed only after culture and sensitivity results are available. A urinalysis is necessary to confirm a bacterial urinary tract infection before initiating antibiotic therapy.

decoys.label: Prescribe alpha-blockers without evaluation
decoys.why: Alpha-blockers are used to manage symptoms of Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH). However, it is crucial to perform an evaluation and rule out other possible causes such as Urinary Tract Infection or Overactive Bladder before prescribing any medication.

decoys.label: Prescribe Desmopressin for increased frequency of urination
decoys.why: Desmopressin is used to manage symptoms of diabetes insipidus, not excessive urination due to Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms or other causes. An evaluation should be conducted first to determine the underlying cause.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
easy.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

089.title: Burns to Face and Buttocks
089.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for burns to face and buttocks of unknown etiology could be thermal burns, chemical burns, or electrical burns. However, it's important to note that the diagnosis may vary based on the specific presentation, history, and examination findings.
exam: Burns to Face and Buttocks — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
089.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
089.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Perform a thorough physical exam with particular attention to the extent and depth of the burns, presence of inhalation injury, and any systemic symptoms
Send blood for complete blood count (CBC), electrolytes, coagulation profile, and arterial blood gas if needed
Obtain a urine toxicology screen if chemical burn is suspected
Administer appropriate pain management and fluid resuscitation per burn wound surface area and patient's hemodynamic status
Apply cool water to the burns for initial relief, then cover with clean, dry, non-adherent dressings
Consult Plastic Surgery, Burn Center, or Emergency Medicine specialists as appropriate for further management and potential escharotomy if needed
Prepare for possible airway management and intubation if the burn involves the face or inhalation injury is suspected
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient has burns to the face and buttocks, which can cause severe distress or acute medical symptoms (AMS), requiring immediate oxygen.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for rapid fluid resuscitation due to the extensive nature of the burn injuries.

decoys.label: Administer ice packs to the burn sites
decoys.why: Ice should not be applied directly to burns, as it can cause further damage by freezing the outer layers of burned skin and slowing down the healing process. Instead, cool water should be used for initial relief.

decoys.label: Apply ointments or creams to the burn sites without first cleaning the area
decoys.why: The burn site should be cleaned thoroughly with mild soap and water before applying any topical medications to prevent infection. Debridement may also be necessary.

decoys.label: Wait to provide pain management until after the physical exam and bloodwork
decoys.why: Pain management should be initiated promptly upon presentation, as burns can cause significant pain and discomfort. Delaying pain management may lead to increased anxiety and stress for the patient.

decoys.label: Perform a skin graft immediately on admission
decoys.why: Skin grafting is typically not performed until several days after the burn injury, as it requires proper wound preparation, debridement, and optimal timing for successful grafting. Immediate skin grafting can lead to poor outcomes and increased risk of complications.

090.title: Prenatal Followup
090.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for an unknown prenatal follow-up could include various pregnancy complications, fetal anomalies, or maternal conditions. However, it's important to note that without specific symptoms or findings, a definitive diagnosis cannot be made.
exam: Prenatal Followup — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
090.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
090.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Maternal lab work (CBC, glucose screen, urinalysis, etc.)
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is pregnant, and supplemental oxygen should be administered in all cases of prenatal followup due to potential fetal hypoxia.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for administering fluids and medications during labor and delivery.

interventions.label: Pulse oximetry
interventions.why: Monitoring the patient's oxygen saturation levels is essential in prenatal care.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Although there are no clear indications of cardiopulmonary issues, a chest X-ray may be useful to rule out potential complications during pregnancy.

decoys.label: Blood pressure monitoring every hour
decoys.why: While blood pressure monitoring is important during prenatal follow-up, it's not necessarily a wrong order. However, the frequency might be excessive and should be based on the patient's medical history.

decoys.label: Amniocentesis without an indication
decoys.why: Amniocentesis is a diagnostic test used to detect genetic disorders in the fetus, but it carries risks and is typically not performed unless there is a specific reason or high-risk factor for the pregnancy.

decoys.label: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
decoys.why: MRI is generally avoided during pregnancy due to the potential risks to the developing fetus. Ultrasound is usually the preferred imaging modality in prenatal care.

decoys.label: Antibiotics for the baby
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not prescribed for the baby during pregnancy as they cross the placenta and can harm the developing fetus. If an infection is suspected, the mother would be treated instead.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
standard.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

091.title: Lump in Breast
exam: Lump in Breast — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
091.clinical_tip: Order the biopsy yourself — do not defer workup to the consultant.
091.objective: Diagnostic mammography, ultrasound, and tissue diagnosis.

interventions.label: Clinic / office setting
interventions.why: Stable mass evaluation in outpatient setting.

interventions.label: Breast + HEENT exam
interventions.why: Document fixed painless mass before imaging.

decoys.label: Administer chemotherapy
decoys.why: Chemotherapy is not a first-line treatment for a newly diagnosed breast cancer patient. It's typically used after the initial surgery, if necessary, and depending on the results of pathology reports.

decoys.label: Prescribe hormone therapy
decoys.why: Hormone therapy is not appropriate for a newly diagnosed breast cancer patient without prior knowledge of receptor status (ER/PR positive or negative, HER2 positive or negative). This information is usually obtained through biopsy results.

decoys.label: Schedule radiation therapy before surgery
decoys.why: Radiation therapy is typically given after surgery as adjuvant treatment. In some cases, it may be used concurrently with chemotherapy for certain types of breast cancer but not before the surgical removal of the tumor.

decoys.label: Refer patient to psychiatrist
decoys.why: While dealing with a breast cancer diagnosis can be emotionally taxing, there is no clinical indication for a psychiatrist referral at this stage. The primary focus should be on diagnostic procedures and treatment planning.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Order the biopsy yourself — do not defer workup to the consultant.
easy.objective: Diagnostic mammography, ultrasound, and tissue diagnosis. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Diagnostic mammography, ultrasound, and tissue diagnosis.

092.title: Vaginal Bleeding
092.diagnosis: Vaginal bleeding could be due to various causes such as endometrial cancer, cervical cancer, uterine fibroids, vaginitis, or trauma. Given the uncertainty of diagnosis, a comprehensive workup is required.
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
092.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
092.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), coagulation panel, urinalysis, and pregnancy test
Imaging studies: Transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI (if available)
Oxygen
interventions.why: Vaginal bleeding can be a symptom of various conditions, including pregnancy. Oxygen and IV access are essential life-saving interventions.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: For potential blood loss assessment and fluid resuscitation if necessary.

interventions.label: pregnancy test (β-hCG)
interventions.why: Vaginal bleeding is a common symptom in pregnancy. A β-hCG test will help determine the stage of pregnancy or possible ectopic pregnancy.

decoys.label: Aminoaciduria test
decoys.why: This test is not relevant to the symptoms of vaginal bleeding. It's used to diagnose inherited disorders that affect the way the body handles certain amino acids.

decoys.label: HIV test
decoys.why: While HIV can cause various gynecological symptoms, it is not a primary diagnostic for vaginal bleeding. A comprehensive workup should be conducted first to rule out other potential causes before considering HIV.

decoys.label: Blood glucose test
decoys.why: Unless there's reason to suspect diabetes or another endocrine disorder, blood glucose testing is not indicated in this context. Diabetes typically does not cause vaginal bleeding as an initial symptom.

decoys.label: Chest X-ray
decoys.why: A chest X-ray is not relevant to the symptoms of vaginal bleeding. It's primarily used for evaluating conditions in the lungs and heart.

easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

093.title: Altered Mental Status
093.diagnosis: Altered Mental Status - Acute brain disorder, likely of infectious, metabolic, toxicological, or structural etiology
093.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
093.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis if no contraindications and suspicion of meningitis or encephalitis
Consult Neurology, Infectious Disease, and/or Toxicology as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen administration is crucial for any altered mental status case to maintain adequate oxygenation and prevent further deterioration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Establishing two large-bore IV access lines ensures the rapid delivery of fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

decoys.label: Obtain a urine drug screen
decoys.why: Urine drug screens are not typically ordered for patients with Altered Mental Status, as blood tests (such as toxicology screens) provide more accurate results.

decoys.label: Order a chest X-ray
decoys.why: While a chest X-ray can help rule out certain conditions, it is not a primary diagnostic tool for Altered Mental Status. The focus should be on evaluating the central nervous system.

decoys.label: Administer an antipsychotic medication
decoys.why: Antipsychotics are used to treat psychosis, not altered mental status caused by organic brain disorders, metabolic imbalances, or toxicities. They can worsen the underlying condition if incorrectly prescribed.

decoys.label: Order a coagulation panel
decoys.why: While coagulation tests may be useful in certain conditions like subdural hematoma, they are not typically ordered as a first line for Altered Mental Status. The focus should be on the central nervous system and other potential causes.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 094.title: Rash with Joint Pain
094.diagnosis: Possible Autoimmune Disorder or Drug Reaction
exam: Rash with Joint Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
094.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
094.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), Comprehensive metabolic panel, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), Antinuclear antibody (ANA), Rheumatoid factor, Liver function tests (LFTs)
Imaging studies: Consider joint imaging (X-ray, MRI) if localized joint pain or swelling
Dermatology consultation for skin lesion evaluation and biopsy

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not effective against autoimmune disorders or drug reactions. They are used for bacterial infections.

decoys.label: Prescribe steroids without consulting a rheumatologist
decoys.why: Steroids can be used to manage symptoms of autoimmune disorders, but they should be prescribed under the supervision of a rheumatologist due to potential side effects.

decoys.label: Order only Complete Blood Count (CBC)
decoys.why: While CBC is useful, other tests such as Comprehensive Metabolic Panel, ESR, CRP, ANA, Rheumatoid factor, Liver function tests, and imaging studies are also necessary for a comprehensive diagnosis.

decoys.label: Recommend topical creams for joint pain
decoys.why: Topical creams are not effective in managing the underlying cause of joint pain associated with autoimmune disorders or drug reactions. They may provide temporary relief but do not address the root cause.

095.title: Vaginal Bleeding
095.diagnosis: Vaginal bleeding could be due to various causes such as hormonal imbalances, infections, trauma, or neoplastic conditions. However, it's crucial to note that the given scenario is a USMLE Step 3 CCS (Clinical Case Simulation) with an unknown diagnosis. In this context, the focus should be on initial stabilization and diagnostic steps rather than assuming a specific etiology.
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
095.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
095.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory Tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Coagulation Panel, Pregnancy Test, HIV Test, Hepatitis B and C Serologies, Syphilis RPR/VDRL
Consults: Obstetrics & Gynecology if the patient is of childbearing age or has recent sexual activity
β-hCG
interventions.why: Vaginal bleeding in a reproductive age woman requires immediate pregnancy testing.

decoys.label: Antibiotics without culture and sensitivity
decoys.why: Initiating antibiotics without identifying the causative organism increases the risk of antibiotic resistance.

decoys.label: Oral contraceptives
decoys.why: In cases of vaginal bleeding, it's important to establish the cause first before administering hormonal therapy like oral contraceptives. The bleeding could be a sign of an underlying condition that needs investigation.

decoys.label: Dilaudid (hydromorphone) for pain management
decoys.why: Initial pain management in cases of vaginal bleeding should focus on non-opioid medications due to the potential complications associated with opioids, such as constipation and respiratory depression.

decoys.label: Surgical intervention without proper evaluation
decoys.why: Surgical intervention should be considered after a thorough diagnostic workup and only when necessary. Initial stabilization and diagnosis are crucial to guide the appropriate management strategy for vaginal bleeding.

096.title: Nausea and Vomiting
096.diagnosis: Acute gastroenteritis or possibly other gastrointestinal disorders based on the presentation of nausea and vomiting
exam: Nausea and Vomiting — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
096.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
096.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: CBC, electrolytes, amylase, lipase
Obtain imaging studies: Abdominal X-ray or CT scan if symptoms persist or complications suspected
Oxygen
interventions.why: Nausea and Vomiting can be signs of various life-threatening conditions such as sepsis, gastrointestinal bleed, or metabolic disorders. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial to prevent hypoxia.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer fluids, medications, and draw blood for lab work if needed.

decoys.label: Antihistamines
decoys.why: Antihistamines are typically used for allergic reactions, not gastroenteritis. They may have limited benefit in symptomatic relief but are not the primary treatment.

decoys.label: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs)
decoys.why: While PPIs can help with acid reflux and peptic ulcers, they are not typically used for acute gastroenteritis. The underlying cause is usually viral or bacterial infection, which PPIs do not address directly.

decoys.label: Laxatives
decoys.why: Laxatives can exacerbate symptoms of dehydration and electrolyte imbalance in cases of acute gastroenteritis. They are usually contraindicated until the condition stabilizes.

decoys.label: Antibiotics (empirically, without culture and sensitivity testing)
decoys.why: Empirical antibiotic treatment is not recommended for acute gastroenteritis because most cases are caused by viruses. Antibiotics should only be prescribed when there's a strong suspicion of bacterial infections based on lab results or other indications.

097.title: Dyspareunia
097.diagnosis: Vulvodynia or Vestibulodynia (common causes of dyspareunia in women)
exam: Dyspareunia — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
097.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
097.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including a gynecologic and sexual history
Perform a physical exam focusing on the vulva, cervix, and vagina
Consider referral to a gynecologist or pelvic pain specialist for further evaluation
Order laboratory tests (e.g., complete blood count, thyroid function tests) if other potential causes are suspected
Prescribe analgesics for symptomatic relief and recommend pelvic floor physical therapy as needed
β-hCG
interventions.why: Dyspareunia can be related to various reproductive issues, and a pregnancy test is essential in women of childbearing age.

decoys.label: Prescribe antibiotics
decoys.why: Vulvodynia and Vestibulodynia are not infections, so antibiotics are not indicated.

decoys.label: Refer for a colonoscopy
decoys.why: Dyspareunia is a gynecological issue, not related to the colon. A colonoscopy would not be appropriate.

decoys.label: Perform a mammogram
decoys.why: Mammograms are for breast examination, not relevant for diagnosing or treating dyspareunia.

decoys.label: Prescribe blood thinners
decoys.why: Dyspareunia due to Vulvodynia or Vestibulodynia is not typically associated with blood clotting issues. Blood thinners would not be the first-line treatment.

098.title: Back Pain
098.diagnosis: Acute Low Back Strain or Strain/Strain (Whiplash Injury) is the most likely diagnosis given the presented chief complaint of back pain with an unknown cause.
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
098.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
098.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including mechanism of injury, onset and progression of symptoms, associated symptoms (e.g., radicular symptoms, neurological deficits), and any aggravating or alleviating factors

Physical exam focusing on the lumbar spine, muscle strength testing, reflexes, sensory examination, and special tests such as straight leg raise and Neurological Level Sign (NLS) to assess for radiculopathy
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide adequate oxygenation in case of respiratory distress

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: For rapid administration of fluids and medications if needed

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: To quickly assess blood glucose level, as low or high levels can cause symptoms similar to back pain

decoys.label: Oxygen
decoys.why: Oxygen is not indicated for the treatment of acute low back strain or whiplash injury as it is not a respiratory issue.

decoys.label: IV access ×2
decoys.why: Multiple IV accesses are typically not necessary for an uncomplicated acute low back strain. Additional IV lines would only be warranted if complications arise or additional treatments requiring IV administration become necessary.

decoys.label: Glucose check
decoys.why: A glucose check is generally not indicated in the initial evaluation of acute low back strain or whiplash injury, unless there is a suspicion of diabetes or another metabolic disorder.

decoys.label: Pain medications (e.g., opioids)
decoys.why: While pain management is important for patient comfort, initial treatment should focus on rest, physical therapy, and non-pharmacological measures to alleviate discomfort. Opioids should be avoided in the acute phase due to their potential for dependence and side effects.

decoys.label: Over-the-counter pain medication
decoys.why: Cauda Equina Syndrome requires immediate attention and specialist management, over-the-counter medications are not sufficient for this emergency.

decoys.why: NSAIDs may not be effective for Cauda Equina Syndrome, which is a severe neurological emergency requiring urgent neuroimaging and specialist consultation.

decoys.label: MRI scan scheduled for next week
decoys.why: Cauda Equina Syndrome requires immediate attention. Delaying an MRI scan until the following week could lead to further complications and permanent damage.
